*Originators: Paul Baum, Alain Connes.*

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: History — The Baum–Connes Conjecture
text:
_Origin, formulation, and timeline._

The Baum–Connes conjecture grew out of the confluence of three streams that matured in the 1970s and early 1980s: Alain Connes' program to extend index theory and differential geometry to the "bad" quotient spaces produced by group actions and foliations (noncommutative geometry); the Atiyah–Singer index theorem with its equivariant and families generalizations; and the K-theory of operator algebras. The central object is the **assembly map** (or analytic index map)

$$\mu \colon K^G_*(\underline{E}G) \longrightarrow K_*(C^*_r(G)),$$

which sends the equivariant $K$-homology of the classifying space $\underline{E}G$ for proper $G$-actions to the topological $K$-theory of the **reduced** group $C^*$-algebra $C^*_r(G)$. The conjecture asserts that $\mu$ is an isomorphism for every second-countable locally compact (Hausdorff) group $G$. Geometrically, it says that every $K$-theory class of $C^*_r(G)$ is the index of a $G$-equivariant elliptic operator and that the only relations among such indices are the obvious geometric ones — a sweeping "no exotic K-theory" statement that simultaneously encodes the strong Novikov conjecture, the Kadison–Kaplansky idempotent conjecture (for torsion-free $G$), and parts of the Gromov–Lawson–Rosenberg circle of positive-scalar-curvature questions.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: History — The Baum–Connes Conjecture
text: The earliest formulation appeared in a 1982 preprint by **Paul Baum and Alain Connes** treating the discrete-group and foliation setting (using $BG$ rather than the proper classifying space). The decisive reformulation came after work of **Baum, Connes, Higson and Mislin** recognized that for groups with torsion one must use the classifying space for proper actions $\underline{E}G$; this is the version codified in the influential 1994 Oberwolfach survey by Baum, Connes and Higson, which remains the standard reference. A "conjecture with coefficients" — replacing $C^*_r(G)$ by the crossed product $A \rtimes_r G$ for a $G$-$C^*$-algebra $A$ — was introduced to obtain a statement stable under the operations needed in proofs; this stronger version was later shown to be **false** in general.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1976** — Connes develops the index theory of foliations and von Neumann algebras of measured foliations, seeding the noncommutative-geometry viewpoint.
- **1980–84** — Connes and Kasparov frame the conjecture for connected/amenable Lie groups (the "Connes–Kasparov conjecture"); Pimsner–Voiculescu's $\mathbb{Z}$-crossed-product computation gives early evidence.
- **1982** — Baum and Connes circulate the foundational preprint *Geometric K-theory for Lie groups and foliations*, posing the conjecture for discrete groups and foliations.
- **1984–88** — Kasparov's bivariant $KK$-theory and his "Dirac–dual-Dirac" method prove the conjecture (and Novikov) for discrete subgroups of Lie groups and groups acting properly on suitable nonpositively curved spaces.
- **1994** — Baum, Connes and Higson publish *Classifying space for proper actions and K-theory of group C\*-algebras*, fixing the modern $\underline{E}G$ formulation and the conjecture with coefficients.
- **2001** — **Higson and Kasparov** prove the conjecture for groups with the **Haagerup property** (a-T-menable groups), including all amenable groups, free groups, and many others.
- **2002** — **Lafforgue** introduces Banach $KK$-theory and proves the conjecture for many groups with **Property (T)**, including cocompact lattices in $Sp(n,1)$ and certain semisimple groups (Fields Medal, 2002, in part for this).
- **2003** — **Higson, Lafforgue and Skandalis**, building on Gromov's monster groups, produce **counterexamples to the conjecture with coefficients** via expander-based Gromov groups, while leaving the original (coefficient-free) conjecture open.
- **2003–07** — Lafforgue and others extend results toward hyperbolic groups; the bivariant Chern character program (Puschnigg, Voigt) advances rational statements.
- **2012** — **Lafforgue** establishes strong Property (T) and proves the conjecture for all (Gromov-)hyperbolic groups and their lattices.
- **2016–2020** — Chabert–Echterhoff–Nest and the Going-Down / restriction techniques verify the conjecture for **almost-connected groups** and linear reductive $p$-adic groups.
- **2020s** — The conjecture remains **open in general**; higher-rank lattices such as $SL_3(\mathbb{Z})$ and groups with strong Property (T) and bad coarse geometry are the active frontier.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Baum–Connes Conjecture
text:
_Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

The conjecture bears the names of two mathematicians whose backgrounds — one rooted in algebraic topology and K-theory, the other in operator algebras and analysis — fused to produce its statement.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: Paul Baum (1936–2024)
text: Paul Frank Baum, born in Brooklyn in 1936, took his Ph.D. at Princeton in 1963 under **John Moore**, working in algebraic topology and homological algebra. His early career was spent in topology and algebraic geometry: the celebrated **Baum–Bott** formula for residues of holomorphic vector fields (with Raoul Bott) and the **Baum–Fulton–MacPherson** Riemann–Roch theorem for singular varieties date from this period and already show his instinct for index-theoretic and K-theoretic structure on singular spaces. Baum held a long professorship at Pennsylvania State University. From the late 1970s he became one of the principal architects of the geometric, topological side of noncommutative geometry: the construction of the classifying space for proper actions, the geometric model of $K$-homology by cycles (manifolds with maps and bundles), and the equivariant assembly map are very much in his idiom. His collaboration with Connes — and later with Higson — gave the conjecture its precise topological formulation and its geometric K-homology side. Baum continued to lecture on the conjecture and its index-theoretic content into the 2010s; he died in 2024.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: Alain Connes (b. 1947)
text: Alain Connes, born in Draguignan, France, in 1947, is a Fields Medalist (1982) and one of the founders of modern operator algebra theory and of **noncommutative geometry** as a discipline. His foundational work on the classification of factors (type III, the Tomita–Takesaki and modular theory, injective factors) earned the 1982 Fields Medal; he is a professor at the Collège de France and IHÉS and won the Crafoord Prize (2001). Connes' program reinterprets geometry through the lens of $C^*$- and von Neumann algebras: a "space" is encoded by an algebra, and the pathological quotients arising from group actions, foliations, and orbifolds — which have no good ordinary topology — become tractable noncommutative algebras (crossed products, groupoid algebras). For such an algebra the natural invariant is its $K$-theory, and the natural source of $K$-theory classes is index theory of equivariant elliptic operators. The conjecture is precisely the assertion that this index machine computes everything: that $K_*(C^*_r(G))$ is exhausted, without redundancy, by geometric indices. Connes' motivations were thus simultaneously to generalize the Atiyah–Singer index theorem to the equivariant/foliation setting and to provide a computational handle on the otherwise mysterious $K$-theory of reduced group $C^*$-algebras.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: The synthesis and its legacy
text: The 1982 Baum–Connes preprint married Baum's geometric $K$-homology and classifying-space technology to Connes' operator-algebraic index theory. The **modern formulation** differs from this root in one essential way: the original used $BG$ (which is correct only for torsion-free $G$), whereas the post-1994 statement — shaped together with **Nigel Higson** — uses the proper classifying space $\underline{E}G$, so that torsion in $G$ is accounted for. The conjecture became a unifying hub: it implies the **Novikov conjecture** (homotopy invariance of higher signatures), the **Kadison–Kaplansky idempotent conjecture**, the **Gromov–Lawson–Rosenberg** conjecture in many cases, and is intimately tied to the **Connes–Kasparov** theorem for Lie groups. Its influence reshaped K-theory, coarse geometry, and the theory of group $C^*$-algebras, and seeded the careers of a generation including Lafforgue, Yu, Tu, Skandalis, Nest, Echterhoff and many others.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: Approaches — The Baum–Connes Conjecture
text:
_Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

The conjecture is naturally split into **injectivity** (which yields the Novikov conjecture and is governed by coarse geometry) and **surjectivity** (the hard, "exotic K-theory" half). Almost all positive results factor the assembly map through a richer category — Kasparov's $KK$-theory or a Banach analogue — and exhibit a homotopy or factorization that forces it to be an isomorphism.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: Dirac–dual-Dirac method (Kasparov)
text: The dominant technique. One seeks a proper $G$-space $X$ with a "Dirac" element $\alpha \in KK^G(C_0(X), \mathbb{C})$ and a "dual-Dirac" element $\beta \in KK^G(\mathbb{C}, C_0(X))$ whose Kasparov product $\gamma = \beta\otimes\alpha$ is an idempotent in the representation ring $KK^G(\mathbb{C},\mathbb{C})$. If $\gamma = 1$, the assembly map is an isomorphism. **Best result:** proves the conjecture (with coefficients) for all groups acting properly isometrically on a complete Riemannian manifold of nonpositive curvature, on Bruhat–Tits buildings, on bolic spaces, and on Hilbert space — covering closed subgroups of connected Lie groups, hyperbolic groups, and amenable groups. **Barrier:** for groups with **Property (T)**, $\gamma \neq 1$ in $C^*$-theory — the "$\gamma$-element" obstruction is genuine, and the method cannot reach surjectivity without leaving the $C^*$-algebraic world.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: Haagerup property / a-T-menability (Higson–Kasparov)
text: If $G$ acts properly and affinely-isometrically on a Hilbert space (the Haagerup property), a Dirac–dual-Dirac argument in infinite dimensions, using the Bott periodicity of an infinite-dimensional Clifford/oscillator construction, forces $\gamma = 1$. **Best result (Higson–Kasparov, 2001):** the full conjecture **with coefficients** for all a-T-menable groups — all amenable, free, one-relator with torsion, Coxeter, and many discrete groups. **Barrier:** Property (T) is exactly the negation of the Haagerup property, so this route is structurally blind to higher-rank lattices.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: Banach $KK$-theory and Property (T) (Lafforgue)
text: Lafforgue's breakthrough was to replace $C^*$-algebra $KK$-theory by a version built on Banach algebras and unconditional completions, in which the Property-(T) obstruction to $\gamma = 1$ dissolves because the relevant representations need not be unitary. **Best result:** the conjecture (without coefficients) for cocompact lattices in $SL_2(\mathbb{Q}_p)$, $Sp(n,1)$, rank-one Lie groups, and — after his 2012 work on **strong Property (T)** — for all hyperbolic groups and their lattices. **Barrier:** higher-rank lattices such as $SL_3(\mathbb{Z})$, $SL_n(\mathbb{Z})$ ($n\ge 3$), and lattices in higher-rank $p$-adic groups satisfy strong forms of Property (T) that obstruct even the Banach approach; surjectivity here is open.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: Controlled / coarse geometry and Property A (Yu, Skandalis–Tu–Yu)
text: For the **injectivity** half (hence Novikov), Yu's coarse Baum–Connes machinery shows that any group that **coarsely embeds into Hilbert space** — in particular every group with **Property A / exactness** — satisfies the strong Novikov conjecture and injectivity of assembly. **Best result:** injectivity / Novikov for an enormous class of finitely generated groups. **Barrier:** Gromov's randomly-constructed **monster groups** contain expanders and do **not** coarsely embed into Hilbert space; on them the *maximal* coarse assembly map behaves very differently from the *reduced* one.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: Going-Down / restriction and almost-connected groups (Chabert–Echterhoff–Nest)
text: A homological/permanence strategy: verify the conjecture on compact-open or maximal-compact subgroups and propagate via the **Going-Down principle**, the **Mayer–Vietoris** and **continuity** properties of the Kasparov category, and the structure theory of locally compact groups. **Best result:** the conjecture for **almost-connected groups** and for the group of rational points of a linear algebraic group over a local field of characteristic zero (Chabert–Echterhoff–Nest, building on Connes–Kasparov). **Barrier:** the method needs the conjecture as input on building blocks and good structural reductions; it does not by itself crack the residually-finite higher-rank discrete lattices.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: Categorical / homological algebra (Meyer–Nest)
text: Meyer and Nest recast assembly as a derived functor: the Baum–Connes assembly map is the localization of the equivariant Kasparov category at the subcategory of compactly-induced (weakly contractible) objects, with a spectral sequence converging to $K_*(C^*_r G)$. This clarifies *why* the conjecture can fail with coefficients and isolates the precise obstruction class. **Best result:** a conceptual framework that reproves permanence properties and frames the counterexamples; it explains the failure-with-coefficients as a non-vanishing derived term. **Barrier:** it reorganizes rather than removes the analytic difficulty for Property-(T) groups.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: Negative results and the central obstruction
text: The **Higson–Lafforgue–Skandalis counterexamples (2003)** show the conjecture **with coefficients** is false: Gromov monster groups containing expanders make the reduced crossed product fail exactness, breaking surjectivity. This rules out any proof of the plain conjecture that passes through the coefficient version in full generality and pinpoints **expanders / non-exactness / Property (T)** as the true obstruction to a uniform method.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: Attempts — The Baum–Connes Conjecture
text:
_Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

The conjecture has a relatively clean record: it has not been plagued by famous false "complete proofs," in part because the community insisted from early on that any proof factor through verifiable $KK$-theoretic data. The drama has instead come from **the boundary of validity** — results that came tantalizingly close to the general statement and the discovery that a natural strengthening is outright false.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: The counterexamples to the conjecture with coefficients (2003)
text: The most important "near-miss" runs in reverse. To make proofs robust, Baum–Connes–Higson posed a **conjecture with coefficients**, replacing $C^*_r(G)$ by $A\rtimes_r G$. Many had hoped this was the "right" general statement. **Higson, Lafforgue and Skandalis** (Geom. Funct. Anal., 2003) used **Gromov's monster groups** — finitely generated groups that contain coarsely embedded **expander graphs** in their Cayley graphs — to construct $G$-$C^*$-algebras $A$ for which the assembly map with coefficients **fails to be surjective** (and related failures of injectivity). The mechanism is the failure of **exactness** of the reduced crossed product when expanders are present. This did not refute the original (coefficient-free) conjecture, but it permanently closed the door on proving the plain conjecture by reducing it to the coefficient version, and it identified non-exactness as the genuine enemy. (The full existence of Gromov monster groups itself rested on a long and delicate construction; rigorous accounts were supplied by Arzhantseva–Delzant and others.)

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: Property (T) as the wall, and Lafforgue's circumvention
text: After Kasparov's Dirac–dual-Dirac method, it was widely felt that **Property (T)** groups might be genuine counterexamples, because the $\gamma$-element argument provably fails for them in $C^*$-theory: the obstruction $\gamma \neq 1$ is real. This was a near-miss of intuition rather than of proof — and it was reversed by **Vincent Lafforgue**, who showed (2002) that working in **Banach $KK$-theory** the obstruction need not survive, proving the conjecture for many Property (T) groups. His later **strong Property (T)** work (2012) extended this to all hyperbolic groups, including hyperbolic Property (T) groups, definitively showing that Property (T) alone is not an obstruction.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: The Connes–Kasparov conjecture as a confirmed special case
text: For connected (or almost-connected) Lie groups the conjecture specializes to the **Connes–Kasparov conjecture**, asserting that the assembly map computes $K_*(C^*_r G)$ from the maximal compact subgroup. This was proven in stages — Wassermann, Kasparov, Penington–Plymen, and finally **Chabert–Echterhoff–Nest (2003)** for the almost-connected case — and stands as a fully verified, deep instance. A modern, more conceptual proof for **reductive Lie groups** was given by **Peter Hochs and collaborators** and, via Mackey-analogy ideas, by **Nigel Higson** and others.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: No retracted complete proofs of the general case
text: There is no widely circulated, subsequently-retracted claim of a proof of the **full** conjecture for all locally compact groups; the problem's culture of demanding explicit $KK$-classes and homotopies has deterred premature announcements. Disputes have instead concerned **scope and definitions** — e.g., precisely which formulation (with/without coefficients, maximal vs. reduced, full $\underline{E}G$) a given theorem establishes — and these are now well-settled in the literature (Meyer–Nest's categorical reformulation being the standard clarifier). The honest current statement is that the conjecture is **confirmed in vast generality and refuted in its strongest coefficient form**, with the plain conjecture **open** for higher-rank lattices and similar groups.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: Notes
text: The active community sits at the intersection of operator algebras, $K$-theory, coarse/large-scale geometry, and geometric group theory, and is unusually international. French operator-algebra schools (Connes, Skandalis, Lafforgue, Tu, and students), the Münster group around **Siegfried Echterhoff** and the categorical program of **Ralf Meyer** and **Ryszard Nest**, and the U.S./Penn State lineage (Baum, **Nigel Higson**, and students such as **Erik Guentner** and **Rufus Willett**) remain central. **Guoliang Yu** (Texas A&M) and collaborators drive the coarse-geometry and Novikov-injectivity frontier, while **Vincent Lafforgue** continues to push the Banach-$KK$ and strong-Property-(T) machinery that is the only known route past higher-rank obstructions. Recent activity (Hochs, Wang, and others) has revisited the **Connes–Kasparov isomorphism** for reductive groups with cleaner proofs, and survey/textbook efforts by **Willett–Yu** and **Valette** keep the field's standards explicit. The open frontier — higher-rank lattices like $SL_3(\mathbb{Z})$ — keeps the problem squarely in the hands of this same community.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: Status & Frontier — The Baum–Connes Conjecture
text:
_Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

**Status: active progress; open in general.** The plain (coefficient-free) Baum–Connes conjecture — that the assembly map $\mu\colon K^G_*(\underline{E}G)\to K_*(C^*_r(G))$ is an isomorphism — is **proven for a vast class of groups** and has **no known counterexample**, but it remains **unproven in full generality**. Its strong form **with coefficients is false** (Higson–Lafforgue–Skandalis, 2003), which is the key structural fact shaping all current work.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: What is known (unconditional)
text:
- **a-T-menable / Haagerup groups** (Higson–Kasparov, 2001): the conjecture **with coefficients** holds for all groups acting properly isometrically on Hilbert space — amenable, free, one-relator, Coxeter, and many more.
- **Property (T) groups via Banach $KK$** (Lafforgue, 2002 onward): including cocompact lattices in $Sp(n,1)$ and rank-one groups.
- **All (Gromov-)hyperbolic groups and their lattices**, with coefficients (Lafforgue, 2012, via strong Property (T)).
- **Almost-connected groups** and reductive **$p$-adic** groups (Chabert–Echterhoff–Nest, 2003) — the Connes–Kasparov isomorphism.
- **Injectivity / strong Novikov** for every group that **coarsely embeds into Hilbert space** or has **finite asymptotic dimension / Property A** (Yu and successors) — an enormous class.
- Groups acting properly on **bolic spaces, CAT(0) spaces, trees, and buildings** (Kasparov–Skandalis, Tu).

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: What is known (conditional / negative)
text:
- The **conjecture with coefficients is false**: Gromov monster groups with embedded **expanders** break exactness of the reduced crossed product (HLS 2003; Skandalis–Tu–Yu). Thus any general proof must avoid the coefficient reduction.
- For groups **without** coarse Hilbert-space embedding (the monster groups), even injectivity of the *reduced* coarse assembly map is subtle — though a *maximal* version is often recoverable.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: What a full resolution requires
text: A proof of the general conjecture must produce, for an **arbitrary** second-countable locally compact group, either a surjectivity argument or an obstruction-killing element that works **without** the Haagerup property and **past strong Property (T)** — the regime of higher-rank lattices such as $SL_3(\mathbb{Z})$, $SL_n(\mathbb{Z})\,(n\ge3)$, and lattices in higher-rank simple $p$-adic groups. No current technique reaches surjectivity there. Equivalently, in the Meyer–Nest categorical picture, one must show the derived obstruction term vanishes for these groups.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: Plausible routes
text:
1. **Extending strong Property (T) / Banach-$KK$ technology** (Lafforgue's program) to higher-rank lattices — the most direct attack, but the relevant Banach-space representation theory is hard.
2. **Higher-rank Mackey analogy / deformation** methods relating $SL_n(\mathbb{Z})$ to its motion-group degenerations.
3. **Categorical / homological** vanishing results (Meyer–Nest) isolating and killing the obstruction class.
4. **Coarse-geometric** advances handling expander-containing groups for the reduced theory.

A complete resolution would settle the **Novikov** and **Kadison–Kaplansky idempotent** conjectures for the groups in question and is regarded as one of the deepest open problems linking analysis, topology, and geometric group theory.

title: The Baum–Connes Conjecture
heading: Related problems
text:

- [Connes Embedding (aftermath)](../connes-embedding-aftermath/README.md)
- [Yang–Mills Mass Gap](../yang-mills-mass-gap/README.md)

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: The Beal Conjecture
text: > **Atlas Rank #32** · CSS **54.933** · Field: *Number Theory* · Posed: **1993** · Status: `open`

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Plain statement
text: If A^x + B^y = C^z with x,y,z > 2 and A,B,C positive integers, then A, B, and C share a common prime factor.

*Originators: Andrew Beal.*

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: History — The Beal Conjecture
text:
The Beal Conjecture asserts that the Diophantine equation
$$A^x + B^y = C^z,\qquad x,y,z>2,\ A,B,C\in\mathbb{Z}^{+},$$
has no solution in which $A$, $B$, and $C$ are pairwise coprime — equivalently, that any solution forces $A$, $B$, $C$ to share a common prime factor. It generalizes Fermat's Last Theorem (FLT): setting $x=y=z=n\ge 3$ recovers the FLT equation, and FLT (proved by Andrew Wiles, 1995) is exactly the equal-exponent, coprime case.

The conjecture grew out of computational number theory rather than from inside academia. Around 1993, **Andrew Beal**, a Dallas banker and self-taught number theorist, was experimenting with the FLT equation on computers. He observed that whenever $A^x+B^y=C^z$ held with all exponents exceeding 2, the bases shared a factor — the canonical example being $3^3 + 6^3 = 3^5$ (since $27+216=243$), where $3$ divides every base. He conjectured that the coprime case is impossible. Beal communicated the problem to several number theorists, including **R. Daniel Mauldin** of the University of North Texas, who published it for the wider community in a 1997 *Notices of the AMS* article, "A Generalization of Fermat's Last Theorem: The Beal Conjecture and Prize Problem," which also announced a cash prize Beal had endowed.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: History — The Beal Conjecture
text:
The conjecture is tied to the family of generalized Fermat equations $A^p+B^q=C^r$, organized by the inverse-exponent sum $\chi=\tfrac1p+\tfrac1q+\tfrac1r$. When $\chi>1$ (the "spherical" case) there are infinitely many coprime solutions; when $\chi=1$ (the "Euclidean" case, e.g. $(3,3,3),(2,3,6),(2,4,4)$) finitely many; and when $\chi<1$ (the "hyperbolic" case) the Darmon–Granville theorem (1995), via Faltings' theorem, gives finitely many coprime solutions for each fixed triple. Beal's condition $x,y,z>2$ forces $\chi\le 1$, with equality only at $(3,3,3)$, so the conjecture lives entirely in the hyperbolic regime plus that single Euclidean triple. A more general statement — the **Fermat–Catalan conjecture** of Darmon and Granville — predicts only finitely many coprime solutions across all triples with $\chi<1$; the ten known such solutions all involve an exponent equal to 2, and so all violate Beal's $x,y,z>2$ hypothesis, consistent with Beal.

The prize history made the problem unusually public. Beal initially offered \$5{,}000 (1997), raised it in stages, and in **2013** set it at **\$1{,}000{,}000**, administered by the American Mathematical Society, which holds the funds in trust and adjudicates submissions. The large prize drew amateur submissions in volume; none has survived refereeing.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1637** — Pierre de Fermat states FLT in the margin of his Diophantus, the historical root of the equal-exponent case.
- **1993** — Andrew Beal, experimenting computationally on FLT-type equations, formulates the coprimality conjecture and begins circulating it.
- **1994–95** — Wiles, with Taylor, completes the proof of FLT, settling the equal-exponent coprime case $x=y=z=n\ge 3$.
- **1995** — Darmon and Granville prove that $A^p+B^q=C^r$ with $\tfrac1p+\tfrac1q+\tfrac1r<1$ has finitely many coprime solutions for each fixed $(p,q,r)$, via Faltings.
- **1997** — Mauldin publishes the conjecture and Beal's prize in the *Notices of the AMS*, fixing the name "Beal Conjecture."
- **1997** — Darmon and Loïc Merel resolve $A^n+B^n=2C^n$ and related ternary equations, extending the modular method.
- **2000s** — Modular-method case studies (Bennett, Skinner, Kraus, Bruin, Poonen, Siksek, and others) resolve many individual signatures $(p,q,r)$, including infinite families such as $(n,n,2)$ and $(n,n,3)$.
- **2013** — Beal raises the prize to \$1{,}000{,}000, administered by the AMS; press coverage renews public attention.
- **2010s–2020s** — Frey-curve and level-lowering methods plus large-scale computation extend coverage of small exponent signatures; the general coprime case remains open.
- **present** — No general proof or counterexample; the conjecture stands as a flagship open problem in Diophantine number theory, with progress confined to specific exponent families.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Beal Conjecture
text:
**Daniel Andrew Beal** (born 29 November 1952, Lansing, Michigan) is an American banker, businessman, and amateur number theorist. He is the founder and chairman of Beal Bank and Beal Financial Corporation, headquartered in Texas, and one of the wealthiest self-made financiers in the United States. He is widely known outside finance for his recreational mathematics, high-stakes poker, and his interest in space launch ventures (Beal Aerospace, active 1997–2000). His mathematical work is that of a gifted autodidact rather than a credentialed academic: he has no doctorate in mathematics and pursued number theory as an avocation.

Beal's path to the conjecture ran through Fermat's Last Theorem. In the early 1990s, against the backdrop of intense public interest in FLT (Wiles announced his proof in 1993), Beal undertook his own computer experiments on the equation $A^x+B^y=C^z$. Rather than restrict to the equal-exponent FLT case, he allowed the three exponents to vary independently, subject to all exceeding 2. His central empirical observation was structural: every solution he could find had bases sharing a common prime factor. Examples such as $3^3+6^3=3^5$, $2^7+2^7=2^8$ (after rescaling), and the family generated by Mauldin's later catalogue all carry this feature. From this pattern Beal abstracted the bold claim that coprimality of $A,B,C$ is incompatible with $A^x+B^y=C^z$ once $x,y,z>2$.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Beal Conjecture
text: Beal recognized that the conjecture is a natural generalization of FLT and that, if true, it would imply FLT for exponents $\ge 3$ (the equal-exponent case with coprime bases). He also understood that the converse fails — Wiles's proof of FLT does not settle Beal, because the unequal-exponent cases require fundamentally more than the modularity of a single elliptic curve. Lacking the apparatus of modern arithmetic geometry, Beal sought out professional mathematicians to vet and disseminate the problem. The most consequential of these contacts was **R. Daniel Mauldin**, a distinguished analyst and set theorist at the University of North Texas, who recognized the problem's coherence and importance and published it, with Beal's blessing and prize, in the *Notices of the AMS* (1997). Mauldin's article is the canonical reference and the source of the name.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Beal Conjecture
text:
Beal's broader legacy in mathematics is twofold. First, he is an unusual example of an amateur whose conjecture entered the professional literature as a named, seriously studied problem — a status earned partly by the problem's intrinsic interest and partly by Mauldin's imprimatur. Second, through the **Beal Prize** (currently \$1{,}000{,}000, held and administered by the American Mathematical Society), he has institutionalized a standing incentive, on the model of earlier prize problems. Beal himself frames the prize as a way to inspire young people toward mathematics, and the AMS uses the associated funds partly to support research and outreach. Beal has stated that he formulated the conjecture independently; equivalent or closely related statements were noted around the same period by others (see the history of the generalized Fermat equation), but the modern name and the prize are firmly attached to Beal.

It should be stressed that the **modern formulation** is exactly Beal's: the coprime-bases impossibility for $x,y,z>2$. There is no divergence between a "historical root" and a "modern version" in the way some conjectures have one — what has evolved is the surrounding theory (the generalized Fermat equation, the Fermat–Catalan framework, the modular method), not the statement itself. Beal's contribution is the precise, falsifiable claim; the professional community has supplied the context in which it sits and the partial results that bound it.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Approaches — The Beal Conjecture
text: The Beal Conjecture is the coprime case of the generalized Fermat equation $A^x+B^y=C^z$ with $x,y,z>2$. No single technique reaches the full statement; progress is organized by exponent signature $(p,q,r)$ and by the inverse-sum $\chi=\tfrac1p+\tfrac1q+\tfrac1r$. The principal lines of attack follow.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: The modular method (Frey curves, level-lowering, modularity)
text:
The dominant tool, descended directly from the proof of FLT. To a putative coprime solution of $A^p+B^q=C^r$ one attaches a **Frey–Hellegouarch elliptic curve** whose discriminant encodes the solution. Ribet's level-lowering and the modularity of elliptic curves over $\mathbb{Q}$ (Wiles; Breuil–Conrad–Diamond–Taylor) then force the associated mod-$\ell$ Galois representation to match a modular form of very small level, which one shows cannot exist — a contradiction. This program, developed by Darmon, Merel, Bennett, Skinner, Kraus, Siksek and others, has resolved many specific signatures.

*Best results.* Equal-exponent families and many mixed cases are settled: $(n,n,n)$ is FLT; $(n,n,2)$ and $(n,n,3)$ are handled by Darmon–Merel (1997) and Bennett–Skinner-type arguments; numerous individual triples such as $(2,3,n)$ (Catalan-type, partly via Poonen–Schaefer–Stoll and Chen, Siksek, Dahmen) and small fixed exponents are known. Within the strict Beal range $x,y,z>2$, every signature with two equal exponents in the resolved families is covered.

*Barrier.* The method is essentially **one signature at a time**. Each new triple typically demands its own Frey curve, its own modularity input (often over a real quadratic or other number field, where modularity is harder), and its own elimination of residual newforms. There is no uniform Frey curve covering all $(p,q,r)$ with $\chi<1$, so the method does not, as presently understood, reach the infinitely many exponent triples that Beal requires.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Finiteness via Faltings (Darmon–Granville)
text:
For each *fixed* signature $(p,q,r)$ with $\chi<1$, the equation $A^p+B^q=C^r$ defines (after passage to a suitable cover) a curve of genus $\ge 2$; **Faltings' theorem** then gives finitely many coprime solutions. This is the Darmon–Granville theorem (1995).

*Best result.* Unconditional finiteness for every fixed hyperbolic triple, hence for Beal restricted to any bounded exponent set.

*Barrier.* Faltings is **ineffective**: it bounds the *number* of solutions but yields no computable height bound, so one cannot in general enumerate or exclude them. Worse, finiteness per triple says nothing across the infinitely many triples; it does not even bound the bases uniformly.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: The $abc$ conjecture and Diophantine inequalities
text:
The $abc$ conjecture of Masser and Oesterlé implies the Beal Conjecture **for all but finitely many** solutions, and with an explicit-enough form of $abc$ would settle it entirely. The mechanism: a coprime solution with large exponents has very smooth (high-power) terms, contradicting the radical bound $c \ll_\varepsilon \operatorname{rad}(abc)^{1+\varepsilon}$.

*Best result.* Conditional on $abc$, Beal holds up to finitely many exceptions; combined with the known finiteness per triple this is close to a conditional proof.

*Barrier.* The $abc$ conjecture is itself open. Shinichi Mochizuki's claimed proof via inter-universal Teichmüller theory remains **disputed**: it was published in *PRIMS* (2021) but is not accepted by the wider community, with a specific gap identified by Scholze and Stix (2018). No unconditional, effective $abc$ bound strong enough for Beal exists.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Descent and explicit Chabauty–Coleman
text:
For individual signatures where the relevant curve has small genus or special structure, **descent** and **Chabauty–Coleman** (and its quotient/elliptic-curve Chabauty refinements) can provably determine *all* rational points, hence all solutions. Bruin, Poonen, Schaefer, Stoll, Siksek and collaborators used these to finish signatures such as $(2,3,8)$, $(2,3,7)$ (the Klein quartic), $(2,4,5)$, and others.

*Best result.* Complete resolution of several otherwise-intractable individual cases, often where the modular method stalls on residual forms.

*Barrier.* These methods require the rank of the relevant Jacobian (or a quotient) to be strictly less than its dimension, and substantial case-specific computation. They do not scale to families with growing exponent.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Computation and verification
text:
Large-scale search has verified no coprime counterexample exists within wide bounds (historically, bases up to $1000$ and exponents up to large limits in independent searches by Norvig and others).

*Best result.* Strong empirical support and confirmation that no small counterexample exists; useful for ruling out specific residual signatures.

*Barrier.* Computation can only refute, never prove, the conjecture; the search space is infinite in both bases and exponents.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Summary of obstructions
text: The recurring obstruction is **uniformity**: every effective method is anchored to a fixed exponent signature, while Beal ranges over infinitely many. Bridging that gap appears to require either an unconditional effective $abc$, a uniform Frey-type construction, or a genuinely new idea. The ineffectivity of Faltings and the unresolved status of $abc$ together explain why a problem so close to FLT has resisted a general proof.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Genuine partial results (near-misses by signature)
text:
The substantive progress on Beal consists of complete resolutions of individual exponent signatures $(p,q,r)$ of the generalized Fermat equation $A^p+B^q=C^r$, each a near-miss in that it disposes of one slice of the infinite problem.

- **Fermat's Last Theorem (Wiles, with Taylor, 1995).** Settles the equal-exponent case $A^n+B^n=C^n$, $n\ge 3$ — the diagonal of the Beal range. This is the deepest established sub-result and the template for all that follow.
- **Darmon–Merel (1997).** Resolved $x^n+y^n=2z^n$ and the signatures $(n,n,2)$ and $(n,n,3)$ for $n\ge$ small bounds, extending the modular method to non-diagonal exponents.
- **Bennett, Skinner, Kraus, and collaborators (2000s).** A long sequence of papers eliminating mixed signatures via Frey curves and level-lowering, e.g. families $(2,4,n)$, $(2,n,4)$, and assorted $(3,3,n)$-type cases.
- **Poonen–Schaefer–Stoll (2007), Bruin, Siksek, Chen, Dahmen (2000s–2010s).** Resolved hard individual signatures including $(2,3,7)$, $(2,3,8)$, $(2,3,9)$, $(2,4,5)$, $(3,4,5)$, and others, often by elliptic-curve Chabauty or descent where the modular method left residual newforms. Note that signatures involving an exponent equal to 2 lie just *outside* the strict Beal hypothesis $x,y,z>2$ but bound the surrounding Fermat–Catalan landscape.

These collectively confirm Beal on an expanding but still finite catalogue of signatures. No method among them generalizes to all triples.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: The $abc$ route and the Mochizuki dispute
text: Because $abc$ implies Beal up to finitely many exceptions, any accepted proof of the $abc$ conjecture would be a decisive near-resolution. **Shinichi Mochizuki** announced a proof of $abc$ via inter-universal Teichmüller theory in 2012, published in *Publications of RIMS* in 2021. The claim is **disputed**: in 2018 **Peter Scholze and Jakob Stix** circulated a detailed objection ("Why $abc$ is still a conjecture"), arguing that a specific identification in the proof of a key inequality (around "Corollary 3.12") collapses a quantity that must be tracked, invalidating the argument. Mochizuki rejected the objection; the broader number-theory community has not accepted the proof. Stated neutrally: $abc$ remains, by mainstream consensus, open, and hence the $abc$-conditional path to Beal is not currently available.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Claimed direct proofs (retracted, withdrawn, or unrefereed)
text:
The Beal Prize (now \$1{,}000{,}000, administered by the AMS) has attracted a steady stream of purported elementary proofs, almost all from outside the research community. None has passed peer review. Common failure modes, documented by referees and in expository commentary, include:

- **Illegitimate modular-arithmetic reductions** that implicitly assume coprimality conclusions, or that handle only finitely many residues.
- **Errors in infinite descent** that silently require an exponent equal to 2 (where descent genuinely works) and break for $x,y,z>2$.
- **Confusing necessary with sufficient conditions**, e.g. deriving a common-factor relation that holds for *known* solutions and asserting it forbids coprime ones.

In keeping with house policy, no specific amateur preprint is endorsed or refuted here beyond noting that, as of the present, the AMS has not awarded the prize and no claimed elementary proof is accepted. Notably, an elementary proof would be surprising precisely because the nearest established result (FLT) required modularity; a short proof would imply an unexpectedly elementary route to FLT as a corollary, which the community regards as strong prior evidence against any brief argument.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Status of the "near-misses" taken together
text: The honest summary: Beal is **confirmed on infinitely many but not all** specific configurations (every fully resolved signature, plus the diagonal via FLT), is **conditionally near-settled** under $abc$, and has **no accepted general proof or counterexample**. The gap between "every fixed exponent triple" and "all exponent triples uniformly" is exactly where every attempt has stalled.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Key Papers — The Beal Conjecture
text: _The most important papers, old and new. Each carries a verification flag. Identifiers are given only where confidently known; "—" means no identifier is asserted (never fabricated)._

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Key Papers — The Beal Conjecture
text: **Notes on reliability.** Rows 1–5, 7, 17, and 20 are canonical and certainly real; identifiers given there are high-confidence. For the modular-method and generalized-Fermat case studies (rows 6, 8–16, 18, 21–24) the *works and authors are real research in this area*, but exact titles, years, and especially DOIs vary across versions (journal vs. arXiv) and are marked "needs-verification"; identifiers are withheld ("—") rather than guessed. Row 19 (Norvig) is a well-known online computational essay on Beal counterexample search; its publication metadata is informal, hence no DOI. Row 25 is Mochizuki's published IUT paper underlying the *disputed* $abc$ claim relevant to Beal's conditional resolution; the Scholze–Stix objection (2018, unpublished note) is discussed in `attempts.md` and is not listed as a separate row because it has no formal identifier. Rows 16–17 (Tijdeman, Mihăilescu) concern Catalan's conjecture — included because Catalan ($A^x - B^y = 1$) is the companion exponential Diophantine problem and Mihăilescu's 2004 proof is a landmark of the surrounding theory. Human source-checking is required before any "needs-verification" or "ai-suggested" row is treated as established.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Top Mathematicians — The Beal Conjecture
text:
| # | Name | Era | Role | Key contribution |
|---|------|-----|------|------------------|
| 1 | Andrew Beal | Contemporary (1952–) | originator | Formulated the conjecture (c. 1993) from computational evidence; endowed the \$1M Beal Prize. |
| 2 | R. Daniel Mauldin | Contemporary | expositor | Published the conjecture and prize in the *Notices of the AMS* (1997), fixing its name and framing. |
| 3 | Andrew Wiles | Contemporary (1953–) | breakthrough | Proved Fermat's Last Theorem (1995), settling the equal-exponent coprime case and supplying the modular template. |
| 4 | Richard Taylor | Contemporary (1962–) | breakthrough | Co-authored the Hecke-algebra paper completing Wiles's proof; central to the modularity machinery used throughout. |
| 5 | Henri Darmon | Contemporary (1965–) | frontier | With Granville, proved finiteness per signature (1995); with Merel resolved key ternary cases; architect of "Darmon's program." |
| 6 | Andrew Granville | Contemporary (1962–) | frontier | Co-proved the Darmon–Granville finiteness theorem; foundational analytic and Diophantine input to the Fermat–Catalan framework. |
| 7 | Loïc Merel | Contemporary (1965–) | breakthrough | With Darmon, resolved $x^n+y^n=2z^n$ and related signatures via winding quotients (1997). |
| 8 | Michael A. Bennett | Contemporary | frontier | Prolific resolver of generalized-Fermat signatures via the modular method; co-author of major surveys of the equation. |
| 9 | Samir Siksek | Contemporary | frontier | Modular method over number fields, asymptotic Fermat, and explicit Chabauty; key to extending coverage of exponent triples. |
| 10 | Bjorn Poonen | Contemporary (1968–) | frontier | With Schaefer and Stoll, resolved hard signatures (e.g. $x^2+y^3=z^7$) by descent and Chabauty methods. |

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Notes
text: The active community on Beal is essentially the **generalized Fermat / Fermat–Catalan** community, since Beal is the coprime $x,y,z>2$ slice of that problem. The center of gravity is the modular-method school — Darmon's program and its descendants — pursued by groups around Michael Bennett (UBC), Samir Siksek and Nuno Freitas (Warwick / and collaborators), Imin Chen, Sander Dahmen, Bartosz Naskręcki, Nicolas Billerey, Luis Dieulefait, and Michael Stoll, among others. Their work proceeds signature by signature, combining Frey curves, modularity over real and imaginary quadratic fields, level-lowering, and explicit (elliptic-curve) Chabauty. A second, looser community connects Beal to the $abc$ conjecture (Masser, Oesterlé; and the disputed Mochizuki program), since an effective $abc$ would essentially settle Beal. The conjecture also draws sustained, mostly fruitless, attention from amateurs because of the seven-figure prize; the refereeing burden falls on the AMS. No single individual is presently "the" Beal expert — progress is distributed across the modular-method network, and a uniform resolution is widely expected to require an idea beyond the current toolkit.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Status & Frontier — The Beal Conjecture
text: **Status: open.** There is no accepted proof and no known counterexample. The conjecture states that $A^x+B^y=C^z$ with $x,y,z>2$ and $A,B,C$ positive integers forces $\gcd(A,B,C)>1$.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: What is known (unconditional)
text:
- **The diagonal case is closed.** For $x=y=z=n\ge 3$, coprime solutions are impossible by Fermat's Last Theorem (Wiles 1995). This is the deepest established sub-statement.
- **Finiteness per signature.** For every fixed exponent triple $(p,q,r)$ with $\tfrac1p+\tfrac1q+\tfrac1r<1$ — which includes all Beal signatures except $(3,3,3)$ — there are only finitely many coprime solutions (Darmon–Granville 1995, via Faltings). This bound is **ineffective**: it gives no way to enumerate or exclude the solutions.
- **Many individual signatures fully resolved.** A large catalogue of triples $(p,q,r)$ has been completely settled by the modular method and by descent/Chabauty: the families $(n,n,2)$, $(n,n,3)$, and numerous mixed and small-exponent cases (Darmon–Merel, Bennett–Skinner, Kraus, Bruin, Poonen–Schaefer–Stoll, Siksek, Chen, Dahmen, Freitas, and others). Within the strict Beal range these confirm the conjecture for infinitely many — but not all — exponent configurations.
- **Computation.** No coprime counterexample exists within the (large) ranges of bases and exponents that have been searched.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: What is known (conditional)
text: - **Under the $abc$ conjecture**, Beal holds for all but finitely many solutions; with a sufficiently effective form of $abc$ it would follow entirely. The $abc$ conjecture itself is open: Mochizuki's claimed proof (published 2021) is **disputed**, with the Scholze–Stix objection (2018) unresolved by community consensus.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: What a full resolution would require
text:
The essential gap is **uniformity across infinitely many exponent triples**. Every effective method is tied to a single signature $(p,q,r)$; Faltings' finiteness is ineffective and non-uniform. A complete proof would need one of:

1. an **unconditional, effective $abc$** (or a Beal-tailored Diophantine inequality) strong enough to bound the smooth terms;
2. a **uniform Frey-curve / modularity construction** covering all hyperbolic signatures at once, rather than case by case; or
3. a genuinely new structural idea linking the coprimality hypothesis to a global obstruction.

A short, elementary proof is regarded as unlikely: it would imply an unexpectedly elementary route to FLT (a corollary of Beal), contrary to the depth FLT is known to require. The most plausible near-term routes are incremental — extending the modular method over number fields (asymptotic-Fermat techniques) to swallow ever-larger families of signatures — while a clean general theorem awaits progress on $abc$ or a new method.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Related problems
text:
- [The abc Conjecture](../abc-conjecture/README.md) — implies Beal up to finitely many exceptions; the natural conditional route.
- [Fermat's Last Theorem context: Goldbach Conjecture](../goldbach-conjecture/README.md) — companion additive/multiplicative number-theory problem of comparable fame.
- [The Erdős–Straus Conjecture](../erdos-straus-conjecture/README.md) — another elementary-to-state Diophantine equation resistant to general proof.
- [The Riemann Hypothesis](../riemann-hypothesis/README.md) — the central conditional-results hub of analytic number theory bordering this area.
- [Odd Perfect Numbers](../odd-perfect-numbers/README.md) — a sibling "elementary statement, deep obstruction" problem in multiplicative number theory.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
text: > **Atlas Rank #6** · CSS **78.8** · Field: *Number Theory* · Posed: **1965** · Status: `open`  · 🏛️ **Millennium Prize Problem**

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Plain statement
text: The rank of the group of rational points of an elliptic curve equals the order of vanishing of its L-function at s = 1.

*Originators: Bryan Birch, Peter Swinnerton-Dyer.*

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: History — The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
text:
_Origin, formulation, and timeline._

The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer (BSD) conjecture was born from numerical experiment, not abstract speculation. In the early 1960s, working in Cambridge, Bryan Birch and Peter Swinnerton-Dyer used the EDSAC 2 computer — one of the first machines available to academic mathematicians — to count the number $N_p$ of points modulo $p$ on a fixed elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$, for many primes $p$. Their guiding intuition was that an elliptic curve with many rational points should also have, on average, many points modulo $p$. They examined the partial products
$$
\prod_{p \le X} \frac{N_p}{p},
$$
and observed empirically that this grew like $C(\log X)^r$, where $r$ appeared to equal the rank of the Mordell–Weil group $E(\mathbb{Q})$. Through the analytic class number formula and the work of Hasse, this product is heuristically tied to the behaviour of the Hasse–Weil $L$-function $L(E,s)$ near $s=1$. Reorganized in the language of $L$-functions, the data suggested the now-canonical statement: the order of vanishing of $L(E,s)$ at $s=1$ (the analytic rank) equals the algebraic rank of $E(\mathbb{Q})$.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: History — The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
text:
The **weak** form asserts equality of analytic and algebraic ranks. The **strong** (refined) form, formulated through the 1960s and sharpened by Tate, gives the leading Taylor coefficient explicitly:
$$
\lim_{s\to 1}\frac{L(E,s)}{(s-1)^r} = \frac{\#\Sha(E)\cdot \Omega_E \cdot \mathrm{Reg}(E)\cdot \prod_p c_p}{(\#E(\mathbb{Q})_{\mathrm{tors}})^2},
$$
involving the (conjecturally finite) Tate–Shafarevich group $\Sha$, the real period $\Omega_E$, the regulator, Tamagawa numbers $c_p$, and torsion. At the time of formulation, the very analytic continuation of $L(E,s)$ to $s=1$ was itself conjectural, contingent on modularity.

A foundational subtlety: BSD presupposes machinery proven only decades later. The analytic continuation and functional equation of $L(E,s)$ for $E/\mathbb{Q}$ follow from the modularity theorem (Wiles, Taylor–Wiles, then Breuil–Conrad–Diamond–Taylor, 2001). Before modularity, BSD's left-hand side was not even known to be defined for general $E/\mathbb{Q}$.

**Timeline**

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: History — The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
text:
**Timeline**

- **1922** — Mordell proves $E(\mathbb{Q})$ is finitely generated (the Mordell–Weil theorem for $\mathbb{Q}$), giving meaning to "rank."
- **1928** — Weil generalizes Mordell's theorem to abelian varieties over number fields.
- **1958–1965** — Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer run EDSAC computations; conjecture announced and published (Crelle, 1965).
- **1965** — Tate formulates the refined conjecture and the BSD formula for the leading coefficient.
- **1967** — Tate's "On the conjectures of Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer and a geometric analog" surveys and clarifies the statement.
- **1977** — Coates and Wiles prove that for CM elliptic curves with $L(E,1)\ne 0$, the rank is $0$ (first major theoretical evidence).
- **1983** — Gross and Zagier prove their formula relating $L'(E,1)$ to heights of Heegner points.
- **1986** — Kolyvagin's method of Euler systems shows analytic rank $0$ or $1$ implies equal algebraic rank (modular curves), with $\Sha$ finite in those cases.
- **1995–2001** — Modularity of all elliptic curves over $\mathbb{Q}$ established (Wiles; Taylor–Wiles; Breuil–Conrad–Diamond–Taylor), validating analytic continuation of $L(E,s)$.
- **2000** — BSD named one of the seven Clay Millennium Prize Problems ($1,000,000).
- **2010s** — Bhargava–Shankar bound average ranks; Bhargava–Skinner–Zhang show BSD holds for a positive proportion of curves. Skinner–Urban and others advance Iwasawa Main Conjectures, controlling $\Sha$ via $p$-adic $L$-functions.
- **Present** — Weak BSD remains open in rank $\ge 2$; the conjecture is a theorem only in analytic rank $0$ and $1$ (with finiteness of $\Sha$ proven only there, prime-by-prime in the refined form).

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
text:
_Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

The conjecture bears the names of two British number theorists whose collaboration at Cambridge in the late 1950s and early 1960s fused classical Diophantine theory with the then-novel resource of electronic computation.

**Bryan John Birch** (born 25 September 1931, Burton-upon-Trent; died 28 November 2025) was an English mathematician educated at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he completed his doctorate under J. W. S. (Ian) Cassels, the dominant figure in postwar British number theory. Birch's early work already touched the questions that would define his career: his 1957 result that every sufficiently large integer is a sum of a bounded number of cubes (now "Birch's theorem" on forms in many variables) showed his command of the circle method and the arithmetic of equations. He held positions at Manchester before settling at Oxford, where he became Professor of Arithmetic and a long-serving fixture of the number theory community. Beyond BSD, Birch's name attaches to **Heegner points** — he revived and systematized Kurt Heegner's neglected construction of rational points on modular curves, a tool that later became central to the proven cases of his own conjecture via the Gross–Zagier formula. The Birch–Tate conjecture (relating $K_2$ of rings of integers to special values of zeta functions) is another of his contributions.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
text: **Sir Henry Peter Francis Swinnerton-Dyer, 16th Baronet** (2 August 1927 – 26 December 2018) came from a different intellectual milieu but the same institution. Educated at Eton and Trinity College, Cambridge, he was a polymath whose work spanned the arithmetic of cubic and quartic surfaces, the theory of modular forms, analytic number theory, and the geometry of Diophantine equations. He was a master of explicit computation in the pre-computer and early-computer eras and an early, fearless user of EDSAC and EDSAC 2 for serious mathematical experiment. His administrative career was equally weighty: he served as Master of St Catharine's College, Vice-Chancellor of Cambridge University, and chairman of the University Grants Committee, shaping British higher education policy. He was knighted (KBE) and inherited a baronetcy, but among mathematicians his enduring fame is the conjecture.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
text: The collaboration that produced BSD reflected complementary strengths. Both men were steeped in the theory of elliptic curves and the Mordell–Weil theorem, which guaranteed that the rational points of an elliptic curve form a finitely generated group $E(\mathbb{Q}) \cong \mathbb{Z}^r \oplus T$ but said nothing computable about the rank $r$. There was, at the time, no general algorithm — and there still is none proven to terminate — to compute $r$. Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer's decisive move was to seek an *analytic* signature of the rank in the local point counts $N_p$, encoded in the $L$-function. Their genius was statistical and experimental: they trusted patterns extracted from large-scale numerical data, then articulated those patterns as a precise arithmetic-analytic identity. This was a methodological landmark — among the first conjectures of the highest importance to be discovered by computer.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
text: The modern formulation owes much to **John Tate**, who recast the conjecture in the language of $L$-functions and Galois cohomology and supplied the refined formula with the Tate–Shafarevich group and Tamagawa factors. Thus the "historical root" (an empirical law about $\prod N_p/p$) and the "modern statement" (equality of analytic and algebraic rank, with an explicit leading coefficient) are two faces of one insight. Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer's legacy is not merely a hard problem but a template: arithmetic invariants of varieties are governed by the analytic behaviour of their $L$-functions — the central creed of the Langlands era and of the Bloch–Kato conjectures that generalize BSD.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Approaches — The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
text: _Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Heegner points and the Gross–Zagier formula
text: The most successful concrete attack constructs rational points directly. **Heegner points** are images of CM points on modular curves; under modular parametrization $X_0(N)\to E$ they yield rational (or near-rational) points on $E$. The **Gross–Zagier formula** (1986) computes the canonical (Néron–Tate) height of a Heegner point in terms of the derivative $L'(E,1)$ of the $L$-function. Its decisive consequence: when the analytic rank is exactly $1$, the Heegner point is non-torsion, so the algebraic rank is at least $1$. Combined with Kolyvagin's bound below, this establishes BSD (weak form) in analytic rank $1$. **Barrier:** Heegner points live in the rank-$1$ eigenspace and produce a *single* independent point. The method is structurally incapable of generating two independent points, so it does not reach rank $\ge 2$. Generalizations (Stark–Heegner / Darmon points, $p$-adic and higher-dimensional analogues) remain largely conjectural.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Euler systems and Kolyvagin's method
text: Kolyvagin (1988–1990) introduced **Euler systems** — compatible families of cohomology classes built from Heegner points — to bound the Selmer group and hence the Tate–Shafarevich group $\Sha$ from above. He proved: if the analytic rank is $0$ or $1$, then the algebraic rank equals it *and* $\Sha(E)$ is finite. This is the deepest unconditional structural result toward BSD. **Best result:** full weak BSD plus finiteness of $\Sha$ in analytic ranks $0$ and $1$. **Barrier:** the Euler system derived from Heegner points encodes information only up to rank $1$. No Euler system is known that controls Selmer groups in analytic rank $\ge 2$; constructing one is a central open problem.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Iwasawa theory and Main Conjectures
text: Iwasawa theory studies how Selmer groups and $p$-adic $L$-functions vary up a tower of cyclotomic (or anticyclotomic) extensions. The **Iwasawa Main Conjecture** equates a characteristic ideal of a Selmer module with a $p$-adic $L$-function, packaging the arithmetic of $\Sha$ and the BSD leading term $p$-adically. **Best results:** Rubin proved the CM case; Kato's Euler system gave one divisibility for modular forms; **Skinner–Urban** (2014) proved the reverse divisibility for a large class of curves, yielding the full main conjecture for many $E$. This lets one verify the $p$-part of the *refined* BSD formula for individual primes $p$ in ranks $0$ and $1$. **Barrier:** controls only one prime at a time and presupposes analytic rank $\le 1$ for the strongest conclusions; the $p$-adic and complex $L$-values are linked but not identical, and exceptional ("trivial zero") cases need separate treatment.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Average ranks and the geometry of numbers
text: Rather than a single curve, **Bhargava–Shankar** (2010s) bound the *average* size of $n$-Selmer groups over all elliptic curves ordered by height, using orbit-counting in representations of arithmetic groups (geometry of numbers). They proved the average rank is bounded (below $1$), so a positive proportion of curves have rank $0$ and a positive proportion have rank $1$. **Bhargava–Skinner–Zhang** combined this with Gross–Zagier–Kolyvagin to show **BSD holds for a positive proportion (over 66%) of elliptic curves over $\mathbb{Q}$**. **Barrier:** these are statistical statements; they do not resolve any individual curve of rank $\ge 2$, and the methods say nothing about the high-rank tail.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: $p$-adic $L$-functions and $p$-adic BSD
text: A parallel programme (Mazur–Tate–Teitelbaum, Perrin-Riou) formulates BSD-type statements for $p$-adic $L$-functions $L_p(E,s)$, where the order of vanishing and leading coefficient are more accessible. **Best result:** strong evidence and many proven cases of the $p$-adic main conjecture; exceptional-zero phenomena governed by the $\mathcal{L}$-invariant. **Barrier:** transferring $p$-adic conclusions to the complex (archimedean) BSD requires comparison results that are themselves deep and partly conjectural.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Descent and explicit computation
text:
Classical **$n$-descent** computes Selmer groups $\mathrm{Sel}^{(n)}(E)$, giving an upper bound for the rank and a lower bound via found points; the gap is exactly the $n$-part of $\Sha$. Modern algorithms (Cremona's tables, magma/pari implementations, visualization of $\Sha$, second/higher descents) verify BSD numerically for vast databases of curves and detect non-trivial $\Sha$. **Barrier:** descent is conditional on the finiteness of $\Sha$ to terminate as a rank algorithm — and that finiteness is itself a (proven only in rank $\le 1$) special case of BSD. There is no unconditional, provably terminating algorithm to compute the rank of a general $E/\mathbb{Q}$.

**Overarching obstruction.** Every known unconditional route to the algebraic rank passes through Heegner-point / Euler-system technology that saturates at rank $1$. Breaking into rank $\ge 2$ — producing arithmetic objects that certify two or more independent rational points from analytic data — is the recognized fundamental barrier, and no current method overcomes it.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Attempts — The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
text:
_Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

**Coates–Wiles (1977): the first theoretical foothold.** John Coates and Andrew Wiles proved that for an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$ with complex multiplication, $L(E,1)\ne 0$ implies $E(\mathbb{Q})$ is finite (rank $0$). This was the first general theorem connecting non-vanishing of the $L$-function to the rank — a partial converse to BSD in the simplest case — and it established the basic shape of all later progress: control the rank from above via $L$-values.

**Gross–Zagier and Kolyvagin: the rank $\le 1$ summit.** The combination of the Gross–Zagier height formula (1986) with Kolyvagin's Euler system (1988–90) settled weak BSD, and finiteness of $\Sha$, whenever the analytic rank is $0$ or $1$. This remains the strongest unconditional result. It is a near-miss in the sense that it stops exactly at rank $1$: the proof produces at most one independent Heegner point, and there is no analogue delivering two independent points, so analytic rank $\ge 2$ is untouched.

**Bhargava–Skinner–Zhang (2010s): a positive proportion.** By bounding average Selmer ranks (Bhargava–Shankar) and feeding curves of analytic rank $\le 1$ into Gross–Zagier–Kolyvagin, they proved BSD for a positive proportion of all elliptic curves over $\mathbb{Q}$ — widely quoted as exceeding $66\%$. This is a genuine advance but explicitly statistical: it certifies no specific curve of rank $\ge 2$ and reveals nothing about the high-rank regime.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Attempts — The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
text:
**Skinner–Urban (2014) and the $p$-part of the refined formula.** Their proof of the Iwasawa Main Conjecture for many modular forms allowed verification of the $p$-part of the *strong* BSD formula (the exact leading Taylor coefficient) for individual primes $p$, in ranks $0$ and $1$. The refined conjecture is therefore known prime-by-prime in those ranks for large classes of curves, but never simultaneously for all primes, and never in rank $\ge 2$.

**Numerical and computational verification.** Tables by John Cremona and others verify BSD (including, where computable, the $\Sha$ prediction) for hundreds of thousands of curves of small conductor. These computations have never produced a counterexample and have repeatedly confirmed predicted values of $\#\Sha$ — strong empirical support, but not proof, and contingent on the unproven finiteness of $\Sha$ in higher rank.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Attempts — The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
text:
**Disputed and erroneous claims.** Because BSD is a Millennium Prize Problem, it attracts purported solutions, almost all from outside the research community, posted to preprint servers or vanity outlets. None has survived expert scrutiny; common defects include conflating analytic and algebraic rank without justification, assuming finiteness of $\Sha$ (itself open), or misusing the modularity dictionary. No such claim has been accepted by referees or by the Clay Mathematics Institute, and as of this writing no announced proof — correct or disputed — has any standing in the literature. The honest status is that the rank $\ge 2$ case is genuinely open, with no credible attempt having breached it.

**The structural near-miss worth naming** is the absence of a higher Euler system. Researchers broadly agree that an Euler system controlling Selmer groups in analytic rank $\ge 2$ — or a height formula expressing higher derivatives $L^{(r)}(E,1)$ in terms of a regulator of $r$ independent points — would resolve the weak conjecture. Both remain conjectural, and constructing them is the recognized frontier rather than a completed attempt.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Key Papers — The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
text: Notes: Several identifiers are deliberately left as "—" where I could not confirm the exact DOI/arXiv id; the works themselves (rows 1–2, 4, 6, 8–10, 12–17, 21) are canonical and certainly real. Row 7 is included only as a placeholder pointing toward the modularity thread and is flagged ai-suggested; the substantive modularity papers are rows 13–15. Birch–Swinnerton-Dyer's "Notes on elliptic curves. I" (1963) is the companion to row 2 and is equally foundational. Where a paper's year or precise title may be off (e.g., rows 3, 5, 19, 24, 25), the Verify flag is set to needs-verification rather than asserting certainty.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Top Mathematicians — The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
text:
_Ten figures who shaped the problem, past and present._

| # | Name | Era | Role | Key contribution |
|---|------|-----|------|------------------|
| 1 | Bryan Birch | 1960s–2020s | originator | Co-formulated BSD from EDSAC data; revived Heegner points, central to all proven cases |
| 2 | Peter Swinnerton-Dyer | 1960s–2010s | originator | Co-formulated BSD; pioneered large-scale computer experiment in number theory |
| 3 | John Tate | 1960s–2010s | originator | Recast BSD via $L$-functions and Galois cohomology; refined formula with $\Sha$ and Tamagawa numbers |
| 4 | Louis Mordell | 1920s | originator | Mordell theorem: $E(\mathbb{Q})$ finitely generated — defines the rank BSD predicts |
| 5 | John Coates | 1970s–2010s | breakthrough | With Wiles, first $L$-value-to-rank theorem (CM curves, rank $0$) |
| 6 | Andrew Wiles | 1970s–present | breakthrough | Coates–Wiles theorem; modularity, which makes $L(E,s)$ analytically continue |
| 7 | Benedict Gross | 1980s–present | breakthrough | Gross–Zagier formula linking $L'(E,1)$ to Heegner point heights |
| 8 | Don Zagier | 1980s–present | breakthrough | Co-author of the Gross–Zagier formula |
| 9 | Victor Kolyvagin | 1980s–1990s | breakthrough | Euler systems; weak BSD and finiteness of $\Sha$ in analytic rank $0$ and $1$ |
| 10 | Manjul Bhargava | 2010s–present | frontier | Selmer-group averages; BSD for a positive proportion of curves (with Skinner, Zhang) |

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Notes
text: The contemporary community is broad and highly active, even though the central rank-$\ge 2$ case is untouched. Key frontier figures beyond the table include **Christopher Skinner** and **Eric Urban** (Iwasawa Main Conjectures for $\mathrm{GL}_2$), **Wei Zhang** (arithmetic of $L$-functions, Gan–Gross–Prasad, positive-proportion BSD), **Karl Rubin** (Euler systems, CM main conjecture), **Kazuya Kato** (Euler system for modular forms), **Henri Darmon** and **Massimo Bertolini** (Stark–Heegner / Darmon points and $p$-adic $L$-functions aiming beyond rank $1$), and **Kartik Prasanna**, **Xinyi Yuan**, **Shou-Wu Zhang**, and **Daniel Disegni** (generalized Gross–Zagier and arithmetic intersection theory). Computational verification is sustained by **John Cremona** and the LMFDB collaboration. The problem sits at the heart of arithmetic geometry and the Langlands programme, and its generalizations (Bloch–Kato, Beilinson–Bloch–Kato for motives) keep a large international cohort engaged. The consensus is that genuinely new ideas — a higher Euler system or a rank-$\ge 2$ height formula — are required, and progress in the 2010s has been incremental and statistical rather than a breach of the core barrier.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Status & Frontier — The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
text:
_Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

**Status: open.** BSD is one of the seven Clay Millennium Prize Problems and remains unsolved in general. It is a theorem only in low analytic rank, and even the refined (strong) form is established only prime-by-prime there.

**What is known (unconditional).**
- *Analytic rank $0$:* If $L(E,1)\ne 0$ then $E(\mathbb{Q})$ has rank $0$ and $\Sha(E)$ is finite (Kolyvagin, building on Gross–Zagier; valid for all $E/\mathbb{Q}$ via modularity).
- *Analytic rank $1$:* If $\mathrm{ord}_{s=1}L(E,s)=1$ then the algebraic rank is $1$ and $\Sha(E)$ is finite (Gross–Zagier supplies a non-torsion Heegner point; Kolyvagin bounds the Selmer group).
- *Statistical:* The average rank of elliptic curves over $\mathbb{Q}$ is bounded (Bhargava–Shankar), and BSD holds for a **positive proportion** (over $66\%$) of all curves ordered by height (Bhargava–Skinner–Zhang).
- *Refined formula:* For many curves of analytic rank $\le 1$, the $p$-part of the exact leading-coefficient formula is verified for individual primes $p$ via the Iwasawa Main Conjecture (Skinner–Urban, Kato, Rubin in the CM case).

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Status & Frontier — The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
text:
**What is known (conditional).** Granting finiteness of $\Sha$, descent algorithms compute the rank in practice and the BSD formula has been numerically confirmed for vast tables (Cremona, LMFDB) with no counterexample. Granting the relevant Iwasawa main conjectures and $p$-adic comparison results, the strong form follows in additional cases. The parity conjecture (that the analytic and algebraic ranks have the same parity) is known in considerable generality.

**What a full resolution requires.**
1. **Rank $\ge 2$, weak form.** No method produces two independent rational points from the vanishing $L^{(2)}(E,1)\ne 0$. A resolution needs either a *higher Euler system* controlling Selmer groups in higher rank, or a *higher Gross–Zagier formula* expressing $L^{(r)}(E,1)$ as a regulator of $r$ independent cohomological/cycle classes that are provably rational and independent.
2. **Finiteness of $\Sha$ in general.** Unproven for rank $\ge 2$; without it there is no terminating rank algorithm. This is arguably the deepest sub-problem.
3. **Strong form for all primes simultaneously.** Even in rank $\le 1$, the exact leading coefficient is verified prime-by-prime; assembling all primes (including bad-reduction and exceptional-zero cases) into the full rational identity remains incomplete.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Status & Frontier — The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
text: **Plausible routes.** (a) Anticyclotomic Iwasawa theory and **Darmon / Stark–Heegner points** extended beyond rank $1$; (b) the **Gan–Gross–Prasad / arithmetic theta** circle and generalized Gross–Zagier (Yuan–Zhang–Zhang, Disegni) pushing height formulas to higher-dimensional cycles; (c) advances in the geometry of numbers raising the proven proportion toward $100\%$ while never settling individual high-rank curves; (d) entirely new input from the Langlands programme or motivic cohomology (Beilinson–Bloch–Kato), of which BSD is the genus-1 case. None is near completion; the rank-$\ge 2$ barrier is regarded as requiring a fundamentally new idea.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Related problems
text:
- [Riemann Hypothesis](../riemann-hypothesis/README.md) — both concern special values and zeros of $L$/zeta functions; BSD is the prototypical "special value at $s=1$" statement.
- [Hodge Conjecture](../hodge-conjecture/README.md) — sibling Millennium problem in arithmetic/algebraic geometry; both relate algebraic cycles to cohomological/analytic data.
- [Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture](../hardy-littlewood-k-tuple/README.md) — like BSD, born from heuristic/numerical reasoning about local-to-global densities (here Euler products of $N_p/p$).
- [abc Conjecture](../abc-conjecture/README.md) — neighbouring problem in Diophantine arithmetic with strong consequences for rational points and heights on curves.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Plain statement
text: The only smooth, strictly convex billiard tables whose dynamics are integrable are ellipses.
