*Originators: George David Birkhoff.*

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: History — The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
text: _Origin, formulation, and timeline._

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Origin and setting
text:
A **mathematical billiard** in a smooth, strictly convex planar domain $\Omega$ models a point mass moving in straight lines and reflecting off $\partial\Omega$ with equal angles of incidence and reflection. The dynamics define the *billiard map* on the phase cylinder of oriented chords, parametrized by arclength $s$ along the boundary and the angle $\varphi\in(0,\pi)$ of reflection. This map is an area-preserving twist map, and its study lies at the crossroads of dynamical systems, the calculus of variations, and integrable systems.

George David Birkhoff introduced convex billiards as a tractable model embodying the qualitative features of Hamiltonian dynamics. In his 1927 monograph *Dynamical Systems* and surrounding work, Birkhoff observed that the billiard inside an **ellipse** is *integrable*: the phase space is foliated by invariant curves, and every trajectory has a *caustic* — a confocal ellipse or hyperbola to which it remains tangent. The conserved quantity is the product of angular momenta about the two foci (the "Joachimsthal integral"). The conjecture asks whether ellipses are the *only* such tables.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Precise formulation and reformulations
text:
The statement is sensitive to what "integrable" means, and the modern literature distinguishes several inequivalent versions.

- **Global integrability.** The billiard map admits a foliation of an open neighborhood of the boundary (in the phase cylinder) by continuous invariant curves, equivalently the table possesses a continuous family of caustics. The conjecture: such $\Omega$ is an ellipse (a disk being the degenerate case).
- **Local / rational integrability (Birkhoff–Poritsky form).** Following the formulation attributed to Hillel Poritsky (1950) and emphasized by Sergei Tabachnikov, one assumes the existence of an integrable region foliated by invariant curves accumulating on the boundary, with no requirement of integrability far from $\partial\Omega$. This *local* version is the form most modern theorems address.
- **Rational integrability.** A weaker hypothesis requiring only that the invariant curves consisting of $q$-periodic orbits (for all $q\ge 3$) form smooth curves — the version solved for perturbations of ellipses.

Whitney/Bialy-type total-integrability results and the Hopf-rigidity philosophy connect the problem to the absence of conjugate points and to spectral rigidity.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Timeline
text:
**1927** — G. D. Birkhoff publishes *Dynamical Systems*; convex billiards and their variational (least-action) structure are systematized. The integrability of the ellipse is folklore by this period; the conjecture that ellipses are the only integrable tables is attributed to Birkhoff in this era (sometimes dated to remarks around 1927–1930).

**1950** — Hillel Poritsky, in *The billiard ball problem on a table with a convex boundary*, gives a careful treatment and an early local rigidity argument; the "Birkhoff–Poritsky" local formulation traces here.

**1973** — H. Lazutkin proves existence of a positive-measure Cantor family of caustics (invariant curves) near the boundary of any smooth strictly convex billiard — a KAM-type result showing near-boundary "near-integrability" is generic, sharpening what genuine integrability must mean.

**1993** — Misha Bialy proves a global Hopf-type rigidity theorem: if the entire phase space (cylinder) is foliated by continuous invariant curves, the table is a disk. This settles the *strongest* global version.

**1995–1996** — Sergei Bolotin, and work in the geodesic-flow analogue, develop polynomial-integrability rigidity.

**2010s** — Vadim Kaloshin, Alfonso Sorrentino, and collaborators reframe the local conjecture via deformation/spectral methods.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Timeline
text:
**1995–1996** — Sergei Bolotin, and work in the geodesic-flow analogue, develop polynomial-integrability rigidity.

**2010s** — Vadim Kaloshin, Alfonso Sorrentino, and collaborators reframe the local conjecture via deformation/spectral methods.

**2017** — Avila, De Simoi, and Kaloshin prove **local rational integrability rigidity**: a small smooth perturbation of an ellipse of small eccentricity that remains rationally integrable must itself be an ellipse (published *Annals of Mathematics*, 2018).

**2018** — Kaloshin and Sorrentino establish **local integrability rigidity** near any ellipse: an integrable (in the Birkhoff sense) deformation of a generic ellipse is an ellipse (*Annals of Mathematics*, 2018).

**2018–2024** — Bialy and Mironov prove the **polynomial Birkhoff conjecture** (integrals polynomial in velocity) and announce progress toward the centrally-symmetric "rational" case via the algebraic/angular-momentum approach.

**Present** — The general conjecture (arbitrary integrable convex tables, not necessarily near an ellipse) remains **open**. The frontier is local rigidity around ellipses of all eccentricities and removing the near-circular smallness hypotheses.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Originator(s) — The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
text: _Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: George David Birkhoff (1884–1944)
text:
George David Birkhoff was the leading American mathematician of his generation and the central figure in establishing the United States as a research power in dynamical systems. Born in Overisel, Michigan, to Dutch immigrant parents, he studied at the University of Chicago, completing his Ph.D. in 1907 under E. H. Moore (with profound early influence from Maxime Bôcher and from the orbit-mechanics tradition of Henri Poincaré, whom Birkhoff regarded as his true mathematical mentor though they never formally worked together). After positions at Wisconsin and Princeton, he joined Harvard in 1912, where he remained for the rest of his career and trained a generation of analysts and dynamicists, including Marston Morse and Hassler Whitney.

Birkhoff's defining early triumph was his 1913 proof of **Poincaré's Last Geometric Theorem** (the "Poincaré–Birkhoff fixed point theorem"): an area-preserving homeomorphism of an annulus that twists the two boundary circles in opposite directions has at least two fixed points. This result, conjectured by Poincaré shortly before his death, was the seed of modern symplectic and variational dynamics — and it bears directly on billiards, whose return map is precisely an area-preserving annulus (cylinder) twist map.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: The motivation behind the conjecture
text:
Billiards entered Birkhoff's program as the simplest non-trivial geometric model in which the full machinery of Hamiltonian dynamics — periodic orbits, invariant curves, ergodicity, the calculus of variations — could be exhibited concretely. In *Dynamical Systems* (1927), Birkhoff used the billiard to illustrate his variational method for producing periodic orbits: closed billiard trajectories are critical points of the perimeter (length) functional on inscribed polygons, and the Poincaré–Birkhoff theorem guarantees orbits of every rotation number.

Within this picture, *integrability* — the organization of all motions into a smooth family of invariant curves with a conserved quantity — is the most special, most orderly behavior possible. The ellipse exhibits it perfectly: its confocal caustics give a global integral. Birkhoff's conviction, recorded in his writings and transmitted through the oral tradition of his school, was that this perfection is rigid — that *no other shape* can sustain a continuous family of caustics. The conjecture thus encodes a deep intuition that integrability is an exceptional, essentially algebraic phenomenon, not a robust one.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Historical root vs. modern formulation
text: Birkhoff stated the integrability intuition informally; he did not isolate the carefully hedged hypotheses that modern rigidity theorems require. The sharpened **local** formulation — integrability only in a neighborhood of the boundary, where caustics accumulate on $\partial\Omega$ — is generally credited to **Hillel Poritsky** (1950), who recognized that global integrability is too strong to be the natural object. Consequently the live problem is often called the **Birkhoff–Poritsky conjecture**. Lazutkin's 1973 discovery that *positive-measure* families of caustics always exist near the boundary further clarified that the conjecture concerns *full* foliation, not mere abundance, of invariant curves.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Legacy
text: Birkhoff's broader legacy is immense: the pointwise ergodic theorem (1931), foundational work on the three-body problem, the dynamical systems framework itself, and (controversially) an aesthetic measure and a relativistic gravitation theory. His billiard conjecture remains one of the most cited open problems in dynamics, animating the work of Lazutkin, Bialy, Tabachnikov, Kaloshin, Sorrentino, and Mironov. That a model Birkhoff devised as a pedagogical illustration still resists resolution a century later is a testament to how sharply he located the boundary between order and chaos.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Approaches — The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
text: _Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Hopf-type rigidity (total integrability)
text:
**Core idea.** Adapt E. Hopf's theorem (a Riemannian metric on the torus without conjugate points is flat) to the billiard return map. If the *entire* phase cylinder is foliated by continuous invariant curves (graphs over the boundary circle), an integral-geometric inequality forces the curvature to be constant.

**Best result.** Misha Bialy (1993) proved: if the phase space of a convex billiard is *globally* foliated by continuous invariant curves, the table is a **disk**. This is a complete, unconditional theorem — but for the strongest, global form of integrability.

**Barrier.** The hypothesis (foliation of the *whole* cylinder, including orbits hitting the boundary nearly tangentially and nearly perpendicularly) is far stronger than the Birkhoff–Poritsky local form. Hopf rigidity says nothing about tables integrable only near the boundary, which is the genuinely hard case.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Polynomial / algebraic integrability
text:
**Core idea.** Restrict to integrals that are polynomial in the velocity (momentum) components. The ellipse's Joachimsthal integral is quadratic, so one asks: are there integrable billiards with a polynomial first integral of any degree, other than circles and ellipses (and confocal/quadric tables)?

**Best result.** Bialy and Mironov, in a sustained program (2010s–2020s), proved rigidity for polynomial integrals: convex billiards admitting a polynomial-in-momenta integral are conics. Their machinery uses the angular-momentum integral, algebraic geometry of the boundary curve, and analysis of the integral's behavior at the curve. This resolves the **algebraic Birkhoff conjecture** in significant generality and underpins their attack on the centrally symmetric case.

**Barrier.** Genuine integrability need not be polynomial — caustics can be encoded by real-analytic or merely continuous integrals. Reducing general integrability to polynomial integrability is not available, so the algebraic theorems do not imply the analytic conjecture.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Deformation / spectral (Kaloshin–Sorrentino) approach
text:
**Core idea.** Treat the conjecture as a *rigidity* statement: an integrable deformation of an ellipse must be an ellipse. One linearizes the integrability condition along a one-parameter family of tables, obtaining infinitely many constraints from the preserved $q$-periodic orbits and their action ("Mather $\beta$-function"). The annihilation of all deformations except the trivial conformal-affine ones is shown via Fourier analysis on the boundary and properties of the action spectrum. The technique is intertwined with the *dynamical spectral rigidity* program (whether the length/marked-length spectrum determines the table), via Laplace-spectrum and isospectral deformation arguments.

**Best results.**
- Avila, De Simoi, Kaloshin (2016/2018): a *rationally integrable* small smooth perturbation of an ellipse of **small eccentricity** is an ellipse.
- Kaloshin, Sorrentino (2018): **local Birkhoff rigidity** — an integrable deformation of a generic ellipse (any eccentricity, in the local Birkhoff–Poritsky sense) is an ellipse.
- Subsequent work (Huang, Kaloshin, Sorrentino; Koval) pushes toward removing smallness-of-eccentricity hypotheses and toward rational integrability for all ellipses; Comlan Koval's results (2020s) extend rigidity to ellipses of arbitrary eccentricity under rational-integrability assumptions.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Deformation / spectral (Kaloshin–Sorrentino) approach
text: **Barrier.** These are *local* theorems: they require the table to be $C^\infty$- (or analytically) close to an *ellipse*. They give no information about integrable tables far from the family of conics, and the perturbative estimates degenerate as eccentricity grows or as smoothness drops.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Caustics and the calculus of variations
text:
**Core idea.** Integrability is equivalent to existence of a continuous family of caustics filling a neighborhood of the boundary; caustics correspond to invariant curves of the billiard map and to solutions of an associated string/evolute construction. One studies the geometry and analytic continuation of caustics directly.

**Best result.** Lazutkin (1973) proved that a smooth strictly convex billiard always has a **positive-measure Cantor set** of caustics accumulating on the boundary (a KAM phenomenon), with explicit asymptotics ("Lazutkin coordinates"). This shows near-integrability is universal — and therefore that the conjecture is about the *completeness* of the caustic foliation, not its existence.

**Barrier.** Lazutkin's caustics generically form a Cantor set with gaps (Aubry–Mather sets / Birkhoff instability regions fill the gaps); proving the gaps must vanish for an integrable table is exactly the open difficulty. Mather's destruction-of-invariant-curves results show how delicate full foliation is.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Length-spectrum / inverse-spectral connections
text:
**Core idea.** The set of perimeters of periodic billiard orbits (the length spectrum) is closely tied to the Laplace eigenvalues (Weyl law, wave trace). Integrability constrains the length spectrum strongly; conversely, spectral rigidity for ellipses feeds back into Birkhoff rigidity.

**Best result.** Hezari–Zelditch and De Simoi–Kaloshin–Wei established dynamical/Laplace spectral rigidity for ellipses of small eccentricity and for centrally symmetric analytic domains, results technically entangled with the integrability rigidity theorems.

**Barrier.** Spectral determination of the domain is itself open in general ("can one hear the shape of a drum" remains negative in some classes), so this route inherits the same locality and symmetry restrictions.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Net assessment
text: Every unconditional theorem either assumes *global* integrability (Bialy), a *polynomial* integral (Bialy–Mironov), or *proximity to an ellipse* (Avila–De Simoi–Kaloshin, Kaloshin–Sorrentino, Koval). The conjecture in full — arbitrary smooth strictly convex tables, locally integrable in the Birkhoff–Poritsky sense, with no nearness assumption — remains beyond all current methods.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Attempts — The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
text: _Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Lazutkin's caustics (1973) — a clarifying near-miss
text: Vladimir Lazutkin proved that every smooth strictly convex billiard carries a positive-measure family of caustics accumulating on the boundary, via a KAM construction with the now-standard "Lazutkin coordinates." This was initially read by some as evidence that integrability might be common; it instead **sharpened** the conjecture by showing that abundant caustics exist universally, so Birkhoff's claim must concern the *complete* foliation (no gaps). The gaps are exactly where Aubry–Mather theory and Mather's destruction-of-invariant-curves results live, and closing them is the crux that no attempt has yet overcome in general.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Bialy's global rigidity (1993) — settled the strongest form
text: Misha Bialy's Hopf-type theorem — global foliation by invariant curves forces the disk — is a genuine, accepted resolution of the *total* integrability version. It is a near-miss for the main problem only because its hypothesis (foliation of the entire phase cylinder) is much stronger than Birkhoff–Poritsky local integrability. Attempts to weaken the hypothesis to a one-sided neighborhood of the boundary have not succeeded; the integral inequality used loses control there.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: The Avila–De Simoi–Kaloshin and Kaloshin–Sorrentino theorems (2016–2018) — the major partial results
text: These are the landmark *positive* partial results, not disputed. Avila, De Simoi, and Kaloshin proved local rigidity for rationally integrable perturbations of low-eccentricity ellipses; Kaloshin and Sorrentino proved local Birkhoff rigidity for deformations of generic ellipses. Their methods (deformation theory, Mather's $\beta$-function, the action spectrum, and delicate Fourier analysis) are widely regarded as the state of the art. The honest caveat — stated plainly by the authors — is that all are *perturbative* and *local around conics*; they do not approach the global conjecture.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Polynomial-integral program (Bialy–Mironov) — accepted, but a different hypothesis
text: Bialy and Mironov's resolution of the conjecture for integrals polynomial in momenta is a substantial, accepted body of work. The recurring point of scholarly caution is logical, not a dispute over correctness: a general integrable billiard is not known to admit a polynomial integral, so these theorems do not close the analytic conjecture. Their more recent announcements on the centrally symmetric ("rational") case have circulated as preprints; as with any frontier work, independent verification of the full argument is ongoing and should be cited as such.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Status of claimed full proofs
text: To date there is **no accepted proof of the general Birkhoff conjecture**, and the dossier is aware of **no widely publicized retracted claim of a complete proof** in the refereed literature. The field's culture has been careful: results are stated with explicit hypotheses (global vs. local, polynomial vs. analytic, near-ellipse vs. arbitrary), so the usual pattern of a dramatic claimed proof followed by withdrawal has largely been avoided. The honest summary is that the problem has been *advanced incrementally and reliably* rather than punctuated by disputed announcements.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Confusable problems (avoiding false attributions)
text: Two adjacent results are sometimes mis-cited as "solving Birkhoff." First, **Treschev's** investigations into formally integrable billiards with non-conic boundary (numerical/asymptotic evidence for analytic but non-elliptic local integrability of a degenerate map) concern a *different* setup and do not contradict the convex conjecture. Second, results on **outer (dual) billiards** and on **polygonal** or **non-convex** tables are separate problem classes. Care should be taken not to present any of these as progress on the strictly convex Birkhoff problem itself.

The four pillar works — Birkhoff (1927), Poritsky (1950), Lazutkin (1973), Bialy (1993) — and the modern breakthroughs Avila–De Simoi–Kaloshin (2016/Annals 2018) and Kaloshin–Sorrentino (2018) are canonical and certainly real (`high-confidence`), though their printed DOIs are omitted rather than risk fabrication. Rows 4, 12, 15, 20, 22 represent expository/survey items whose exact titles or venues I could not pin down precisely; they are flagged accordingly and should be confirmed against MathSciNet/zbMATH before citation. title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Top Mathematicians — The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
text: _Ten figures who shaped the problem, past and present._

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Top Mathematicians — The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
text:
_Ten figures who shaped the problem, past and present._

| # | Name | Era | Role | Key contribution |
|---|------|-----|------|------------------|
| 1 | George D. Birkhoff | 1884–1944 | originator | Systematized convex billiards in *Dynamical Systems* (1927); posed the integrability-rigidity conjecture; proved the Poincaré–Birkhoff twist theorem underlying the dynamics. |
| 2 | Hillel Poritsky | 1898–1990 | originator | 1950 analysis of convex billiards; sharpened the problem into its local "Birkhoff–Poritsky" form, the version most modern theorems address. |
| 3 | Vladimir F. Lazutkin | 1944–2000 | breakthrough | Proved (1973) a positive-measure KAM family of caustics exists near the boundary of any smooth convex billiard; introduced Lazutkin coordinates. |
| 4 | Misha Bialy | contemporary | breakthrough | Proved (1993) global Hopf-type rigidity: full foliation by invariant curves forces the disk; co-leads the polynomial-integrability program. |
| 5 | Andrey E. Mironov | contemporary | breakthrough | With Bialy, resolved the algebraic/polynomial Birkhoff conjecture and advanced the centrally symmetric (Birkhoff–Poritsky) case. |
| 6 | Vadim Kaloshin | contemporary | frontier | Co-author of the local rigidity breakthroughs (2016, 2018); central organizer of the deformation/spectral-rigidity program. |
| 7 | Alfonso Sorrentino | contemporary | frontier | With Kaloshin, proved local Birkhoff rigidity for generic ellipses (2018); developed Mather-theory ($\beta$-function) tools for the problem. |
| 8 | Jacopo De Simoi | contemporary | frontier | Co-author of the small-eccentricity rigidity theorem (2016) and of dynamical spectral rigidity for symmetric domains. |
| 9 | Artur Avila | contemporary | breakthrough | Fields Medalist; co-author of the 2016 rational-integrability rigidity result for low-eccentricity ellipses. |
| 10 | Serge Tabachnikov | contemporary | expositor | Authoritative surveys and the book *Geometry and Billiards*; framed and popularized the modern Birkhoff–Poritsky formulation and caustic geometry. |

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Notes
text: The active community is compact and tightly interconnected, centered on a few collaboration clusters: the **Kaloshin–Sorrentino–De Simoi** axis (deformation theory, Mather's $\beta$-function, dynamical and Laplace spectral rigidity), the **Bialy–Mironov** program (algebraic and polynomial integrability, central symmetry), and the expository/structural work of **Tabachnikov** that keeps the precise formulations honest. Younger contributors such as **Comlan Koval** and collaborators of Kaloshin (e.g., Hong-Kun Zhang, Ke Zhang in adjacent billiard dynamics; Hamid Hezari and Steve Zelditch on the inverse-spectral side) continue to push local rigidity toward larger eccentricity and weaker regularity. Activity concentrates at the University of Maryland / ETH–IST lineage around Kaloshin, Tel Aviv University (Bialy), Sobolev Institute / Novosibirsk (Mironov), and Roma Tor Vergata (Sorrentino). The problem features regularly at dynamical-systems conferences and in ICM-level surveys; progress is steady and verifiable rather than sporadic.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Status & Frontier — The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
text: _Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Current status: open (active progress)
text: The general Birkhoff conjecture — that the *only* smooth strictly convex billiard tables whose dynamics are integrable are ellipses — remains **unproven**. The metadata status is *active-progress*: there is no accepted full proof, but the last decade produced decisive *local* and *algebraic* rigidity theorems that constrain where a counterexample could possibly live.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: What is known unconditionally
text:
- **Global (total) integrability ⇒ disk.** If the *entire* phase cylinder is foliated by continuous invariant curves, the table is a disk (Bialy, 1993, Hopf-type rigidity). The disk and ellipse are the only confirmed integrable convex tables.
- **Universal near-boundary caustics.** Every smooth strictly convex billiard has a positive-measure Cantor family of caustics near the boundary (Lazutkin, 1973). Integrability is the statement that this family is *complete* (gap-free).
- **Polynomial integrals ⇒ conics.** Convex billiards admitting an integral polynomial in momenta are conics (Bialy–Mironov program). This settles the *algebraic* Birkhoff conjecture.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: What is known conditionally / locally
text:
- **Small-eccentricity rational integrability.** A rationally integrable small smooth perturbation of an ellipse of small eccentricity is an ellipse — Avila, De Simoi, Kaloshin, *Annals of Mathematics* (2018); arXiv:1412.2853.
- **Local Birkhoff rigidity near generic ellipses.** An integrable (Birkhoff–Poritsky) deformation of a generic ellipse of any eccentricity is an ellipse — Kaloshin, Sorrentino, *Annals of Mathematics* (2018); arXiv:1612.09194.
- **Toward arbitrary eccentricity.** Extensions (e.g., Koval, 2023, arXiv:2306.04143) push rational-integrability rigidity to ellipses of arbitrary eccentricity, and Bialy–Mironov address the centrally symmetric Birkhoff–Poritsky case (preprints, ~2021).

All of these are **local**: they assume proximity to an ellipse (or a polynomial/algebraic integral, or central symmetry).

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: What a full resolution requires
text:
1. **Remove the nearness hypothesis.** Pass from "integrable deformations *of an ellipse*" to "*any* integrable convex table," with no perturbative assumption — the central gap.
2. **Bridge from local to global / from polynomial to analytic.** Either show every integrable billiard admits a polynomial (or algebraic) integral, reducing to Bialy–Mironov, or develop a non-perturbative caustic-foliation rigidity that does not start from a conic.
3. **Close the Aubry–Mather gaps.** Prove that a complete caustic foliation accumulating on the boundary forces global confocal structure, controlling the instability regions where Mather theory destroys invariant curves.
4. **Weaken regularity.** Most theorems require $C^\infty$ or analytic boundaries; the natural conjecture is robust and should hold under finite smoothness.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Plausible routes
text: The deformation/spectral-rigidity program (Kaloshin–Sorrentino–De Simoi) is the most likely to extend the local results outward, possibly merging with inverse-spectral ("hearing the shape") techniques. The Bialy–Mironov algebraic line is the most likely to deliver clean global statements under structural hypotheses (central symmetry, polynomial integrals). A genuine resolution probably requires a new idea linking these two — a non-perturbative mechanism forcing algebraicity of integrable caustics.

title: The Birkhoff Conjecture (Integrable Billiards)
heading: Related problems
text:
- [Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem](../hilbert-sixteenth-problem/README.md) — rigidity and finiteness in planar dynamical/algebraic systems.
- [Weinstein Conjecture](../weinstein-conjecture/README.md) — periodic orbits and Hamiltonian/Reeb dynamics, the variational backdrop of billiards.
- [Mandelbrot Set Local Connectivity](../mandelbrot-locally-connected/README.md) — another rigidity-flavored conjecture at the order/chaos boundary in dynamics.
- [Invariant Subspace Problem](../invariant-subspace-problem/README.md) — a structural "the only objects are the obvious ones" rigidity question in a different setting.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Plain statement
text: On a variety of general type over a number field, the rational points are not Zariski dense; a higher-dimensional analogue of the Mordell conjecture.

*Originators: Enrico Bombieri, Serge Lang.*

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: History — The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
text: _Origin, formulation, and timeline._

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Origin
text:
The Bombieri–Lang conjecture is the central higher-dimensional generalization of the Mordell conjecture, asking how the geometry of an algebraic variety controls the distribution of its rational points. Mordell's 1922 conjecture predicted that a smooth projective curve of genus $g \ge 2$ over a number field $K$ has only finitely many $K$-rational points — and such a curve is "of general type" precisely when $g \ge 2$. The natural question is what replaces *finiteness* in higher dimension, where finiteness manifestly fails: a general-type surface can contain rational or elliptic curves carrying infinitely many points, so $X(K)$ need not be finite.

The resolution, due independently to **Enrico Bombieri** (in a 1980 University of Chicago seminar, never formally published by him) and **Serge Lang** (in print across the late 1970s and 1980s), is to weaken "finite" to "**not Zariski dense**." For a smooth projective variety $X$ of general type (with big canonical bundle $K_X$) over a number field $K$, the conjecture asserts that $X(K)$ lies in a proper Zariski-closed subset $Z \subsetneq X$. Lang's stronger geometric form predicts a closed proper **exceptional (special) locus** absorbing all but finitely many points outside it, conjecturally equal to the union of all positive-dimensional images of non-constant maps from abelian varieties.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Formulations and reformulations
text:
Several intertwined statements travel under the name:

- **Weak (number-field) form:** $X$ of general type $\Rightarrow X(K)$ not Zariski dense.
- **Lang's geometric conjecture:** the special set $\mathrm{Sp}(X)$ is a proper closed subset, and outside it points are sparse; conjecturally $X \setminus \mathrm{Sp}(X)$ is Mordellic and Kobayashi-hyperbolic.
- **Lang–Vojta (integral-point) version** for quasi-projective varieties of log general type, plus function-field and positive-characteristic analogues.

These now sit beneath **Vojta's conjecture** (1987), which would imply Bombieri–Lang via a single height/discriminant inequality.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Timeline
text:
**1922** — Mordell conjectures finiteness of rational points on curves of genus $\ge 2$.

**1974** — Lang articulates a programmatic vision linking hyperbolicity, integral points, and Diophantine finiteness ("Higher dimensional Diophantine problems," _Bull. AMS_).

**1980** — Bombieri poses the Zariski-density formulation for surfaces of general type in a Chicago seminar.

**1983** — Faltings proves the Mordell conjecture (with the Tate and Shafarevich conjectures), settling the curve case and energizing the higher-dimensional program.

**1986** — Lang's survey "Hyperbolic and Diophantine analysis" (_Bull. AMS_) consolidates the conjectural web and the special-set picture.

**1987** — Vojta proposes his conjectures, drawing the analogy with Nevanlinna theory and giving a unifying inequality implying Bombieri–Lang.

**1991** — Faltings proves the Mordell–Lang conjecture for subvarieties of abelian varieties via a Vojta-type method; extended in **1994** ("The general case of S. Lang's conjecture") to semiabelian varieties.

**1991–1996** — Vojta and McQuillan give further proofs/extensions of Mordell–Lang; McQuillan's value-distribution methods enter the program.

**1997** — Caporaso, Harris, and Mazur show that **uniform** Bombieri–Lang would imply a bound on the number of rational points on curves depending only on genus — a striking, counterintuitive corollary.

**2000s** — Conditional results proliferate (Abramovich, Hassett, Pacelli) for moduli of curves and subvarieties of abelian varieties.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Timeline
text:
**2000s** — Conditional results proliferate (Abramovich, Hassett, Pacelli) for moduli of curves and subvarieties of abelian varieties.

**2018–2021** — Lawrence–Venkatesh give a new period-map proof of the Mordell conjecture and partial higher-dimensional non-density statements, reviving optimism about unconditional progress.

**2021–2024** — Activity around Lang–Vojta for surfaces (Corvaja–Zannier, Levin, Autissier, and others) yields integral-point finiteness in special cases.

**Present** — The conjecture remains unproven in every dimension $\ge 2$; no general-type surface is known unconditionally to have non-dense rational points purely on account of being of general type.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
text:
_Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

The conjecture carries two names because two mathematicians arrived at the same higher-dimensional generalization of Mordell from different directions — Bombieri through the analytic and effective study of points on surfaces, Lang through a sweeping structural philosophy linking hyperbolicity and Diophantine geometry.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Enrico Bombieri (b. 1940)
text:
Born in Milan, Bombieri was a prodigy who published on number theory before completing his doctorate at the University of Milan (1963). He held chairs in Pisa before moving to the Institute for Advanced Study in Princeton in 1977, where he remained as a permanent member. In 1974 he received the **Fields Medal**, principally for work on the large sieve, the Bombieri–Vinogradov theorem, and his contributions to the theory of minimal surfaces (including a famous counterexample, with De Giorgi and Giusti, to the Bernstein problem). His range is exceptional, spanning analytic number theory, several complex variables, the geometry of numbers, and Diophantine approximation; he later gave an influential elementary reworking of Vojta's proof of the Mordell conjecture ("The Mordell conjecture revisited," 1990).

Bombieri's route to the conjecture was concrete. Working on surfaces of general type and effective bounds for points, he recognized around 1980 that the right higher-dimensional statement could not be finiteness but rather *Zariski non-density*: the points must collapse onto a proper subvariety. He presented this in a Chicago seminar but, characteristically, never wrote it up formally — so the published record of the conjecture is largely Lang's.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Serge Lang (1927–2005)
text:
Born in Saint-Germain-en-Laye, France, Lang emigrated to the United States as a teenager, earned his Ph.D. under Emil Artin at Princeton (1951), and spent most of his career at Columbia and then Yale. A member of the Bourbaki group and one of the most prolific textbook authors in mathematics, Lang shaped Diophantine geometry as a unified subject — indeed the term "Diophantine geometry" is essentially his. His 1962 book of that title, and his later *Fundamentals of Diophantine Geometry* (1983), set the agenda for generations.

Lang's contribution to the conjecture was philosophical and architectural. From the 1970s he advanced a web of conjectures asserting an equivalence between three notions: a variety being **of general type** (positivity of the canonical bundle), being **Mordellic** (rational points non-dense over every number field), and being **Kobayashi hyperbolic** (admitting no entire curves, i.e. no non-constant holomorphic map from $\mathbb{C}$). The "special set" — the union of images of non-trivial maps from group varieties — was Lang's device for isolating exactly the part of a variety where points and entire curves can accumulate. Outside it, he predicted finiteness and hyperbolicity in lockstep. This vision, transferring intuition between value-distribution theory (Nevanlinna) and arithmetic, became the organizing principle of the entire field and was the soil from which Vojta's conjectures grew.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: The two formulations
text: The **historical root** is Mordell's finiteness for curves and Lang's general-type/hyperbolicity program; Bombieri supplied the sharp surface-level reformulation in terms of Zariski density. The **modern formulation** — "$X$ of general type over a number field has non-Zariski-dense rational points," together with Lang's exceptional-locus refinement — is the synthesis now universally attributed to both men. Vojta later showed the conjecture is a formal consequence of his height inequality, embedding it in an even larger structure.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Legacy
text: Bombieri and Lang reframed Diophantine geometry as the study of how birational positivity governs arithmetic. Faltings's proof of Mordell, and his later proof of the Mordell–Lang conjecture for abelian varieties, realized fragments of the program; the full Bombieri–Lang conjecture remains one of the defining open problems of arithmetic geometry. Lang's relentless advocacy ensured the conjectures were written down, taught, and pursued; Bombieri's analytic instinct gave them their precise modern shape.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Approaches — The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
text: _Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Diophantine approximation and the Vojta inequality
text:
**Core idea.** The most influential framework recasts the conjecture as a statement about heights. Vojta's conjecture predicts, for a smooth projective $X/K$ with canonical divisor $K_X$ and an effective ample $A$, an inequality of the shape $h_{K_X}(P) \le \epsilon\, h_A(P) + d(P) + O(1)$ outside a proper closed subset, where $d(P)$ is a discriminant term. For $X$ of general type this forces the height to be bounded away from the ample height, confining all but finitely many points to the exceptional set — exactly Bombieri–Lang.

**Best result reached.** Vojta (1989, 1991) and, in a celebrated elementary reworking, Bombieri (1990) and Faltings used precisely this circle of ideas to *reprove* Mordell and then to prove the **Mordell–Lang conjecture** for subvarieties of abelian and semiabelian varieties (Faltings 1991, 1994; Vojta 1996, McQuillan 1995). This is the deepest unconditional evidence: the conjecture holds whenever $X$ embeds in (a subvariety of) a semiabelian variety.

**Barrier.** The method controls points using a global section of a carefully chosen line bundle on $X \times X$ (or higher products) and the Faltings–Vojta product theorem. It crucially exploits the *group structure*. For a general variety of general type there is no ambient group, no Néron–Tate height machinery, and no known substitute for the index estimates. Extracting an inequality for arbitrary general-type $X$ is open and is essentially equivalent to the conjecture itself.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Kobayashi hyperbolicity and the function-field / Nevanlinna analogy
text:
**Core idea.** Lang's program asserts that arithmetic non-density should mirror complex hyperbolicity: a general-type $X$ should contain no entire curves outside its special set. By the dictionary between Nevanlinna theory and Diophantine approximation, theorems about holomorphic maps $\mathbb{C} \to X$ guide and predict the arithmetic.

**Best result reached.** Substantial progress exists on the *complex-analytic* side. McQuillan (1998) proved the Green–Griffiths–Lang conjecture for surfaces of general type with positive Segre class $c_1^2 - c_2 > 0$ (and second Segre number condition), establishing degeneracy of entire curves. Demailly, Diverio–Merker–Rousseau, and Brotbek (2017, proving the Kobayashi hyperbolicity of generic high-degree hypersurfaces) pushed hyperbolicity far. The function-field analogue of Bombieri–Lang is known in significant generality (e.g. via Manin, Grauert, and later work).

**Barrier.** Complex hyperbolicity and non-density are theorems about $\mathbb{C}$, not number fields; the analogy is heuristic, not a proof. There is no implication "hyperbolic $\Rightarrow$ Mordellic" available — that implication is itself part of Lang's conjecture. Translating analytic degeneracy into arithmetic finiteness remains the missing bridge.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: $p$-adic period maps (Lawrence–Venkatesh)
text:
**Core idea.** Lawrence and Venkatesh (2020) reproved Mordell by studying the variation of $p$-adic Galois representations (or Hodge structures) in a family over $X$: if the period map has large image, rational points must be sparse because $p$-adic points with the same monodromy cluster on subvarieties. The method is geometric and avoids the equidistribution input of Faltings's original proof.

**Best result reached.** Beyond curves, the technique gives non-density of integral/rational points for several higher-dimensional families — e.g. for the moduli of hypersurfaces and certain ramified covers, and (Lawrence–Sawin) the Shafarevich conjecture for hypersurfaces in abelian varieties. It produces genuinely new unconditional finiteness statements in dimension $> 1$.

**Barrier.** The method needs a family with a sufficiently non-degenerate period map (large monodromy / variation of Hodge structure) sitting over $X$. A general variety of general type carries no such family, so the approach proves special cases rather than the conjecture. Constructing the requisite geometry is itself a hard open problem.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Positivity, the canonical bundle, and the special set
text:
**Core idea.** Attack the *geometric* heart: prove that the special set $\mathrm{Sp}(X)$ is a proper closed subset and understand its structure, so that arithmetic statements need only be proved on the complement. This draws on the minimal model program, Iitaka fibrations, and the structure theory of varieties of general type.

**Best result reached.** Much is known about the special set in examples and for fibered varieties; Abramovich, and Campana's "orbifold" / "special variety" theory, refine the dichotomy between general-type and "special" behaviour. For abelian varieties the special set is fully understood (it is everything when there is positive-dimensional abelian subvariety structure), matching Faltings.

**Barrier.** No general unconditional control of $\mathrm{Sp}(X)$ for arbitrary $X$ exists, and even granting the geometry, no mechanism converts "outside the special set" into a finiteness theorem without Vojta-type input.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Counting and density heuristics (Batyrev–Manin / negative evidence)
text:
**Core idea.** Asymptotic-counting philosophy (Batyrev–Manin) predicts how many points of bounded height a variety carries; for general type the predicted exponent is so small as to be consistent with non-density.

**Status / cautionary results.** This is *evidence*, not proof, and it cuts both ways: there exist general-type surfaces with provably dense rational points after passing to a finite extension if and only if certain subvarieties exist — making clear that the conjecture cannot be strengthened to finiteness and that any approach must respect the role of the exceptional locus. Caporaso–Harris–Mazur (1997) further showed the conjecture has *surprising* uniform consequences, a warning that any proof technique must be powerful enough to yield uniformity.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Attempts — The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
text:
_Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

The Bombieri–Lang conjecture has not attracted the cottage industry of claimed elementary proofs that surrounds problems like Goldbach or Fermat — its technical entry barrier is high, and the experts who work on it are cautious. Progress has come instead through a sequence of landmark *partial* theorems and a few corrections to the literature.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Faltings's theorems — the decisive partial results
text: Faltings's 1983 proof of the **Mordell conjecture** settled the curve case (dimension 1) completely and remains the foundational evidence. His 1991 paper, *Diophantine approximation on abelian varieties*, proved the **Mordell–Lang conjecture** for subvarieties of abelian varieties: if $X \subseteq A$ is a subvariety not containing a translate of a positive-dimensional abelian subvariety, then $X(K)$ is finite. The 1994 sequel, *The general case of S. Lang's conjecture*, extended this to semiabelian varieties. These are unconditional Bombieri–Lang in the special situation where the variety sits inside a group — the single largest body of confirmed cases. The proofs rely on Vojta's product-theorem method and were a genuine surprise in their power.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Vojta and McQuillan — independent confirmations and refinements
text: Vojta (1991, 1996) gave alternative and strengthened proofs of the Mordell–Lang statements, embedding them in his height-inequality framework. McQuillan (1995) reproved the semiabelian case via a different, more dynamical argument. None of these extend to general $X$, but together they show the abelian-variety case is now extremely robust — a near-miss only in the sense that the techniques stop precisely at the boundary of the group structure.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Lawrence–Venkatesh — a new method, new cases
text: The 2020 Lawrence–Venkatesh period-map proof of Mordell, and its higher-dimensional offshoots (including Lawrence–Sawin on the Shafarevich conjecture for abelian-variety hypersurfaces, 2020), are the most recent genuine advances. They establish non-density in higher-dimensional families with large monodromy — true new instances of Bombieri–Lang-type behaviour — but do not reach a general-type variety lacking a suitable period map.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Conditional and structural near-misses
text: Caporaso, Harris, and Mazur (1997, *Uniformity of rational points*) is famous not as a proof but as a conditional bombshell: assuming a uniform version of Bombieri–Lang, the number of rational points on a curve of genus $g$ over a fixed number field is bounded by a constant depending only on $g$ (and the field). The result was so counterintuitive that it prompted intense scrutiny of whether the conjecture *should* be believed; no error was found, and the implication stands. Later work by Pacelli, Abramovich, and others tightened these conditional consequences. This episode is the closest the subject has to a "dispute": the worry was about the *plausibility* of the hypothesis, not about a flawed proof.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: On claimed proofs
text: There is **no widely circulated claimed proof** of the full conjecture that has been retracted or is under active dispute, in contrast to neighbouring areas (e.g. the long controversy over Mochizuki's claimed proof of the closely related **abc conjecture**, which — if correct — would yield deep Diophantine consequences but is not generally accepted by the community). The honest state of affairs is that Bombieri–Lang remains open in every dimension $\ge 2$, with the abelian-variety and large-monodromy families as the only unconditionally settled regimes, and with Vojta's conjecture as the principal route by which a full proof is expected to arrive, if at all.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Notes
text:
- The conjecture's defining sources are partly **unpublished**: Bombieri's 1980 formulation circulated through his Chicago seminar and was disseminated in Lang's writings rather than a paper of Bombieri's own. Rows 2, 3, 5, 6, 11 represent Lang's published transmission of the conjectural framework.
- DOIs/arXiv ids are given **only** where confidently recalled. The Caporaso–Harris–Mazur DOI (row 15) is the *J. Amer. Math. Soc.* article; the Lawrence–Venkatesh DOI (row 24) is the *Inventiones* paper "The shape of the Shafarevich/period map proof of Mordell" — the exact title wording is approximate, hence **needs-verification**. Where I am not confident of the precise title or identifier I have written "—" and flagged the row rather than fabricate a number.
- Several entries (Vojta 1991/1996, McQuillan 1995/1998, Campana, Demailly, Brotbek) are real, canonical works in the field, but their exact titles, venues, and identifiers should be checked against MathSciNet/zbMATH before citation; they are marked **needs-verification** accordingly.

text:
_Ten figures who shaped the problem, past and present._

| # | Name | Era | Role | Key contribution |
|---|------|-----|------|------------------|
| 1 | Serge Lang | 1970s–2000s | originator | Built the general-type / Mordellic / hyperbolic conjectural web and the special-set formalism; published the conjecture and made it canonical. |
| 2 | Enrico Bombieri | 1980s–present | originator | Gave the sharp Zariski-density formulation for surfaces of general type (1980 Chicago seminar); elementary reworking of Vojta's Mordell proof (1990). |
| 3 | Louis Mordell | 1920s | originator | Conjectured finiteness of rational points on curves of genus $\ge 2$ — the dimension-1 root of the whole program. |
| 4 | Gerd Faltings | 1980s–1990s | breakthrough | Proved Mordell (1983) and the Mordell–Lang conjecture for abelian (1991) and semiabelian (1994) varieties — the deepest confirmed cases. |
| 5 | Paul Vojta | 1980s–present | breakthrough | Formulated the Vojta conjectures implying Bombieri–Lang; gave Diophantine-approximation proofs of Mordell–Lang. |
| 6 | Michael McQuillan | 1990s–present | breakthrough | Foliation/value-distribution proof of semiabelian Mordell–Lang and of Green–Griffiths–Lang for surfaces of general type. |
| 7 | Joe Harris | 1990s | frontier | Co-author of the uniformity theorem showing uniform Bombieri–Lang bounds points on curves by genus alone. |
| 8 | Barry Mazur | 1990s | frontier | Co-author of Caporaso–Harris–Mazur; clarified the structural and philosophical stakes of the conjecture. |
| 9 | Frédéric Campana | 2000s–present | frontier | Orbifold / "special variety" theory refining the general-type-vs-special dichotomy underlying the conjecture. |
| 10 | Brian Lawrence & Akshay Venkatesh | 2018–present | frontier | $p$-adic period-map proof of Mordell and new higher-dimensional non-density results (with Sawin). |

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Notes
text: The active community sits at the intersection of arithmetic geometry, complex hyperbolicity, and Diophantine approximation. Around the **Vojta-conjecture** core work continues from Vojta himself, with related height-inequality and integral-point research by **Pietro Corvaja**, **Umberto Zannier**, **Aaron Levin**, and **Pascal Autissier** (Lang–Vojta for surfaces). On the **hyperbolicity** side, **Jean-Pierre Demailly**, **Damian Brotbek**, **Erwan Rousseau**, **Simone Diverio**, and **Junjiro Noguchi** drive the complex-analytic analogue, while **Frédéric Campana**'s orbifold program reframes the geometric dichotomy. The **period-map** lineage (Lawrence, Venkatesh, Sawin, and collaborators such as **Daniel Litt** and **Will Sawin**) is the newest and most dynamic unconditional front. Conditional structural consequences continue to be explored by **Dan Abramovich**, **Brendan Hassett**, and others. No single group "owns" the problem; progress is incremental and spread across these overlapping schools, with Faltings's theorems and Vojta's conjecture as the shared reference points.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Status & Frontier — The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
text:
_Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

**Status: open** in every dimension $\ge 2$. No variety of general type is known unconditionally to have non-Zariski-dense rational points *solely* by virtue of being of general type. The dimension-1 case (Mordell) is a theorem (Faltings, 1983), and a substantial sub-case in all dimensions — subvarieties of semiabelian varieties — is also a theorem (Faltings 1991, 1994; with Vojta and McQuillan giving independent proofs). Beyond these regimes the conjecture is unproven.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: What is known
text:
**Unconditional.**
- **Curves:** Mordell's conjecture holds — finiteness of $X(K)$ for $g \ge 2$ (Faltings 1983; reproved by Vojta 1989, Bombieri 1990, and Lawrence–Venkatesh 2020).
- **Subvarieties of (semi)abelian varieties:** the Mordell–Lang conjecture is proved; rational points are confined to translates of subgroups (Faltings, Vojta, McQuillan). This is the largest fully settled higher-dimensional family.
- **Large-monodromy families:** the Lawrence–Venkatesh period method yields non-density / Shafarevich-type finiteness for certain higher-dimensional varieties carrying a sufficiently non-degenerate variation of Hodge structure (e.g. Lawrence–Sawin, 2020).
- **Complex-analytic analogue:** strong hyperbolicity and degeneracy results (McQuillan's Green–Griffiths–Lang for general-type surfaces with $c_1^2 > c_2$; Brotbek's hyperbolicity of generic high-degree hypersurfaces) confirm the *geometric* side of Lang's web, though these do not transfer to number fields.

**Conditional.**
- Assuming a **uniform** Bombieri–Lang, Caporaso–Harris–Mazur (1997) deduce a genus-only bound on the number of rational points on curves over a fixed number field — and various further uniformity statements follow.
- The full conjecture follows from **Vojta's conjecture**, which is therefore the principal target of choice.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: What a full resolution requires
text: A proof of the general case must produce, for an arbitrary smooth projective $X/K$ of general type, a mechanism forcing $X(K)$ into a proper closed subset *without* an ambient group structure. Concretely it would need either: (i) a Vojta-type height inequality valid on general $X$ (currently out of reach, and essentially equivalent to the conjecture); (ii) a transfer principle converting complex hyperbolicity into arithmetic non-density (the "$\text{hyperbolic} \Rightarrow \text{Mordellic}$" implication, itself conjectural); or (iii) a vast generalization of the period-map method covering varieties with no natural family of motives. Each route currently terminates at a hard, well-identified obstruction.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Plausible routes
text:
1. **Vojta's inequality** for general-type varieties — the consensus "right" approach, but no partial inequality for arbitrary $X$ is known.
2. **Period maps / $p$-adic Hodge theory** (Lawrence–Venkatesh and successors) — the most active unconditional front, steadily enlarging the list of settled families.
3. **Hyperbolicity transfer** via the Green–Griffiths–Lang conjecture and Campana's orbifold theory — strong on the analytic side, awaiting an arithmetic bridge.

The community's working belief is that Bombieri–Lang is true and that it will fall, if at all, as a corollary of Vojta's conjecture or of a dramatic extension of period-theoretic methods — not by an isolated elementary argument.

title: The Bombieri–Lang Conjecture
heading: Related problems
text:
- [Vojta's Conjecture](../vojta-conjecture/README.md) — the master height inequality implying Bombieri–Lang.
- [Mordell Conjecture / abc Conjecture](../abc-conjecture/README.md) — closely linked Diophantine finiteness, with effective ramifications.
- [Tate Conjecture](../tate-conjecture/README.md) — sibling arithmetic-geometry conjecture on algebraic cycles and Galois representations.
- [Hodge Conjecture](../hodge-conjecture/README.md) — period-map and Hodge-theoretic methods overlap with the period approach here.
- [Birch–Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture](../birch-swinnerton-dyer/README.md) — companion conjecture on rational points, in the abelian-variety setting.

title: The Borel Conjecture
heading: Plain statement
text: Closed aspherical manifolds with isomorphic fundamental groups are homeomorphic, and any homotopy equivalence between them is homotopic to a homeomorphism.

*Originators: Armand Borel.*

title: The Borel Conjecture
heading: History — The Borel Conjecture
text:
_Origin, formulation, and timeline._

The Borel Conjecture is a statement of **topological rigidity** for closed aspherical manifolds. A closed manifold $M$ is *aspherical* if its universal cover is contractible, equivalently if $\pi_n(M)=0$ for $n\geq 2$; such an $M$ is a $K(\pi,1)$, and its homotopy type is determined by the single group $\pi=\pi_1(M)$. The conjecture asserts that this homotopy-theoretic determination is in fact a *topological* one:

> If $M$ and $N$ are closed aspherical manifolds and $f:M\to N$ is a homotopy equivalence, then $f$ is homotopic to a homeomorphism. In particular, closed aspherical manifolds with isomorphic fundamental groups are homeomorphic.

The conjecture has no historical "paper of origin." It was communicated by **Armand Borel** in correspondence and conversation around 1953, motivated by the **Mostow rigidity** phenomenon then crystallizing for locally symmetric spaces. Borel's insight was to ask whether the rigidity Mostow established *geometrically* (isometry, requiring dimension $\geq 3$ and stronger hypotheses) might hold *topologically* under the far weaker hypothesis of asphericity alone. The conjecture entered the literature mainly through later attributions; **Wall** and others record Borel's question in print in the 1970s.

title: The Borel Conjecture
heading: History — The Borel Conjecture
text:
A central caution: the **homeomorphism** category is essential. The smooth and PL analogues are **false** — Browder, and later Farrell–Jones, produced closed aspherical manifolds that are homeomorphic but not diffeomorphic (e.g. via exotic tori and negatively curved manifolds with exotic smooth structures). Thus the Borel Conjecture is the topological rigidity statement precisely; it is the higher-dimensional aspherical analogue of the **Poincaré Conjecture** (which concerns the simply connected case, $\pi=1$).

**Timeline**

title: The Borel Conjecture
heading: History — The Borel Conjecture
text:
**Timeline**

- **1953** — Armand Borel formulates the rigidity question, prompted by Mostow's emerging work on rigidity of locally symmetric spaces.
- **1968** — Mostow proves strong (geometric) rigidity for closed hyperbolic manifolds of dimension $\geq 3$, giving the conjecture its archetypal positive instance.
- **1969** — Kirby–Siebenmann torus trick and the structure theory of topological manifolds supply the surgery-theoretic framework in which Borel rigidity is naturally phrased.
- **1970s** — C.T.C. Wall's surgery theory recasts the problem: the conjecture is equivalent to the vanishing/assembly statement that the surgery **structure set** $\mathcal S(M)$ is trivial, controlled by the assembly map in $L$-theory.
- **1977–1983** — Farrell–Hsiang prove rigidity for flat and almost-flat manifolds and many infranilmanifolds; topological rigidity established for tori $T^n$.
- **1981** — Waldhausen's algebraic $K$-theory of spaces and the parametrized $h$-cobordism theorem provide the $K$-theoretic half of the picture.
- **1980s–1990s** — **Farrell–Jones** develop their controlled-topology / geodesic-flow method, proving the conjecture for closed manifolds of nonpositive curvature (Riemannian, dim $\neq 3,4$) and for many word-hyperbolic groups.
- **1993** — Farrell–Jones formulate the **Farrell–Jones Conjecture (FJC)** in $K$- and $L$-theory; its $L$-theoretic part implies the Borel Conjecture for the corresponding groups.
- **2004–2012** — **Bartels–Lück–Reich** prove FJC for word-hyperbolic groups (2008–2012) and, with Wegner, for CAT(0) groups, via controlled topology and flow spaces — yielding Borel rigidity for vast new classes.
- **2014–present** — FJC extended to lattices in virtually connected Lie groups, mapping class groups (partial), $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb Z)$, relatively hyperbolic and many normally/finitely-generated groups (Bartels, Lück, Rüping, Kammeyer, and collaborators). The conjecture is proven for an enormous family of groups yet remains **open in general**, with dimensions $3$ (entangled with geometrization) and $4$ (where surgery is obstructed) especially delicate.

title: The Borel Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Borel Conjecture
text:
_Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

**Armand Borel** (1923–2003) was a Swiss mathematician whose work reshaped the modern theory of Lie groups, algebraic groups, arithmetic groups, and the topology of their associated symmetric and locally symmetric spaces. Born in La Chaux-de-Fonds, he trained at the **ETH Zürich**, where he came under the influence of Heinz Hopf and Eduard Stiefel, absorbing the topological tradition of characteristic classes and fiber bundles. A formative period at Paris in the late 1940s and early 1950s placed him at the center of the Cartan–Leray–Serre revolution in spectral sequences and the topology of homogeneous spaces; his thesis on the cohomology of Lie groups and their classifying spaces, computing the cohomology of $BG$ and the structure of $H^*(G)$, became foundational. He was elected to the **Bourbaki** group and spent the bulk of his career at the **Institute for Advanced Study** in Princeton from 1957.

Borel's signature was the fusion of three normally separate worlds: algebraic geometry (linear algebraic groups, the Borel subgroup, Borel–Weil theory), number theory (arithmetic groups, the Borel–Serre compactification, automorphic forms and continuous cohomology), and topology (cohomology of classifying spaces, equivariant cohomology — the "Borel construction" $EG\times_G X$). It is from this last vantage, and from his deep engagement with locally symmetric spaces $\Gamma\backslash G/K$, that the rigidity question naturally arose.

title: The Borel Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Borel Conjecture
text:
**The idea behind the conjecture.** A locally symmetric space of noncompact type is aspherical: its universal cover is a contractible symmetric space $G/K$. Such a space is a $K(\Gamma,1)$, so its homotopy type is encoded entirely by the arithmetic group $\Gamma=\pi_1$. By the late 1950s it was understood — and would be made precise by **Mostow** in 1968 — that for many of these spaces the *geometry* is rigid: the lattice $\Gamma$ determines the metric up to isometry. Borel asked, around 1953, the bolder topological question: forget the metric and forget that $M$ is locally symmetric — suppose only that $M$ is a closed *aspherical* manifold. Is its **homeomorphism type** already determined by $\pi_1(M)$? This strips Mostow rigidity of all its geometric hypotheses and asks whether asphericity alone forces topological rigidity. The conjecture is thus the topological shadow of geometric rigidity, and the higher-dimensional aspherical counterpart of the Poincaré Conjecture.

Borel never published the conjecture as a formal statement; it propagated through correspondence (notably with Wall and others) and was recorded in the surgery-theory literature of the 1970s. This oral origin is faithful to Borel's style — he posed organizing questions whose resolution would require machinery he did not himself build.

title: The Borel Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Borel Conjecture
text:
**Historical root vs. modern formulation.** Borel's original phrasing was the rigidity assertion above. The *modern* formulation is **homotopy-theoretic and assembly-map-based**: the Borel Conjecture for a group $\Gamma$ is implied by the **Farrell–Jones Conjecture**, the statement that the assembly map
$$ H_*^{\Gamma}(\underline{\underline{E}}\Gamma; \mathbf{L}) \;\longrightarrow\; L_*(\mathbb Z\Gamma) $$
(and its $K$-theoretic analogue) is an isomorphism. This recasts a geometric rigidity statement as the computation of $K$- and $L$-groups of the integral group ring $\mathbb Z\Gamma$ from equivariant homology over a category of virtually cyclic subgroups — exactly the kind of structural translation Borel's own career exemplified.

**Legacy.** Beyond the conjecture, Borel's name is attached to the Borel subgroup, Borel–Moore homology, the Borel construction, Borel–Serre compactification, and Borel regulators. He won the **Brouwer Medal** (1978), the **AMS Steele Prize** (1991), and the **Balzan Prize** (1992). The Borel Conjecture remains one of the deepest open organizing problems of geometric topology, animating surgery theory, controlled topology, and the algebraic $K$- and $L$-theory of group rings to this day.

title: The Borel Conjecture
heading: Approaches — The Borel Conjecture
text: _Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

title: The Borel Conjecture
heading: Surgery theory and the structure set
text:
**Core idea.** The Browder–Novikov–Sullivan–Wall surgery program reduces classification of manifolds homotopy equivalent to a fixed $M^n$ ($n\geq 5$) to the **surgery structure set** $\mathcal S^{\mathrm{TOP}}(M)$, which sits in the surgery exact sequence
$$ \cdots \to L_{n+1}(\mathbb Z\pi) \to \mathcal S^{\mathrm{TOP}}(M) \to [M,G/\mathrm{TOP}] \xrightarrow{\;\sigma\;} L_n(\mathbb Z\pi). $$
The Borel Conjecture for $M$ is *equivalent* to $\mathcal S^{\mathrm{TOP}}(M)=\{*\}$ (rigidity) together with vanishing of the relevant Whitehead group (so $h$-cobordisms are products). For aspherical $M$ the normal invariants $[M,G/\mathrm{TOP}]$ are governed by $H^*(M;\mathbf L)$, and triviality of $\mathcal S$ follows once the **assembly map** is an isomorphism.

**Best result.** A complete reduction: Borel rigidity in dimensions $\geq 5$ ⇔ the $L$-theoretic Farrell–Jones isomorphism plus $\mathrm{Wh}(\pi)=0$, $\widetilde K_0(\mathbb Z\pi)=0$.

**Barrier.** Surgery requires $n\geq 5$ (Whitney trick). Dimensions **3 and 4** lie outside; dimension 4 needs Freedman's topological surgery, which works only for "good" fundamental groups (a constrained class).

title: The Borel Conjecture
heading: Farrell–Jones via controlled topology and flows
text:
**Core idea.** Farrell and Jones attack the assembly map directly using the dynamics of the **geodesic flow** on nonpositively curved manifolds, combined with **controlled $h$-cobordism** and **boundedness control** to split or invert assembly. The flow expands distances, allowing surgery obstructions to be pushed to infinity and absorbed.

**Best result.** The Borel Conjecture holds for closed Riemannian manifolds of **nonpositive sectional curvature** in dimensions $\neq 3,4$ (Farrell–Jones, 1989–1993), and for many word-hyperbolic and crystallographic groups. They also showed the *smooth* Borel statement is **false**, exhibiting homeomorphic-but-not-diffeomorphic negatively curved manifolds.

**Barrier.** The original method needs an actual nonpositively curved metric (geometry), excluding general aspherical manifolds whose groups carry no such structure.

title: The Borel Conjecture
heading: The Farrell–Jones Conjecture (assembly via $E_{\mathcal{VCyc}}\Gamma$)
text:
**Core idea.** Reformulate everything as: the $K$- and $L$-theoretic assembly maps relative to the family $\mathcal{VCyc}$ of virtually cyclic subgroups,
$$ H_n^{\Gamma}(E_{\mathcal{VCyc}}\Gamma;\mathbf K/\mathbf L) \to K_n/L_n(\mathbb Z\Gamma), $$
are isomorphisms. The $L^{\langle-\infty\rangle}$-version **implies the Borel Conjecture** for closed aspherical manifolds with $\pi_1=\Gamma$ in dimension $\geq 5$; FJC is the central modern target because it is **inheritance-closed** (passes to subgroups, extensions, directed colimits, finite products, free/amalgamated products).

**Best result.** Bartels–Lück–Reich (2008–2012) proved FJC for **word-hyperbolic** groups; Bartels–Lück and Wegner for **CAT(0)** groups; later for lattices in virtually connected Lie groups, $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb Z)$, $\mathrm{Out}(F_n)$-related and many relatively hyperbolic / solvable groups. This delivers Borel rigidity for an enormous family.

**Barrier.** No known proof for arbitrary groups; obstructions include groups with **no nonpositive-curvature geometry** (e.g. some amenable or exotic groups) and the difficulty of controlling flow spaces without geometric input. Key open targets: general **mapping class groups**, $\mathrm{Out}(F_n)$, and arbitrary residually finite or sofic groups.

title: The Borel Conjecture
heading: Controlled and bounded topology (Quinn, Chapman–Ferry)
text:
**Core idea.** Replace geometric control by *abstract* metric control: the controlled $h$-cobordism and $\alpha$-approximation theorems (Chapman–Ferry) and Quinn's homology theory of $\mathbf{L}$-spectra let one prove local triviality of structure sets when obstructions can be shrunk below any $\varepsilon$.

**Best result.** The engine inside Farrell–Jones; yields rigidity whenever sufficient contraction toward a controlled space exists. Quinn's obstruction theory pins the assembly to a generalized homology theory.

**Barrier.** Producing the requisite contractions for groups without geometric models remains the central unsolved technical problem.

title: The Borel Conjecture
heading: Topological rigidity in dimension 4 (Freedman–Quinn)
text:
**Core idea.** Use Freedman's topological surgery and the disk-embedding theorem for **good groups** (groups of subexponential growth / amenable, where Casson handles convert to embedded disks).

**Best result.** Borel-type rigidity for aspherical 4-manifolds with **good** $\pi_1$ — e.g. for poly-(finite or cyclic) and many solvable groups.

**Barrier.** The **disk-embedding conjecture** in dimension 4 is open for general (e.g. free, hyperbolic) groups; without it, 4-dimensional surgery — and hence Borel rigidity in dimension 4 — stalls. This is the same wall as the smooth 4D Poincaré problem.

title: The Borel Conjecture
heading: Geometrization in dimension 3
text:
**Core idea.** In dimension 3 a closed aspherical manifold is irreducible with infinite $\pi_1$; Perelman's proof of **geometrization** (with the $K(\pi,1)$ structure) yields that such manifolds are determined by $\pi_1$.

**Best result.** Borel rigidity holds in dimension 3 as a **consequence of geometrization** (Perelman, 2003), modulo standard $3$-manifold topology (Waldhausen rigidity for Haken manifolds was the classical antecedent).

**Barrier.** This is special to dimension 3 and gives no leverage on the general conjecture; it is logically independent of the surgery/Farrell–Jones machinery.

title: The Borel Conjecture
heading: Attempts — The Borel Conjecture
text:
_Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

**Mostow rigidity as proof of concept (1968).** Mostow's theorem — that closed hyperbolic $n$-manifolds ($n\geq 3$) with isomorphic fundamental groups are isometric — is the geometric archetype. It is *stronger* than Borel (isometry, not just homeomorphism) but *narrower* (requires a hyperbolic metric). It convinced the community the topological statement was plausible and set the agenda.

**Waldhausen's 3-manifold rigidity (1968).** Waldhausen proved homotopy equivalences of Haken (sufficiently large, aspherical) 3-manifolds are homotopic to homeomorphisms — a complete Borel-type result in dimension 3 for a large class, decades before geometrization closed the remaining cases.

**Farrell–Hsiang on tori and flat manifolds (1977–1983).** Using the surgery exact sequence and a clever use of expanding endomorphisms ("Frobenius" / transfer arguments), Farrell and Hsiang proved topological rigidity of the torus $T^n$ and of flat and infranil manifolds. This was the first systematic confirmation beyond the geometric cases and established the structure-set-vanishing strategy.

title: The Borel Conjecture
heading: Attempts — The Borel Conjecture
text:
**Farrell–Jones nonpositive curvature (1989–1993).** The landmark partial result: rigidity for closed nonpositively curved manifolds in dimensions $\neq 3,4$. Their geodesic-flow / controlled-topology method became the template for all subsequent progress. Crucially, they also showed the **smooth Borel statement is false**, finding negatively curved manifolds that are homeomorphic but carry exotic (non-diffeomorphic) smooth structures — a permanent caution that the conjecture lives in $\mathrm{TOP}$, not $\mathrm{DIFF}$ or $\mathrm{PL}$.

**Bartels–Lück–Reich, hyperbolic and CAT(0) groups (2008–2012).** The current state of the art: a proof of the Farrell–Jones Conjecture (hence Borel in dim $\geq 5$) for word-hyperbolic groups, and with Wegner for CAT(0) groups, via flow spaces and transfer reducibility. This dramatically enlarged the class of groups for which Borel is a theorem, without requiring a manifold-level nonpositively curved metric.

**Dimension-4 near-misses (Freedman–Quinn).** Topological surgery in dimension 4 (Freedman 1982; Freedman–Quinn 1990) yields Borel-type rigidity for aspherical 4-manifolds with **good** fundamental groups (amenable / subexponential growth). For general $\pi_1$ the **disk-embedding** problem blocks the argument, leaving 4-dimensional Borel rigidity genuinely open — a near-miss conditioned on a separate hard conjecture.

title: The Borel Conjecture
heading: Attempts — The Borel Conjecture
text:
**Disputes and corrections.** The Borel Conjecture has not attracted famous *retracted* claimed proofs of the full statement — its difficulty has deterred premature global claims. The disputes that arise are narrower and technical:

- **Smooth vs. topological category.** Early informal statements of "rigidity" sometimes blurred categories; the Farrell–Jones counterexamples settled that only the **homeomorphism** statement can be true. Any claim of *smooth* Borel rigidity is false and is the standard objection raised against category-confused formulations.
- **Hypotheses on the assembly map.** Several published proofs of Farrell–Jones-type statements have required careful corrections to the decoration ($L^{\langle-\infty\rangle}$ vs. $L^h$, $L^s$) and to the family ($\mathcal{FIN}$ vs. $\mathcal{VCyc}$); the appearance of **Nil** and **UNil** terms (Cappell) showed naïve assembly statements over finite subgroups are *incorrect*, and that virtually cyclic subgroups are essential. This is a correction-of-record rather than a dispute, but it is the most common technical pitfall.
- **Mapping class groups / $\mathrm{Out}(F_n)$.** Announced extensions of FJC to these groups have at times outpaced complete published verification; the community treats such cases as "in progress" pending full refereeing rather than settled.

title: The Borel Conjecture
heading: Attempts — The Borel Conjecture
text: The honest summary: every confirmed advance is a theorem about a *restricted class of groups or dimensions*. No attempt to date resolves the conjecture for **all** closed aspherical manifolds, and the general statement remains open.
