| # | Name | Era | Role | Key contribution |
|---|------|-----|------|------------------|
| 1 | Christian Goldbach | 18th c. (1690–1764) | originator | Posed the original "every integer $>2$ is a sum of three primes" in his 1742 letter to Euler. |
| 2 | Leonhard Euler | 18th c. (1707–1783) | originator | Reformulated it as the strong binary conjecture: every even $n>2$ is $p+q$; judged it "certainly true." |
| 3 | Viggo Brun | early 20th c. (1885–1978) | breakthrough | Invented the Brun sieve (1920); first finite bound ("$9+9$"), founding the sieve attack. |
| 4 | G. H. Hardy | early 20th c. (1877–1947) | breakthrough | With Littlewood, applied the circle method and conjectured the quantitative asymptotic for representations. |
| 5 | J. E. Littlewood | early 20th c. (1885–1977) | breakthrough | Co-developer of the Hardy–Littlewood circle method and singular-series heuristics. |
| 6 | Lev Schnirelmann | early 20th c. (1905–1938) | breakthrough | Density method: every integer is a sum of a bounded number of primes (additive basis of primes). |
| 7 | I. M. Vinogradov | 20th c. (1891–1983) | breakthrough | Solved the ternary problem for large odd $n$ unconditionally (1937) via minor-arc estimates. |
| 8 | Chen Jingrun | 20th c. (1933–1996) | breakthrough | Proved "$1+2$" (1966/1973): every large even number is $p+P_2$ — the closest approach to the binary conjecture. |
| 9 | H. L. Montgomery & R. C. Vaughan | 20th–21st c. | breakthrough | Power-saving bound on the Goldbach exceptional set: $E(X)=O(X^{1-\delta})$ (1975). |
| 10 | Harald Helfgott | 21st c. (b. 1977) | frontier | Proved the weak (ternary) conjecture in full (2013) for all odd $n>5$, with David Platt's verification. |

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Notes
text: The current active community spans analytic number theory and computation. Helfgott (Göttingen/CNRS) and David Platt (Bristol) drove the ternary resolution and continue to refine explicit prime-counting and verified-computation tools that feed Goldbach-type problems. Olivier Ramaré (CNRS) remains central to explicit Schnirelmann-constant and sieve work, and Tomás Oliveira e Silva (Aveiro) leads the large-scale empirical verification ($4\times10^{18}$). On the binary frontier, the relevant expertise overlaps heavily with the school working on bounded gaps between primes and on improving exceptional-set savings (figures such as János Pintz, and the circle-of-ideas around Yitang Zhang, James Maynard, and Terence Tao on additive prime problems). The honest consensus is that no one currently has a credible route to the *binary* conjecture; effort concentrates on sharpening the partial results (exceptional set, explicit constants) rather than on a frontal proof.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Status & Frontier — Goldbach's Conjecture
text: _Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Current status: OPEN (strong/binary form)
text: The **strong (binary) Goldbach conjecture** — every even integer $n>2$ is a sum of two primes — remains **unproven**. Its sibling, the **weak (ternary) conjecture**, is settled: every odd $n>5$ is a sum of three primes (Helfgott, 2013, building on Vinogradov 1937). The binary form is strictly harder and does **not** follow from the ternary result.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: What is known (unconditional)
text:
- **Almost all even numbers** satisfy Goldbach: the exceptional set $E(X)=\#\{n\le X \text{ even}: n\neq p+q\}$ satisfies $E(X)=O(X^{1-\delta})$ with an explicit power saving (Montgomery–Vaughan 1975; later $\delta\approx0.7$ via Pintz and others).
- **Chen's theorem (1973):** every sufficiently large even $n$ is $p+P_2$, where $P_2$ has at most two prime factors. This is the best structural approximation to $p+p$.
- **Bounded number of primes:** every even integer is a sum of at most **4 primes** (a corollary of Helfgott's ternary theorem; Ramaré's earlier bound was $\le 6$).
- **Computational verification:** the conjecture holds for all even $n\le 4\times10^{18}$ (Oliveira e Silva–Herzog–Pardi).
- **Heuristic support:** the Hardy–Littlewood asymptotic predicts the representation count $r(n)\sim 2\Pi_2\big(\prod_{p\mid n,\,p>2}\tfrac{p-1}{p-2}\big)\tfrac{n}{(\log n)^2}$, so representations are abundant and grow — the conjecture is expected to hold with overwhelming margin.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: What is known (conditional)
text: Under the **Generalized Riemann Hypothesis**, major-arc estimates sharpen and exceptional-set bounds improve, and the ternary theorem becomes elementary for all $n$ — but GRH **does not** yield the binary conjecture. No standard hypothesis (GRH, GLH, even pair-correlation assumptions) is currently known to imply $p+p$ for every even $n$.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: What a full resolution would require
text: The crux is the **binary minor-arc problem**. In the circle method, $r_2(n)=\int_0^1 S(\alpha)^2 e(-n\alpha)\,d\alpha$ is dominated by the minor arcs, where the available square-mean cancellation of $S(\alpha)=\sum_{p\le n}e(p\alpha)$ is too weak to guarantee a positive main term for *every* $n$ (it suffices only on average). A proof would need either (i) a genuinely new way to control binary exponential sums over primes pointwise in $n$, or (ii) a sieve that defeats **Selberg's parity barrier** — classical sieves provably cannot separate "two primes" from "two almost-primes," which is exactly why Chen stalls at $1+2$. Closing $E(X)$ from a power-saving set to the empty set is, in effect, equivalent to the conjecture itself.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Plausible routes
text: None is currently credible for the binary form. The realistic near-term targets are: pushing the exceptional-set exponent $\delta\to1$; lowering explicit constants; transferring ideas from the bounded-gaps revolution (Zhang/Maynard/Tao) and from additive-combinatorics transference principles to additive prime problems. A resolution likely awaits a structural insight beyond the circle method and sieve theory rather than incremental sharpening.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Related problems
text:
- [Twin Prime Conjecture](../twin-prime-conjecture/README.md) — the multiplicative-additive dual; shares the parity barrier and Hardy–Littlewood heuristics.
- [Riemann Hypothesis](../riemann-hypothesis/README.md) — its generalization controls the major arcs and exceptional-set bounds.
- [Polignac Conjecture](../polignac-conjecture/README.md) — every even number as a prime gap; same circle of sieve methods.
- [Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture](../hardy-littlewood-k-tuple/README.md) — the general quantitative framework predicting Goldbach's singular series.
- [Legendre Conjecture](../legendre-conjecture/README.md) — a sibling additive-distributional question about primes in short intervals.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
text: > **Atlas Rank #54** · CSS **52.267** · Field: *Combinatorics* · Posed: **1964** · Status: `active-progress`

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Plain statement
text: Every tree admits a graceful labeling: vertices labeled with distinct integers in [0,n] so edge labels (absolute differences) are all distinct.

*Originators: Gerhard Ringel, Anton Kotzig, Alexander Rosa.*

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: History — The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
text:
_Origin, formulation, and timeline._

The Graceful Tree Conjecture grew out of a problem in combinatorial design theory rather than from any abstract interest in labeling per se. In 1963, **Gerhard Ringel** posed what is now called the **Ringel Conjecture**: the complete graph $K_{2n+1}$ can be decomposed into $2n+1$ edge-disjoint copies of any fixed tree $T$ on $n+1$ vertices. Decomposing $K_{2n+1}$ into copies of a path or star was classical; Ringel's question generalized this to arbitrary trees. The search for a *systematic* way to produce such decompositions led directly to the labeling that now bears the name "graceful."

The decisive step was taken by **Alexander Rosa** in his 1967 paper *On certain valuations of the vertices of a graph* (in the proceedings of the Rome symposium *Théorie des graphes*). Rosa introduced a hierarchy of vertex valuations — which he named $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\sigma$, and $\rho$ — for a graph with $m$ edges. A **$\beta$-valuation** assigns to the vertices distinct integers in $\{0,1,\dots,m\}$ so that the $m$ induced edge labels $|f(u)-f(v)|$ are exactly $\{1,2,\dots,m\}$. Rosa proved that if a tree $T$ with $n$ edges has a $\beta$-valuation, then $K_{2n+1}$ has a cyclic decomposition into $2n+1$ copies of $T$ — connecting his labeling to Ringel's conjecture. **Solomon W. Golomb**, around 1972, renamed the $\beta$-valuation "**graceful labeling**," and that terminology stuck after appearing in popular accounts including Martin Gardner's *Scientific American* column.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: History — The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
text:
The precise modern statement: a tree on $n$ vertices (hence $n-1$ edges) is **graceful** if its vertices can be labeled with distinct integers from $\{0,1,\dots,n-1\}$ such that the edge labels — absolute differences of endpoint labels — are exactly $\{1,2,\dots,n-1\}$. The **Graceful Tree Conjecture (GTC)**, attributed to Ringel, Kotzig, and Rosa and often called the **Ringel–Kotzig conjecture**, asserts that *every* tree is graceful. **Anton Kotzig** was an energetic promoter of the problem and conjectured the strong form; the conjecture's joint attribution reflects Ringel's decomposition motivation, Kotzig's advocacy, and Rosa's formalization.

A finer invariant emerged immediately: Rosa's **$\alpha$-labeling** is a graceful labeling for which there exists a threshold $\lambda$ separating the labels of the two color classes of the (bipartite) tree. Trees with $\alpha$-labelings are far more powerful for decomposition arguments, and much of the field bifurcated into "graceful" versus the stronger "$\alpha$" question.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1963** — Gerhard Ringel poses the Ringel Conjecture on tree-decompositions of complete graphs, the motivating problem.
- **1964** — The strong "every tree is graceful" form is articulated in the Ringel–Kotzig circle; commonly cited as the conjecture's origin year.
- **1966–67** — Alexander Rosa introduces $\alpha,\beta,\sigma,\rho$ valuations and proves the link between $\beta$-valuations and cyclic $K_{2n+1}$ decompositions.
- **c. 1972** — Solomon Golomb coins the term "graceful labeling"; Martin Gardner popularizes it.
- **1979** — Kotzig surveys decompositions and labelings, consolidating "graceful" as a research program.
- **1980s** — Bermond and others verify gracefulness for caterpillars, certain lobsters, symmetrical trees; the difficulty of general trees becomes apparent.
- **1998 (and continuing)** — Joseph Gallian launches *A Dynamic Survey of Graph Labeling* in the *Electronic Journal of Combinatorics*, updated yearly, the field's reference catalog.
- **1999** — Aldred and McKay computationally verify that all trees up to 27 vertices are graceful (later extended).
- **2002** — Brankovic, Murch, Pond, Rosa et al. extend computational verification (trees to ~29–35 vertices in subsequent work).
- **2004** — Van Bussel and others give bounded-diameter and near-graceful results; "range-relaxed" graceful labelings introduced.
- **2009–2010** — Superior and Hrnčiar–Haviar establish gracefulness for trees of diameter at most 5; bounded-degree subclasses advance.
- **2020** — Montgomery, Pokrovskiy, and Sudakov prove the **Ringel Conjecture** for all large $n$, and (with Keevash–Staden–Su independent work on related decompositions) resolve a long-standing motivating problem — though *not* the GTC itself.
- **2021–2025** — Bipartite/$\alpha$-labeling techniques, probabilistic absorption, and large-tree asymptotics continue; the GTC remains open for general trees.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Originator(s) — The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
text:
_Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

The conjecture carries three names, reflecting a layered origin: **Gerhard Ringel** supplied the motivating decomposition problem, **Anton Kotzig** championed and sharpened the strong form, and **Alexander Rosa** gave the precise labeling-theoretic formulation.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Gerhard Ringel (1919–2008)
text: Ringel was a German-born topological and combinatorial graph theorist, among the most influential of the twentieth century. Born in Kollnbrunn, Austria, he was a prisoner of war for five years before completing his doctorate at the University of Bonn in 1951. He held positions in Berlin and at the Free University, and from 1970 at the University of California, Santa Cruz, where he spent the rest of his career. Ringel's monumental achievement was the proof — with **J. W. T. Youngs** in 1968 — of the **Heawood map-coloring conjecture**, establishing the genus of every complete graph on a surface. His work on surface embeddings, Eulerian and Hamiltonian structure, and graph decompositions gave him a deep instinct for *building large structures out of repeated small ones*. The Ringel Conjecture on decomposing $K_{2n+1}$ into copies of a tree is exactly of this flavor: it asks for a perfect, balanced tiling of the complete graph's edge set by a single tree pattern. Graceful labeling entered as the algebraic device that, when present, mechanically produces the cyclic decomposition. Ringel's legacy includes the books *Map Color Theorem* (1974) and *Pearls in Graph Theory* (with Nora Hartsfield), and the field of topological graph theory he helped found.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Anton Kotzig (1919–1991)
text: Kotzig was a Slovak–Canadian mathematician whose breadth spanned graph theory, combinatorics, and probability. Born in Kočovce (then Czechoslovakia), he worked in Bratislava — where he supervised and collaborated with a generation of Slovak combinatorialists, Rosa among them — before emigrating to Canada in 1969, settling at the Université de Montréal (CRM). Kotzig is remembered for the theory of *graph decompositions*, for "Kotzig's theorem" on the structure of 3-connected planar graphs (every such graph has an edge whose endpoint degrees sum to at most 13), and for sustained work on magic and graceful labelings. He was the conjecture's most vocal advocate: he repeatedly emphasized the strong claim that *all* trees are graceful and framed it as one of the central open problems of labeling theory, reportedly calling the difficulty of the general case "a disease." His enthusiasm and lecturing turned a technical decomposition tool into a named conjecture pursued for its own sake.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Alexander Rosa (b. 1937)
text: Rosa, a Slovak–Canadian combinatorialist and student of Kotzig's circle, is the figure who made the conjecture precise. In his 1967 paper *On certain valuations of the vertices of a graph*, he defined the $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\sigma$, $\rho$ valuations and proved the foundational equivalence between $\beta$-valuations (graceful labelings) of a tree $T$ with $m$ edges and cyclic decompositions of $K_{2m+1}$ into copies of $T$. The still-stronger $\alpha$-labeling he introduced in the same work remains the most useful refinement, because $\alpha$-labeled graphs decompose complete *bipartite* graphs and lift to a wide range of further decompositions. Rosa spent his career at McMaster University and became a leading authority on combinatorial design theory, Steiner systems, and graph labelings. His valuations paper is among the most cited starting points in the entire labeling literature.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Two formulations, one problem
text: The *historical root* is Ringel's tree-decomposition conjecture; the *modern formulation* is Rosa's purely arithmetic statement that every tree admits distinct vertex labels in $[0,n-1]$ inducing the full edge-difference set $\{1,\dots,n-1\}$. The two are tightly linked — every graceful tree yields a cyclic decomposition — but they are not identical: the 2020 resolution of the Ringel Conjecture for large $n$ did not settle the GTC, because near-decompositions can be achieved without each individual tree being graceful.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Approaches — The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
text:
_Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

No general method is known. Progress has come by carving out structured subclasses of trees, by strengthening the labeling to the more powerful $\alpha$-form, by transfer/composition theorems that build large graceful trees from small ones, and by probabilistic and computational attacks. Each line has a hard ceiling.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Subclass induction (caterpillars, lobsters, diameter bounds)
text: The oldest and most productive line proves gracefulness for restricted families. **Caterpillars** — trees in which deleting all leaves yields a path — are graceful; this is a classical result (Rosa, and elaborated by others). Beyond caterpillars one reaches **lobsters** (deleting leaves yields a caterpillar), for which **Bermond** conjectured gracefulness; this remains open in general, though large subfamilies (e.g., lobsters with specific spine/leg structure, Morgan's diameter-restricted lobsters) are settled. Trees of small diameter form a parallel ladder: diameter $\le 4$ was handled early, and **Hrnčiar and Haviar (2001)** proved *all trees of diameter at most 5 are graceful* — currently a notable frontier of the diameter ladder. The barrier here is combinatorial explosion: each diameter or "level" increment multiplies the case analysis, and no inductive scheme is known that crosses all diameters uniformly. The methods are constructive and do not generalize to unbounded diameter.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: $\alpha$-labelings and the bipartite refinement
text: Rosa's $\alpha$-labeling (a graceful labeling with a separating threshold between the bipartition classes) is strictly stronger and far more composable: $\alpha$-labeled trees can be concatenated, subdivided, and combined to yield more $\alpha$-labeled (hence graceful) trees. **Symmetrical trees**, complete binary trees, certain spiders, and many caterpillars admit $\alpha$-labelings. The strength is also the weakness: not every tree can have an $\alpha$-labeling. A tree with an $\alpha$-labeling must be bipartite with a balanced-enough structure, and there are graceful trees (and conjecturally all trees are graceful) that provably lack $\alpha$-labelings. So $\alpha$-techniques can never resolve the GTC alone; they form a powerful engine for subclasses but hit a genuine obstruction for general (even some quite simple) trees.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Transfer, composition, and "graceful-preserving" operations
text: A second constructive school proves theorems of the form "if $T_1$ and $T_2$ are graceful (or $\alpha$), then a controlled combination is graceful." Kotzig's and Rosa's identification/amalgamation lemmas, attachment of paths and stars, and "blow-up" constructions generate infinite graceful families and underpin the $\alpha$-composition results. **Stanton–Zarnke** and **Koh–Rogers–Tan** product/composition constructions build large graceful trees from smaller ones. The barrier: these operations are *closure* results, not *universality* results. They show the graceful family is rich and closed under many moves, but the orbit of any finite seed set under known operations does not provably exhaust all trees, since arbitrary trees need not decompose into the building blocks the operations require.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Probabilistic and asymptotic / large-tree methods
text: Modern combinatorics has imported absorption, randomized labeling, and entropy/counting arguments. The landmark adjacent result is **Montgomery, Pokrovskiy, and Sudakov (2020/2021)**, who proved the **Ringel Conjecture for all sufficiently large $n$** (a near-perfect decomposition of $K_{2n+1}$ into copies of any given tree) using probabilistic absorption; related work by **Keevash, Staden, Su** and the **Glock–Joos–Kühn–Osthus** decomposition program handles tree- and graph-decomposition thresholds. These results show the *decomposition* dream that motivated graceful labeling is essentially true asymptotically. The barrier for the GTC proper is sharp: these proofs achieve decompositions *without* exhibiting a graceful labeling of each tree — they use approximate/absorbing structure that tolerates a small defect, whereas gracefulness is an exact, defect-free arithmetic condition on every single tree, including small ones. Bandwidth/large-girth absorption does not produce the rigid edge-difference bijection graceful labeling demands.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Algebraic and polynomial-method attempts
text: There have been attempts to encode gracefulness as the non-vanishing of a polynomial (Combinatorial Nullstellensatz style) or as a system over $\mathbb{Z}_{2n+1}$, hoping a degree/coefficient argument forces a solution. **Range-relaxed** graceful labelings (allowing labels in a larger interval $[0, k]$ with $k > n-1$) are always achievable and give "near-graceful" labelings with small excess, but tightening the range back to exactly $[0,n-1]$ is precisely where every such argument stalls. No Nullstellensatz certificate or algebraic invariant is known that distinguishes graceful from non-graceful for trees, and the relevant permanents/coefficients are not understood. This remains a suggestive but unproductive line.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Computational verification
text: Exhaustive and randomized search has confirmed the conjecture for all trees up to a substantial vertex count (all trees on $\le 27$ vertices by Aldred–McKay 1998–99, extended to roughly $\le 29$–$35$ by Fang and by later GPU/heuristic searches). This builds confidence and guides constructions but is, by nature, incapable of proving a statement over all trees. Its value is as an oracle for counterexample-hunting (none found) and for testing conjectured labeling schemes.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Attempts — The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
text:
_Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

The GTC is unusual among famous open problems in that it has attracted many *partial* successes and a steady trickle of *flawed claimed proofs*, but no widely accepted general resolution. The pattern is instructive: constructions that work beautifully on structured trees break exactly where trees become "generic."

**Caterpillars and the diameter ladder (genuine partial results).** Rosa's early result that all caterpillars are graceful is the prototype near-universal success. The ladder then climbs by diameter: diameter $\le 3$ and $\le 4$ trees are graceful, and **Hrnčiar and Haviar (2001)** proved all trees of **diameter $\le 5$** are graceful via an explicit, intricate transfer of labels — a celebrated near-miss precisely because diameter $6$ already resists the same scheme. Each rung required a fresh, longer argument, dramatizing why a uniform proof is hard.

**Bermond's lobster conjecture (open sub-conjecture).** Bermond (1979) conjectured that all lobsters are graceful. Many subfamilies are settled (e.g., lobsters with specified leg/spine constraints, by Morgan, Ng, and others), and no counterexample exists, but the full lobster case remains open — a strict subproblem whose difficulty already approximates the general one.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Attempts — The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
text:
**$\alpha$-labeling limits (a structural near-miss).** The drive to prove every tree has an $\alpha$-labeling — which would imply gracefulness with strong extra properties — failed for a concrete reason: there exist trees that are graceful but provably admit **no** $\alpha$-labeling. This was a productive negative result, redirecting effort away from the false hope that the stronger property is universal.

**Stanton–Zarnke and product constructions (real, with limits).** Stanton and Zarnke, and later Koh–Rogers–Tan, built graceful trees by product/composition operations. These are correct and generate infinite families, but the constructions presuppose factor structure that arbitrary trees lack, so they extend the catalog without closing the conjecture.

**The Ringel Conjecture resolution (a near-miss for GTC specifically).** **Montgomery, Pokrovskiy, and Sudakov (2020)** proved that $K_{2n+1}$ decomposes into copies of any given tree for all large $n$ — the original motivation for graceful labeling. It is frequently *misreported* as resolving the GTC. It does not: the decomposition is produced by probabilistic absorption that tolerates a controlled defect and does not yield a graceful labeling of the individual tree. The two statements remain logically distinct, and the GTC stays open.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Attempts — The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
text:
**Claimed general proofs (disputed / not accepted).** Over the decades several manuscripts — mostly on the arXiv or in unrefereed venues — have announced a proof that *all* trees are graceful. None has been accepted by the community. A recurring, neutrally stated objection applies to many of them: the argument establishes a **range-relaxed** or near-graceful labeling (distinct vertex labels in some interval $[0,k]$ with $k>n-1$, or distinct edge differences without surjectivity onto $\{1,\dots,n-1\}$) and then asserts, without a valid argument, that the labels can be compressed back into exactly $\{0,\dots,n-1\}$. The compression step is precisely where the difficulty lives, and it is where these proofs fail. Other claimed proofs rely on an unjustified induction on tree size that does not preserve the exact edge-difference bijection when a vertex or branch is reattached. As of mid-2026 there is **no peer-reviewed, community-accepted proof** of the GTC, and authoritative surveys (Gallian's *Dynamic Survey of Graph Labeling*) list it as open. Readers should treat any "solved" announcement with caution until it appears in a refereed journal and is independently checked.

**Computational non-counterexamples.** Aldred–McKay (1998–99) verified all trees up to 27 vertices; later searches pushed the bound higher (Fang and others) with no counterexample. This is a strong empirical near-certainty but, of course, not a proof.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Top Mathematicians — The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
text: _Ten figures who shaped the problem, past and present._

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Top Mathematicians — The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
text:
_Ten figures who shaped the problem, past and present._

| # | Name | Era | Role | Key contribution |
|---|------|-----|------|------------------|
| 1 | Gerhard Ringel | 1960s–2000s | originator | Posed the tree-decomposition conjecture for $K_{2n+1}$ that motivated graceful labeling; founder of topological graph theory. |
| 2 | Anton Kotzig | 1960s–1990s | originator | Advocated and sharpened the strong "every tree is graceful" form; foundational decomposition and labeling theory. |
| 3 | Alexander Rosa | 1960s–present | originator | Defined $\alpha,\beta,\sigma,\rho$ valuations (1967); proved the $\beta$-valuation/cyclic-decomposition equivalence; introduced $\alpha$-labelings. |
| 4 | Solomon W. Golomb | 1970s | expositor | Coined "graceful labeling"; reframed the problem for a broad mathematical audience. |
| 5 | Martin Gardner | 1970s | expositor | Popularized graceful labelings via *Scientific American*, drawing wide attention to the conjecture. |
| 6 | Jean-Claude Bermond | 1970s–1980s | frontier | Formulated the lobster sub-conjecture; advanced graceful-labeling decomposition theory. |
| 7 | Joseph A. Gallian | 1990s–present | expositor | Author/maintainer of the *Dynamic Survey of Graph Labeling*, the field's authoritative living bibliography. |
| 8 | Brendan D. McKay | 1990s–present | frontier | With Aldred, computationally verified gracefulness of all trees up to 27 vertices; tooling (nauty) for the search. |
| 9 | Pavel Hrnčiar & Alfonz Haviar | 2000s | breakthrough | Proved all trees of diameter at most 5 are graceful — a high rung of the diameter ladder. |
| 10 | Richard Montgomery, Alexey Pokrovskiy, Benny Sudakov | 2020s | breakthrough | Proved Ringel's conjecture for large $n$ via probabilistic absorption — resolving the motivating decomposition problem (adjacent to GTC). |

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Notes
text: The active community is concentrated in combinatorics groups working on **graph labeling** and **graph/design decompositions**. The labeling-theory wing — descendants of the Rosa–Kotzig school, plus large groups in India, Indonesia, and Iran — continues to extend graceful and $\alpha$-labeling results to new tree families (lobsters, banana/spider trees, bounded-diameter trees) and maintains the empirical record via Gallian's continuously updated survey. A second, more recent wing comes from **extremal and probabilistic combinatorics** (Montgomery, Pokrovskiy, Sudakov; the Glock–Joos–Kühn–Osthus decomposition program; Keevash and collaborators), whose absorption and iterative-absorption methods resolved the asymptotic decomposition questions but have not yet been adapted to produce the *exact* edge-difference bijection the GTC demands. The interesting frontier is whether these two cultures can be merged — using probabilistic structure to force an exact graceful labeling on all trees, or at least on all sufficiently large trees — which most experts regard as the most plausible route to an eventual proof.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Status & Frontier — The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
text:
_Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

**Status: open — active progress.** The Graceful Tree Conjecture — every tree on $n$ vertices admits distinct vertex labels in $\{0,\dots,n-1\}$ whose induced edge differences are exactly $\{1,\dots,n-1\}$ — remains unproven and undisproven. No counterexample is known, and the empirical and structural evidence in its favor is strong, but a general proof is not in sight.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: What is known (unconditional)
text:
- **Caterpillars** are graceful (Rosa and successors); more generally many structured families — symmetrical trees, complete binary trees, certain spiders and bananas — are graceful, often with the stronger $\alpha$-labeling.
- **Diameter ladder:** all trees of diameter $\le 5$ are graceful (**Hrnčiar–Haviar, 2001**). Diameter $\ge 6$ is open in general.
- **Computational verification:** all trees on at most 27 vertices are graceful (**Aldred–McKay, 1998–99**), extended to roughly $29$–$35$ vertices by later searches (Fang and others) with no counterexample.
- **Closure results:** large infinite families are generated by product/composition/transfer constructions (Stanton–Zarnke; Koh–Rogers–Tan; numerous $\alpha$-composition theorems).
- **Range-relaxed labelings** always exist: every tree has distinct vertex labels in some interval $[0,k]$ with $k$ only modestly larger than $n-1$ inducing distinct edge differences. Tightening $k$ to exactly $n-1$ is the open core.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Strongest adjacent / conditional results
text: The motivating **Ringel Conjecture** — $K_{2n+1}$ decomposes into $2n+1$ copies of any given tree on $n+1$ vertices — was **proved for all sufficiently large $n$** by **Montgomery, Pokrovskiy, and Sudakov** (2020; arXiv:2001.02665), using probabilistic absorption. This settles the structural dream that originally motivated graceful labeling. Crucially, it does **not** imply the GTC: the decomposition is built with an absorbing/defect-tolerant method and does not exhibit a graceful labeling of each individual tree. Related decomposition thresholds (Glock–Joos–Kühn–Osthus; Keevash–Staden–Su) similarly bypass exact gracefulness.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: What a full resolution would require
text: A proof must produce, for **every** tree (not merely large or structured ones), an exact bijection between the edge set and $\{1,\dots,n-1\}$ realized by an integer vertex labeling in $[0,n-1]$. The two principal obstructions any approach must overcome are: (1) the **compression problem** — converting an easily obtained near-/range-relaxed labeling into one using exactly the integers $\{0,\dots,n-1\}$; and (2) **uniformity across structure** — handling trees of arbitrary diameter and degree sequence in a single argument, since every known constructive method is tied to bounded diameter, specific spine structure, or composability that generic trees lack.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)
heading: Plausible routes
text: The most discussed routes are: (a) **merging probabilistic absorption with exactness**, adapting the Montgomery–Pokrovskiy–Sudakov machinery to force an exact graceful labeling for all large trees, then handling small trees by the existing computer verification; (b) pushing the **diameter ladder** past 5 with a genuinely inductive scheme; (c) resolving **Bermond's lobster conjecture** as a stepping stone; and (d) an **algebraic/Nullstellensatz** certificate distinguishing graceful trees, currently the least developed. Most experts view route (a) as the likeliest eventual path, while cautioning that the gap between approximate decomposition and exact labeling has so far proven stubborn.

title: The Graceful Tree Conjecture (Ringel–Kotzig)

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Plain statement
text: All non-trivial zeros of every automorphic L-function (or every member of the Selberg class) lie on the critical line Re(s) = 1/2; simultaneously generalizes the Riemann and generalized Riemann hypotheses.

*Originators: Adolf Piltz, Bernhard Riemann (original case).*

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: History — The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
text: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis (GRH, "Grand" rather than "Generalized") is the maximal generalization of Riemann's 1859 conjecture: **every** $L$-function in a suitably broad class has all of its non-trivial zeros on the critical line $\mathrm{Re}(s)=\tfrac12$. In modern form it is asserted for all automorphic $L$-functions attached to cuspidal automorphic representations of $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{A}_{\mathbb{Q}})$ (more generally over any number field), and, in axiomatic form, for every element of the **Selberg class** $\mathcal{S}$.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Origin and successive reformulations
text:
The seed is Riemann's *Über die Anzahl der Primzahlen unter einer gegebenen Größe* (1859), which conjectured RH for $\zeta(s)$. The first nontrivial generalization is due to **Adolf Piltz** (1884), who, studying the distribution of primes in arithmetic progressions, extended the hypothesis to Dirichlet $L$-functions $L(s,\chi)$ — now called the **Generalized Riemann Hypothesis** (GRH$_{\text{Dirichlet}}$). The acronym is genuinely ambiguous: "Grand" is reserved for the version over the full automorphic/Selberg class, while "Generalized" usually means the Dirichlet (or Dedekind/Hecke) case.

Three structural reformulations drove the problem toward its grand form:

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Origin and successive reformulations
text:
Three structural reformulations drove the problem toward its grand form:

1. **Dedekind and Hecke.** Dedekind zeta functions $\zeta_K(s)$ of number fields, and Hecke's $L$-functions with Größencharakteren (1918–1920), placed RH inside class field theory; their RH is the **Extended Riemann Hypothesis** (ERH).
2. **Artin and automorphy.** Artin $L$-functions (1923–1930) and the conjectural correspondence with automorphic forms — crystallized in the **Langlands program** (1967–1970) — recast all arithmetic $L$-functions as automorphic $L$-functions $L(s,\pi)$ for cuspidal $\pi$ on $\mathrm{GL}_n$. GRH became the statement that each such $L(s,\pi)$ satisfies RH.
3. **Selberg's axioms.** In 1989–1992 **Atle Selberg** isolated an axiomatic class $\mathcal{S}$ of Dirichlet series (Euler product, analytic continuation, functional equation, Ramanujan-type growth) and conjectured RH for every $F\in\mathcal{S}$, together with orthonormality conjectures governing how zeros of distinct primitive elements interact. The Selberg class is believed to coincide with automorphic $L$-functions, making GRH and "RH for $\mathcal S$" effectively the same conjecture.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1859** — Riemann conjectures RH for $\zeta(s)$ in his eight-page memoir.
- **1884** — Piltz extends the hypothesis to Dirichlet $L$-functions (origin of the "generalized" form).
- **1896** — Hadamard and de la Vallée Poussin prove the Prime Number Theorem via zero-free regions — the template later used for all $L$-functions.
- **1917** — Hardy proves infinitely many zeros of $\zeta$ lie on the critical line.
- **1918–1920** — Hecke develops $L$-functions with Größencharakteren; ERH crystallizes.
- **1923–1930** — Artin introduces non-abelian $L$-functions; Artin's holomorphy conjecture links them to automorphy.
- **1942–1956** — Selberg proves a positive proportion of $\zeta$-zeros lie on the critical line.
- **1967–1970** — Langlands formulates his program; automorphic $L$-functions $L(s,\pi)$ become the natural home of GRH.
- **1974** — Deligne proves the Weil conjectures (RH for varieties over finite fields); the *function-field* analogue is a theorem, sharpening the contrast with the open number-field case.
- **1974** — Levinson shows $>34\%$ of $\zeta$-zeros are simple and on the line.
- **1989** — Conrey raises the proportion past $40\%$.
- **1989–1992** — Selberg defines the class $\mathcal{S}$ and states the Selberg conjectures; GRH attains its axiomatic "grand" formulation.
- **1999–2011** — Kaczorowski–Perelli classify low-degree elements of $\mathcal{S}$ (degrees $0$, $1$, and gaps in $(1,2)$), confirming structural expectations without resolving RH.
- **2011–2020** — Bui–Conrey–Young, Pratt–Robles, and others push the critical-line proportion past $41\%$ for $\zeta$; random-matrix and pair-correlation heuristics (Montgomery 1973, Katz–Sarnak 1999) frame GRH statistically.
- **Present** — GRH remains **open** for every degree-$\ge 1$ element beyond what RH/ERH already cover; it is unproven even for a single $\mathrm{GL}_2$ cusp form's $L$-function.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Originator(s) — The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
text: GRH has no single author; it is the cumulative widening of one idea by several figures. The Atlas records two: **Adolf Piltz**, who first generalized Riemann's hypothesis to a family of $L$-functions, and **Bernhard Riemann**, whose original 1859 conjecture is the seed case. The axiomatic "grand" formulation belongs to **Atle Selberg**, and the conceptual frame that makes it grand — automorphy — to **Robert Langlands**.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Bernhard Riemann (1826–1866)
text: Born in Breselenz, Hanover, the son of a Lutheran pastor, Riemann studied at Göttingen and Berlin under Gauss, Dirichlet, and Jacobi. His 1859 Berlin Academy memoir, written on the occasion of his election, introduced the analytic continuation of $\zeta(s)$, its functional equation, the explicit formula linking zeros to primes, and — almost in passing — the hypothesis that all non-trivial zeros lie on $\mathrm{Re}(s)=\tfrac12$. He framed it as a remark he could not prove and "set aside" for his main goal, the Prime Number Theorem. Riemann died of tuberculosis at 39 in Selasca, Italy; his collected works fill a single slim volume, yet seeded analytic number theory, complex geometry, and general relativity. The original RH is the degree-one, level-one, trivial-nebentypus case of GRH.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Adolf Piltz (1855–1940)
text: Far less famous, Piltz was a German mathematician who studied at Jena and worked on the distribution of primes. In 1884 he formulated what is now the Generalized Riemann Hypothesis: that Dirichlet $L$-functions $L(s,\chi)$ — Riemann's $\zeta$ being the principal character case — should also have all non-trivial zeros on the critical line. His motivation was concrete: control of primes in arithmetic progressions, where the error term in the prime-counting function $\pi(x;q,a)$ is governed precisely by the zeros of the relevant $L(s,\chi)$. GRH for Dirichlet $L$-functions would give essentially square-root-error equidistribution of primes across residue classes. Piltz's name is also attached to the Piltz (Dirichlet) divisor problem. His 1884 step is the first recognition that RH is not about $\zeta$ alone but about a *class* of objects sharing an Euler product and functional equation — the conceptual move that, iterated, becomes GRH.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Atle Selberg (1917–2007) and the grand formulation
text: Norwegian-born, a permanent member of the Institute for Advanced Study and a 1950 Fields Medalist, Selberg gave GRH its modern axiomatic shape. In lectures and the 1992 Amalfi paper he defined the **Selberg class** $\mathcal{S}$ by four axioms — Dirichlet series convergence, analytic continuation with finite-order growth, a functional equation of Riemann type, and an Euler product with a Ramanujan-type prime bound. He conjectured RH for *every* element of $\mathcal{S}$, and added deep "orthonormality" conjectures (Selberg's conjectures A and B) about the statistical independence of zeros of distinct primitive elements, which imply unique factorization in $\mathcal{S}$ and Artin's conjecture. This is the cleanest statement of GRH not depending on the (still partly conjectural) automorphic dictionary.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Robert Langlands (b. 1936) and the automorphic home
text: Langlands' program (1967 onward) supplies the structural reason the class is "grand": all arithmetically meaningful $L$-functions should be automorphic $L$-functions $L(s,\pi)$ for cuspidal representations of $\mathrm{GL}_n$ over a number field. Under functoriality, the Selberg class and the automorphic class are expected to coincide, so GRH is one conjecture wearing two notations.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Historical root vs. modern formulation
text: The historical root (Riemann 1859, Piltz 1884) concerns a concrete list — $\zeta$, Dirichlet, later Dedekind and Hecke $L$-functions. The modern formulation (Selberg, Langlands) is *axiomatic and uniform*: GRH is a single hypothesis quantified over an entire class, so that any new $L$-function entering the class inherits the prediction automatically. The legacy is that "the Riemann Hypothesis" is now understood not as a fact about one function but as a structural law about all $L$-functions.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Approaches — The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
text: GRH inherits every line of attack on RH, and adds challenges specific to higher degree: the absence of an unconditional Euler product bound (Ramanujan), poorly understood functional-equation data, and the difficulty of mollifying $L$-functions whose Dirichlet coefficients are not multiplicative in a tractable way. No approach has resolved RH even for $\zeta$, so all entries below are partial.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Critical-line / mollifier method (Hardy–Selberg–Levinson–Conrey)
text:
**Core idea.** Show a positive *proportion* of zeros lie on $\mathrm{Re}(s)=\tfrac12$ by computing mollified second (and higher) moments of $L$ on the critical line and applying Levinson's method to the logarithmic derivative. Hardy (1914) proved infinitely many on-line zeros for $\zeta$; Selberg got a positive proportion; Levinson (1974) reached $34\%$; Conrey (1989) $40\%$; Bui–Conrey–Young, Pratt–Robles, and Feng pushed past $41\%$ (current records hover near $41.7\%$).

**Best reached for GRH.** The machinery has been extended to Dirichlet, Dedekind, and some $\mathrm{GL}_2$ $L$-functions, giving positive proportions of on-line zeros there too.

**Barrier.** The method cannot in principle reach $100\%$: mollifier length is limited by available moment estimates, and even an optimal mollifier leaves a positive proportion uncontrolled. It also says nothing about a *single* high zero being off the line.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Pair correlation and random matrix theory (Montgomery–Odlyzko–Katz–Sarnak)
text:
**Core idea.** Montgomery's 1973 pair-correlation conjecture matches the statistics of $\zeta$-zeros to eigenvalues of large random unitary (GUE) matrices; Katz–Sarnak (1999) extended this to families of $L$-functions with symmetry type (unitary, orthogonal, symplectic). This makes GRH statistically *expected* and predicts fine zero statistics.

**Best reached.** Strong numerical confirmation (Odlyzko) and theorems on low-lying zeros of families under GRH, plus unconditional results for function-field families (where RH is Deligne's theorem).

**Barrier.** RMT is a heuristic for *where zeros are on average*; it provides no mechanism forbidding an individual off-line zero, and pair correlation is itself only partially proven (Montgomery's theorem holds in a restricted range).

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Function-field analogue (Weil–Deligne)
text:
**Core idea.** For curves and varieties over finite fields $\mathbb{F}_q$, the analogue of RH is a *theorem*: Weil (1948) for curves, Deligne (1974) for general varieties, via $\ell$-adic cohomology and the Frobenius eigenvalue bound $|\alpha|=q^{w/2}$.

**Best reached.** A complete proof in the geometric setting, and a working template (spectral interpretation of zeros as eigenvalues).

**Barrier.** $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}$ is not a variety over a field; there is no known cohomology theory ("the field with one element", $\mathbb{F}_1$) that makes $\zeta$ a geometric zeta function. Transporting Deligne's proof to number fields is the central open obstruction.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Hilbert–Pólya / spectral approach
text:
**Core idea.** Find a self-adjoint operator $H$ whose eigenvalues are the imaginary parts $\gamma$ of the non-trivial zeros; self-adjointness forces $\gamma\in\mathbb{R}$, i.e. zeros on the line. Berry–Keating and Connes propose explicit (semiclassical / adèlic) candidates.

**Best reached.** Connes (1999) gave a trace formula on the adèle class space $\mathbb{A}_{\mathbb{Q}}^\times/\mathbb{Q}^\times$ whose *spectral side* is equivalent to GRH for all $L$-functions with Größencharakter — a genuine reformulation covering many GRH cases at once.

**Barrier.** In Connes' framework GRH becomes a *positivity* statement (a Weil-type explicit formula must be a positive distribution); proving that positivity is exactly as hard as the original problem. No operator with the required spectrum has been constructed.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Selberg class structure theory (Kaczorowski–Perelli)
text:
**Core idea.** Rather than prove RH, classify $\mathcal{S}$. The degree $d_F$ (from the functional-equation gamma factors) is conjectured to be a non-negative integer; primitive elements should factor uniquely.

**Best reached.** Kaczorowski–Perelli proved there are **no** elements of degree $0<d<1$, classified degree $0$ (constants) and degree $1$ (Dirichlet $L$-functions and shifts of $\zeta$), and showed no elements exist with $1<d<2$. Each confirms the automorphic picture.

**Barrier.** Structural classification constrains *which* $L$-functions exist but never locates their zeros; the degree-$2$ case (which would include $\mathrm{GL}_2$ cusp forms) is already out of reach, and RH is untouched by these methods.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Positivity / explicit-formula criteria (Weil, Li, Bombieri)
text:
**Core idea.** Reformulate RH as a positivity condition: Weil's explicit formula is a positive quadratic form iff RH holds; Li's criterion (1997) states RH $\iff \lambda_n\ge 0$ for all $n$, where $\lambda_n=\sum_\rho[1-(1-1/\rho)^n]$. These extend verbatim to the Selberg class (Li–Sekatskii, Lagarias).

**Best reached.** Clean equivalent reformulations and partial positivity verifications; the de Bruijn–Newman constant work (Rodgers–Tao 2020 proved $\Lambda\ge 0$, with RH $\iff \Lambda\le 0$) shows the truth is "barely" true if true.

**Barrier.** Each criterion is logically equivalent to GRH, so it relocates rather than removes the difficulty; verifying the positivity unconditionally remains open.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Attempts — The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
text: No correct proof or disproof of RH — and hence of GRH — exists. What follows are the most consequential partial results, near-misses, and notable disputed claims. The pattern is consistent: every genuine advance proves something *about* zeros (counts, proportions, statistics, zero-free regions) without ever excluding a single off-line zero.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Famous partial results
text:
- **Hardy (1914):** infinitely many zeros of $\zeta$ on the critical line — the first toehold, later extended to Dirichlet and automorphic $L$-functions.
- **Selberg (1942):** a *positive proportion* of $\zeta$-zeros on the line, the first quantitative breakthrough.
- **Levinson (1974):** at least $34\%$ on the line, via the mollified logarithmic-derivative method; **Conrey (1989)** raised this to $>40\%$, and modern work (Bui–Conrey–Young 2011, Pratt–Robles–Zeindler–Keating 2020) to roughly $41.7\%$.
- **Korobov–Vinogradov (1958):** the widest known zero-free region for $\zeta$, the best unconditional input to prime-counting; analogues exist for $\mathrm{GL}_n$ $L$-functions but are weaker.
- **Deligne (1974):** RH *proven* in the function-field analogue (Weil conjectures) — the one setting where a Grand-RH-type statement is a theorem.
- **Rodgers–Tao (2020):** proved the de Bruijn–Newman constant $\Lambda\ge 0$; since RH $\iff \Lambda\le0$, this shows RH, if true, is true by the narrowest margin.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Conditional cascades and approximations to GRH
text:
- **Bombieri–Vinogradov theorem (1965):** an unconditional "GRH on average" over moduli — strong enough to substitute for GRH in many analytic-number-theory applications.
- **Density theorems** (Montgomery, Huxley, Jutila): bound the number of possible off-line zeros, severely limiting how badly GRH could fail even if it does.
- **Numerical verification:** the first $\sim 10^{13}$ zeros of $\zeta$ (Gourdon 2004, building on Odlyzko) and large initial ranges for many Dirichlet and $\mathrm{GL}_2$ $L$-functions lie exactly on the line. This is evidence, not proof.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Disputed and withdrawn claimed proofs (stated neutrally)
text:
Because RH/GRH attracts the most claimed proofs of any open problem, the community treats announcements with extreme caution. Notable episodes:

- **Louis de Branges** has circulated, over several decades, a program based on Hilbert spaces of entire functions claiming to prove RH (and extensions toward GRH). **Objection:** Conrey and Li (2000) exhibited specific counterexamples to structural hypotheses required by de Branges' positivity argument, showing the approach as stated cannot work for $\zeta$; the proofs are not accepted.
- **Michael Atiyah (2018)** announced a short proof of RH at the Heidelberg Laureate Forum, deriving it from a claimed "Todd function." **Objection:** the argument relied on properties of that function that were never substantiated and contradicted known behavior; mathematicians (e.g., commentary by Krzysztof Pomorski, J. C. Lagarias, and others) judged it incomplete. It is not accepted; Atiyah died months later.
- A steady stream of arXiv/vixra preprints claim elementary proofs; none has survived expert scrutiny. The general failure modes are (i) mis-applying a positivity/spectral criterion, (ii) an unjustified interchange of limits, or (iii) proving a statement strictly weaker than RH.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Why attempts stall
text: The recurring obstruction is structural, not computational. Critical-line methods are capped below $100\%$; spectral and positivity reformulations are *equivalent* to GRH and merely relocate the difficulty; and the one decisive proof technique we possess — Deligne's $\ell$-adic cohomology — has no known counterpart over $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}$. Until such a counterpart (or a genuinely new mechanism) appears, partial results accumulate but the line is never closed.

text: _The 25 most important papers. Each carries a verification flag. Verify flags: high-confidence = canonical, certainly real; needs-verification = real work, citation details to confirm; ai-suggested = attribution to confirm._

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Key Papers — The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
text: **Notes.** Items 1–2 are the historical roots; 3 (Piltz) is the origin of the generalized hypothesis but its precise 1884 reference is hard to pin down and is flagged accordingly. Items 11, 15, 16 anchor the automorphic ("grand") framing. Items 9, 14, 17, 24 trace the critical-line proportion record for $\zeta$; 13 is the function-field theorem; 18, 22 are the Selberg-class structure theory. DOIs are supplied only for the four items where they are confidently known; all others are pre-DOI or left as "—" rather than risk a fabricated identifier.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Top Mathematicians — The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
text:
| # | Name | Era | Role | Key contribution |
|---|------|-----|------|------------------|
| 1 | Bernhard Riemann | 1826–1866 | originator | 1859 memoir: analytic continuation, functional equation, explicit formula, and the hypothesis for $\zeta(s)$ — the seed case. |
| 2 | Adolf Piltz | 1855–1940 | originator | 1884 generalization of the hypothesis to Dirichlet $L$-functions; first recognition that RH concerns a *class* of $L$-functions. |
| 3 | Erich Hecke | 1887–1947 | breakthrough | $L$-functions with Größencharakteren; analytic theory extending RH to general number fields (ERH). |
| 4 | Emil Artin | 1898–1962 | breakthrough | Artin $L$-functions and the holomorphy conjecture; opened the non-abelian / automorphic horizon of GRH. |
| 5 | Atle Selberg | 1917–2007 | originator | Positive proportion of $\zeta$-zeros on the line; defined the Selberg class $\mathcal{S}$ and stated GRH in axiomatic "grand" form. |
| 6 | Pierre Deligne | b. 1944 | breakthrough | Proof of the Weil conjectures (1974): RH as a theorem in the function-field analogue, the model for what GRH would mean. |
| 7 | Robert P. Langlands | b. 1936 | frontier | Langlands program: automorphic $L$-functions as the natural home of GRH; functoriality unifying the class. |
| 8 | Hugh L. Montgomery | b. 1944 | frontier | Pair-correlation conjecture (1973) linking zeros to random-matrix statistics; reframed GRH probabilistically. |
| 9 | Alain Connes | b. 1947 | frontier | Noncommutative-geometry trace formula reformulating GRH for $L$-functions with Größencharakter as a positivity statement. |
| 10 | J. Brian Conrey | b. 1955 | frontier | Record proportions of on-line zeros ($>40\%$); with Katz–Sarnak and others, leader of the moments/RMT program for $L$-functions. |

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Notes
text: The active community spans several overlapping clusters. **Analytic number theorists** (Conrey, Soundararajan, Iwaniec, Sarnak, Bui, Young, Pratt, Robles) push the critical-line proportion and moment conjectures, increasingly via the connection to random matrix theory pioneered by Montgomery, Odlyzko, Katz, and Sarnak. **Selberg-class structure theory** is driven by Kaczorowski and Perelli, who classify low-degree elements and pursue the degree conjecture. **Spectral / noncommutative approaches** center on Connes and the Berry–Keating circle, seeking a Hilbert–Pólya operator. The **Langlands-program** side (Langlands, Arthur, Ngô, and the broader automorphic-forms community) supplies the functoriality results that make "every automorphic $L$-function" a coherent target. Polymath-style collaborations (e.g., the Rodgers–Tao work on the de Bruijn–Newman constant) and large-scale numerical verification (the LMFDB collaboration) keep both the theoretical and computational frontiers active. Despite this breadth, no group claims a route to a full proof; consensus holds that a genuinely new idea — most likely an arithmetic analogue of $\ell$-adic cohomology — is required.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Status & Frontier — The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
text: **Status: open.** GRH is unproven for *every* degree-$\ge 1$ $L$-function, and is not known even for a single automorphic $L$-function on $\mathrm{GL}_n$ with $n\ge 2$. It is strictly harder than RH (the degree-one, level-one case) and than the Generalized RH for Dirichlet $L$-functions, both of which it contains. There is no claimed proof accepted by the community; the recurring disputed announcements (de Branges, Atiyah) are addressed in `attempts.md`.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: What is known
text:
**Unconditional.**
- Each $L$-function in the Selberg class with the standard axioms has its non-trivial zeros confined to the critical strip $0\le\mathrm{Re}(s)\le 1$, with a classical zero-free region near $\mathrm{Re}(s)=1$ (the input to prime-number theorems in all these settings).
- For $\zeta$: a positive proportion — roughly $41.7\%$ (Bui–Conrey–Young 2011 and successors) — of non-trivial zeros lie exactly on $\mathrm{Re}(s)=\tfrac12$, and infinitely many are simple. Analogous positive-proportion results hold for Dirichlet and some $\mathrm{GL}_2$ $L$-functions.
- Density theorems bound the number of possible off-line zeros; **Bombieri–Vinogradov** (1965) gives "GRH on average" over moduli, sufficient for many applications.
- The **function-field analogue is a theorem** (Weil 1948; Deligne 1974): over $\mathbb{F}_q$, the corresponding RH holds.
- **Rodgers–Tao (2020)** proved the de Bruijn–Newman constant satisfies $\Lambda\ge 0$; since RH $\iff \Lambda\le 0$, RH is "barely" true if true (arXiv:1801.05914; *Forum of Mathematics, Pi*, doi:10.1017/fmp.2020.6).
- **Structure of $\mathcal{S}$:** Kaczorowski–Perelli proved there are no elements of degree $0<d<1$ and none with $1<d<2$, and classified degrees $0$ and $1$ — confirming the automorphic picture without locating zeros.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: What is known
text: **Conditional.** Assuming GRH, one obtains: square-root error in primes in arithmetic progressions; effective Chebotarev with strong bounds; deterministic polynomial-time primality and small least-quadratic-residue/primitive-root results; sharp subconvexity-flavored estimates; and precise low-lying-zero statistics matching Katz–Sarnak symmetry types. Vast tracts of analytic number theory are written "under GRH."

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: What a full resolution requires
text: A proof must exclude *every* off-line zero of *every* element of the class simultaneously — a uniform statement, not a function-by-function argument. The leading diagnosis is that we lack an arithmetic analogue of the geometric tools (an $\ell$-adic cohomology over $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}$, or a working "field with one element" $\mathbb{F}_1$ geometry) that made Deligne's proof possible. Equivalent positivity reformulations (Weil explicit formula, Li's criterion, Connes' trace formula) convert GRH into a single positivity statement but do not make it tractable.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Plausible routes
text:
1. **Spectral / Hilbert–Pólya:** construct a self-adjoint operator whose spectrum is the set of zero ordinates; Connes' adèlic trace formula is the most developed version, reducing GRH to a positivity.
2. **Arithmetic geometry over $\mathbb{F}_1$:** import Deligne's cohomological mechanism to the number-field setting — currently the most-hoped-for but least-developed path.
3. **Moments and RMT:** continued growth of critical-line proportions and full moment conjectures; unlikely to reach $100\%$ but disciplines expectations.
4. **Selberg-class structure:** prove the degree conjecture and Selberg's orthonormality conjectures, narrowing the class enough that automorphic input applies uniformly.

title: The Grand Riemann Hypothesis
heading: Related problems
text:
- [Riemann Hypothesis](../riemann-hypothesis/README.md) — the degree-one seed case GRH contains.
- [Montgomery's Pair Correlation Conjecture](../montgomery-pair-correlation/README.md) — the statistical face of the zeros.
- [Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture](../birch-swinnerton-dyer/README.md) — concerns the same automorphic $L$-functions at the central point.
- [Chowla Conjecture](../chowla-conjecture/README.md) — multiplicative-function statistics deeply tied to $L$-function zeros.
- [Artin's Primitive Root Conjecture](../artin-primitive-root-conjecture/README.md) — a classic consequence of GRH for Dirichlet $L$-functions.

title: Grimm's Conjecture
heading: Grimm's Conjecture
text: > **Atlas Rank #36** · CSS **51.233** · Field: *Number Theory* · Posed: **1969** · Status: `open`

title: Grimm's Conjecture
heading: Plain statement
text: Consecutive composite numbers can be assigned distinct prime divisors, one dividing each.
