*Originators: David Hilbert.*

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: History — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text:
_Origin, formulation, and timeline._

Hilbert's sixteenth problem has two parts. The **first part** concerns the topology of real algebraic curves and surfaces — the mutual position ("Lage," whence the German title *"Problem der Topologie algebraischer Kurven und Flächen"*) of the ovals of a nonsingular real plane algebraic curve of degree $n$, building on Harnack's 1876 bound of $\tfrac{(n-1)(n-2)}{2}+1$ ovals. The **second part**, which this dossier treats, asks the analogous question for trajectories: to determine the maximum number and relative position of **limit cycles** of a planar polynomial differential system
$$\dot x = P(x,y),\qquad \dot y = Q(x,y),$$
where $P,Q$ are real polynomials of degree at most $n$. A limit cycle is an isolated periodic orbit in the phase plane. The quantity in question is the **Hilbert number** $H(n)$, the supremum over all degree-$n$ systems of the number of limit cycles.

Hilbert posed the problem in his address to the International Congress of Mathematicians in Paris on 8 August 1900, as the sixteenth of his famous twenty-three problems. He was motivated by Poincaré's qualitative theory of differential equations (1881–1886), which introduced limit cycles, and by the contemporaneous work on the location of ovals. Hilbert explicitly connected the two parts as questions about the topology of real solution sets — algebraic ones in the first part, transcendental (phase-portrait) ones in the second.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: History — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text: The problem has proven extraordinarily resistant. Even the **existence of a finite bound** for the simplest nonlinear case $n=2$ (quadratic systems) remains open; no one knows whether $H(2)$ is even finite. The closely related but logically distinct **finiteness problem** — whether a *single fixed* polynomial system can have infinitely many limit cycles — is known to have a negative answer (each individual system has finitely many), but this "individual finiteness" was itself the subject of a celebrated saga of flawed and repaired proofs.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1881–1886** — Henri Poincaré develops the qualitative theory of planar differential equations and defines the notion of a limit cycle (*cycle limite*).
- **1900** — David Hilbert poses the problem at the ICM in Paris; the second part asks for bounds on the number and configuration of limit cycles of degree-$n$ polynomial systems.
- **1923** — Henri Dulac publishes a proof that an individual analytic planar system has finitely many limit cycles ("Dulac's theorem"); the argument is later found to contain an essential gap.
- **1939–1952** — N. N. Bautin studies the cyclicity of foci/centers; his 1952 result bounds the number of small-amplitude limit cycles bifurcating from a focus or center of a quadratic system by **3**.
- **1955–1957** — Petrovskii and Landis announce a (false) proof that $H(2)=3$; the error is acknowledged in the early 1960s.
- **1979–1980** — Shi Songling and, independently, Chen Lansun and Wang Mingshu construct concrete quadratic systems with **4** limit cycles, disproving the Petrovskii–Landis claim and showing $H(2)\ge 4$.
- **1981** — Yu. S. Ilyashenko exposes the gap in Dulac's 1923 proof, reopening the individual finiteness question.
- **1985–1988** — Roussarie and others recast the problem via the **Hilbert–Arnold problem** and the cyclicity of limit periodic sets (polycycles).
- **1991–1992** — Ilyashenko (*Finiteness Theorems for Limit Cycles*) and, independently, J. Écalle, complete correct proofs that each fixed analytic system has finitely many limit cycles.
- **1994** — Yu. Ilyashenko and S. Yakovenko frame the **existential / infinitesimal Hilbert 16th problem** (uniform bounds via Abelian integrals).
- **2010** — Binyamini, Novikov, and Yakovenko prove an explicit uniform bound for the number of zeros of Abelian integrals on bounded distance from the boundary (a partial result on the infinitesimal problem).
- **2016–present** — Activity on the **restricted/tangential Hilbert problem**, Liénard systems, slow–fast (geometric singular perturbation) methods, and computer-assisted lower bounds (current records put $H(2)\ge 4$, $H(3)\ge 13$ and similar growth $\gtrsim n^2\log n$ for general $n$).

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Originator(s) — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text:
_Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

**David Hilbert** (23 January 1862, Königsberg, Prussia — 14 February 1943, Göttingen) was the preeminent mathematician of the turn of the twentieth century and, with Poincaré, one of its two universalists. He took his doctorate at Königsberg in 1885 under Ferdinand von Lindemann, was shaped intellectually by his lifelong friendship with Hermann Minkowski and by Adolf Hurwitz, and in 1895 was appointed to the chair at Göttingen, which under his leadership became the world's center of mathematics until the Nazi expulsions of 1933 destroyed it.

Hilbert's career is often divided into phases, each of which he entered, transformed, and left: invariant theory (where his 1888 finite-basis theorem overturned the computational program of Gordan), algebraic number theory (the 1897 *Zahlbericht*), the foundations of geometry (*Grundlagen der Geometrie*, 1899, which axiomatized Euclid with full rigor), integral equations and spectral theory (whence "Hilbert space"), the foundations of mathematics and proof theory (the "Hilbert program"), and mathematical physics, where he worked in parallel with Einstein on the field equations of general relativity. This breadth is essential context: when Hilbert posed his problems he was not a specialist in any one of the fields they touched but a synthesist mapping the whole terrain of mathematics.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Originator(s) — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text: The sixteenth problem grew directly out of two strands alive in 1900. The first was the **topology of real algebraic curves**: Hilbert had himself contributed to the study of the ovals of sextic curves, sharpening the question of which arrangements permitted by Harnack's bound ($\tfrac{(n-1)(n-2)}{2}+1$ ovals) are actually realizable. The second was **Poincaré's qualitative theory of differential equations**, the *Mémoire sur les courbes définies par une équation différentielle* (1881–1886), which had introduced limit cycles and the index theory of singular points and had shown that the global structure of phase portraits is a topological object worthy of study. Hilbert's insight — characteristic of his taste for problems that join distant fields — was that the *position of the ovals of an algebraic curve* and the *position of the limit cycles of a polynomial vector field* are two instances of one question: how complicated can the real solution set of a degree-$n$ object be, and how are its pieces arranged in the plane? He grouped both under a single heading precisely to advertise this analogy.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Originator(s) — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text:
Hilbert expected, as the rhetoric of his Paris address makes clear, that a sufficiently developed method would yield finite, computable bounds. He did not anticipate that the second part would resist all comers for over a century, that even the existence of a bound for quadratic systems would stay open, or that the auxiliary "individual finiteness" statement (each fixed system has finitely many limit cycles) would itself require the deep transcendental machinery later supplied by Ilyashenko and Écalle. The problem's difficulty is in some sense a measure of how far the analytic theory of differential equations lagged behind the algebraic geometry that inspired it.

Note on formulation: Hilbert's original wording asks for the "maximum number and position" of limit cycles for each degree $n$ — the existence of the Hilbert number $H(n)$ as a finite quantity is implicitly assumed. The modern reading separates this into (a) the **existence** of a finite $H(n)$, (b) its **value** or growth rate, and (c) the auxiliary individual-finiteness statement. Hilbert did not distinguish these; the century of work since has shown them to be of very different depth.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Originator(s) — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text: Hilbert's broader legacy needs little rehearsal: the axiomatic method, the program for the foundations of mathematics (whose limits Gödel exposed in 1931), the formalism that organizes functional analysis, and above all the **list of 23 problems** itself, which set much of the agenda of twentieth-century mathematics. His epitaph in Göttingen reads *"Wir müssen wissen. Wir werden wissen."* — "We must know. We will know." — the closing words of his 1930 Königsberg address. For the sixteenth problem, at least, the "will" remains a hope rather than a fact.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Approaches — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text: _Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Bifurcation of small-amplitude limit cycles (focus/center cyclicity)
text: The oldest concrete line of attack counts limit cycles that bifurcate from a single weak focus or from a center under perturbation. One computes the **Lyapunov (focal) quantities** $V_1, V_3, V_5, \dots$ — coefficients in the return-map expansion — whose successive vanishing controls how many small loops can emerge. Bautin's 1952 theorem is the model result: for quadratic systems the cyclicity of any focus or center is at most $3$, achieved by perturbing within the Bautin ideal. The method gives **local** lower bounds and, when the ideal structure is understood, exact local upper bounds. **Barrier:** it is intrinsically local — it bounds cycles near one equilibrium, not the global total, and the algebra of Lyapunov quantities becomes computationally intractable as degree grows (the ideal membership problem explodes). It says nothing about large-amplitude or non-bifurcating cycles.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Abelian integrals and the infinitesimal / weakened Hilbert problem
text: Perturb an integrable (Hamiltonian or Darboux-integrable) system $H = \text{const}$ by a small polynomial term $\varepsilon(\dots)$. To first order in $\varepsilon$, limit cycles are born from ovals $\gamma_h \subset \{H=h\}$ where the **Abelian integral** (Poincaré–Pontryagin–Melnikov function) $I(h)=\oint_{\gamma_h}\omega$ vanishes. The **infinitesimal (or weakened, or tangential) Hilbert 16th problem** asks for a uniform bound $Z(n)$ on the number of zeros of such $I(h)$ over all Hamiltonians and forms of degree $\le n$. This is the most actively studied tractable proxy. Best results: the bound is known to be finite and at most **doubly exponential / explicit** in restricted settings — Binyamini, Novikov and Yakovenko (2010) gave an explicit (though astronomically large) uniform upper bound for zeros away from the boundary of the period annulus; Petrov and others gave sharp bounds for special families (e.g. elliptic Hamiltonians). **Barrier:** even this linearized problem is hard; passing from the first-order (Melnikov) approximation back to the *actual* limit cycles requires controlling all higher-order terms, and a finite infinitesimal bound does not by itself yield a finite $H(n)$.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Individual finiteness (the Dulac problem)
text: A prerequisite, not the full problem: show that a *single fixed* analytic vector field has only finitely many limit cycles. Dulac claimed this in 1923 by analyzing the **Dulac (return) map** near polycycles, but Ilyashenko found in 1981 that the asymptotic expansions used did not preclude an accumulation of cycles on a polycycle. Independently in 1991–1992, **Ilyashenko** (via complex-analytic continuation and functional cochains) and **Écalle** (via resurgent functions and "transseries"/the *Tougeron* theory) gave complete, very different proofs. **Result:** every analytic planar system has finitely many limit cycles — full stop. **Barrier:** the proofs are non-effective; they give no bound depending on degree, so they do not constrain $H(n)$ at all. Uniformity across a family (the **Hilbert–Arnold problem**: finite cyclicity of polycycles in generic finite-parameter families) remains open in general, proven only for elementary polycycles (Ilyashenko–Yakovenko, Kaloshin).

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Liénard systems and slow–fast (geometric singular perturbation) methods
text: Restricting to **Liénard equations** $\ddot x + f(x)\dot x + x = 0$ makes the problem a sandbox. Smale singled out the polynomial Liénard case as one of his eighteen problems for the 21st century, conjecturing a bound polynomial in the degree of $f$. Lins, de Melo and Pugh (1977) settled the classical case $\deg f = 2k+1$ giving exactly $k$ cycles, and conjectured the general count; this **LMP conjecture was disproved** by Dumortier, Panazzolo and Roussarie (2007), who found extra cycles. **Slow–fast** analysis (blow-up of the singular limit, Dumortier–Roussarie, Krupa–Szmolyan) produces and counts **canard** cycles and currently drives many of the strongest lower-bound constructions. **Barrier:** results are confined to special normal forms; transfer to general degree-$n$ systems is not available.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Computer-assisted and constructive lower bounds
text: Explicit constructions, often computer-verified, establish the records on the left side of the inequality $H(n)\ge \cdot$. Quadratic systems with **4** cycles (Shi 1980; Chen–Wang 1979) give $H(2)\ge 4$ — widely conjectured to be the exact value. Cubic systems have been pushed to **13** cycles (Li, Liu, Yang and others). For general $n$, configurations of order $\tfrac{1}{2}n^2\log n$ cycles have been built (Christopher–Lloyd type constructions, refined later), establishing the asymptotic lower bound $H(n) \gtrsim n^2\log n$ that exceeds the naive $\sim n^2$ guess. **Barrier:** these are existence proofs of *lower* bounds; they certify nothing about finiteness or upper bounds, which is where the actual problem lies.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Algebraic / model-theoretic and o-minimality programs
text: A newer strand seeks **effective** finiteness via tame geometry: pfaffian functions, o-minimal structures, and effective bounds on zeros of solutions of polynomial ODEs (Khovanskii's fewnomial theory, Novikov–Yakovenko, recent point-counting à la Pila–Wilkie applied to period integrals). The aim is to make the Ilyashenko–Écalle finiteness *quantitative and uniform*. **Result:** explicit bounds in restricted infinitesimal settings (as above). **Barrier:** the structures controlling the full nonlinear return map for degree-$n$ systems are not yet known to be o-minimal in a way that yields a degree-dependent bound; the "monodromic" accumulation that defeated Dulac is exactly the tame-geometry obstruction.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Attempts — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text:
_Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

**Dulac's 1923 "theorem" (gap, later repaired).** Henri Dulac published *Sur les cycles limites* claiming that every analytic planar vector field has finitely many limit cycles. The proof studied the return map near a polycycle through normal forms of the Dulac maps at the singular points. In **1981 Yu. Ilyashenko** showed the argument was incomplete: the asymptotic series used did not rule out limit cycles accumulating on a polycycle (the function might oscillate beyond all orders of its expansion). The statement was eventually re-proved correctly and independently by **Ilyashenko (1991)** and **Écalle (1992)** with entirely new transcendental machinery. This is the canonical example of a long-accepted "result" that was in fact open for nearly six decades.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Attempts — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text:
**Petrovskii–Landis (1955–1957), the false $H(2)=3$.** I. G. Petrovskii and E. M. Landis published a proof that quadratic systems have at most three limit cycles and that $H(n)$ obeys an explicit formula. The method used complexification and estimates on the variation of the return map along complex paths. By the early 1960s the authors themselves acknowledged a fundamental error in the argument; the claimed bound was wrong. The refutation became concrete around 1979–1980 when **Shi Songling** and, independently, **Chen Lansun and Wang Mingshu** exhibited explicit quadratic systems with **four** limit cycles (a small-amplitude triple near one focus plus a large cycle around another), proving $H(2)\ge 4$. Whether $H(2)=4$ remains open; $4$ is the widely held conjecture.

**Bautin's center cyclicity (1952), a genuine and durable partial result.** N. N. Bautin proved that no more than three small-amplitude limit cycles can bifurcate from a single focus or center of a quadratic system, via the structure of the ideal generated by the focal quantities (the "Bautin ideal"). This is correct and remains a cornerstone, but it is *local*: it bounds cycles near one equilibrium, not the global Hilbert number, so it does not bound $H(2)$.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Attempts — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text:
**The Lins–de Melo–Pugh conjecture (1977), disproved 2007.** For the Liénard equation with $f$ of degree $2k+1$ they proved exactly $k$ cycles in the classical case and conjectured this count as a general upper bound for polynomial Liénard systems. **Dumortier, Panazzolo and Roussarie (2007)** disproved the conjecture by exhibiting Liénard systems with more limit cycles than predicted, using slow–fast/canard techniques. This near-miss reshaped expectations about how cycle counts grow.

**Periodic claims of complete solutions.** As with several famous open problems, preprints announcing a full resolution of Hilbert's 16th (a finite explicit $H(n)$, or even just finiteness of $H(2)$) appear from time to time on arXiv and elsewhere. None has been accepted by the research community as of this writing; the consensus is that the existence of a finite bound for quadratic systems is unproven. Such claims should be treated with caution: the history above shows that even expert arguments in this area have concealed fatal gaps for decades, and the burden of independent verification is high.

**Lower-bound records as honest progress.** Distinct from disputed upper bounds, the constructive lower bounds are uncontested. Cubic systems with $11$ and later $13$ limit cycles, and general-degree configurations realizing $\sim n^2\log n$ cycles, are accepted constructions. They sharpen the conjectured growth rate but, by their nature, cannot resolve the existence question that lies at the heart of the problem.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Notes
text: The active community sits at the intersection of dynamical systems, real algebraic geometry, and transcendental number theory / tame geometry. The "Moscow school" lineage of Arnold–Ilyashenko–Yakovenko (now including Gal Binyamini and Dmitry Novikov at the Weizmann Institute) drives the infinitesimal/effective-finiteness program via o-minimality and point-counting. A strong "Catalan/Belgian–French" school around Roussarie, Freddy Dumortier, Jaume Llibre, Armengol Gasull, and Joan Torregrosa develops bifurcation theory, slow–fast/canard methods, and explicit cycle constructions. A large and productive Chinese school (Jibin Li, Yulin Zhao, Maoan Han, Chengzhi Li and many others) holds most of the current lower-bound records for cubic and higher-degree systems. Lubomir Gavrilov and Pavao Mardešić contribute on the algebraic-geometry side of Abelian integrals. Despite this breadth, the central existence question — is $H(2)$ finite? — has seen no decisive movement, and the field's recent energy is concentrated on the tractable infinitesimal proxy and on computer-assisted constructions.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Status & Frontier — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text:
_Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

**Status: open.** More than a century after 1900, the core question is unanswered even in its simplest nontrivial case. The Hilbert number $H(n)$ — the maximum number of limit cycles of a real planar polynomial vector field of degree $n$ — is not known to be **finite** for any $n \ge 2$. In particular it is unknown whether $H(2) < \infty$ for quadratic systems. This is the central, embarrassing gap: no one can rule out that some sequence of degree-2 systems has unboundedly many limit cycles.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: What is known (unconditional)
text:
- **Individual finiteness.** Every *fixed* analytic planar vector field has finitely many limit cycles (Ilyashenko 1991; Écalle 1992), repairing Dulac's flawed 1923 proof. This bounds cycles for each system separately but gives **no** degree-uniform bound, hence does not bound $H(n)$.
- **Local cyclicity.** Bautin (1952): at most $3$ small-amplitude cycles bifurcate from a focus/center of a quadratic system. Analogous finite local bounds exist for cubics.
- **Lower bounds.** $H(2)\ge 4$ (Shi 1980; Chen–Wang 1979); $H(3)\ge 13$ (Chinese-school constructions); and for general $n$, configurations of order $n^2\log n$ cycles are known, giving $H(n)\gtrsim \tfrac{1}{2}n^2\log n$. The growth is therefore at least slightly super-quadratic.
- **Smale's Liénard restriction** and slow–fast canard theory give finite, computable bounds in special families, but the Lins–de Melo–Pugh conjectured bound for general polynomial Liénard systems is **false** (Dumortier–Panazzolo–Roussarie 2007).

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Strongest conditional / partial results
text: The most tractable proxy is the **infinitesimal (weakened) Hilbert 16th problem**: bound the number of zeros of Abelian integrals $I(h)=\oint_{\gamma_h}\omega$ over degree-$n$ Hamiltonians and forms. Binyamini, Novikov and Yakovenko (2010, *Inventiones*) proved an **explicit uniform upper bound** for the number of such zeros at bounded distance from the boundary of the period annulus — the first fully effective uniform result, albeit with an astronomically large bound. Sharp counts are known for special families (elliptic Hamiltonians, the quadratic infinitesimal case). A finite infinitesimal bound is necessary groundwork but is **not** equivalent to a finite $H(n)$: passing from the first-order Melnikov approximation to the actual cycles requires controlling all higher orders and the loss of integrability.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: What a full resolution would require
text: A complete solution would need to (i) prove $H(n)$ is finite for every $n$ — the **existence** of the bound — and ideally (ii) compute or bound it, plus describe the **relative position/configuration** of the cycles. The decisive obstruction is the possible accumulation of limit cycles onto a **polycycle** (the Hilbert–Arnold problem: uniform finite cyclicity of limit periodic sets across a family). This is proven only for **elementary** polycycles (all vertices hyperbolic/semi-hyperbolic; Ilyashenko–Yakovenko, Kaloshin); the general case, with degenerate (nilpotent) and non-monodromic vertices, is open and is exactly what blocks a uniform bound.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Plausible routes
text:
1. **Effective/tame-geometry program** — make Ilyashenko–Écalle finiteness *quantitative* via o-minimality, pfaffian bounds, and Pila–Wilkie-style point counting (Binyamini–Novikov and collaborators). Most credible path to a uniform bound.
2. **Complete the Hilbert–Arnold finite-cyclicity program** for all polycycles up to some degree, then assemble a bound on $H(n)$ via compactness of the parameter space.
3. **Quadratic-only attack** — even just proving $H(2)<\infty$ (the long-conjectured value is $4$) would be a landmark and might isolate the essential difficulty.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Related problems
text:
- [Kakeya Conjecture](../kakeya-conjecture/README.md) — another deep question at the interface of real analysis and geometric measure of solution sets.
- [Jacobian Conjecture](../jacobian-conjecture/README.md) — polynomial maps and their global behavior, a sibling in real/complex algebraic dynamics.
- [Riemann Hypothesis](../riemann-hypothesis/README.md) — the comparison case among Hilbert's problems for "famous, deep, and resistant."
- [Navier–Stokes Smoothness](../navier-stokes-smoothness/README.md) — a sibling open problem in the qualitative theory of differential equations.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
text: > **Atlas Rank #36** · CSS **60.733** · Field: *Geometric Topology* · Posed: **1933** · Status: `active-progress`

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: Plain statement
text: A locally compact group acting faithfully and effectively on a connected topological manifold must be a Lie group; equivalently, no p-adic integer group acts effectively on a manifold.

*Originators: David Hilbert (5th problem lineage), Paul A. Smith.*

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: History — The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
text:
_Origin, formulation, and timeline._

The Hilbert–Smith conjecture grew out of the convergence of two distinct currents: Hilbert's fifth problem on the analytic structure of continuous transformation groups, and Paul A. Smith's study of periodic transformations and the algebra of finite group actions.

**Hilbert's fifth problem (1900)** asked whether every locally Euclidean topological group is necessarily a Lie group — equivalently, whether the requirement of differentiability in the definition of a continuous group is superfluous. Phrased in terms of group actions, a natural strengthening asks: if a locally compact group $G$ acts continuously, faithfully, and effectively on a (connected) manifold $M$, must $G$ be a Lie group? Hilbert's original problem concerns the case where $G$ itself is the manifold (acting on itself by translation); the action-theoretic version is strictly stronger and is the form the Hilbert–Smith conjecture takes.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: History — The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
text:
The decisive structural reduction came from the theory of locally compact groups. By the **Gleason–Montgomery–Zippin–Yamabe** solution of Hilbert's fifth problem (1952–1953), a connected locally compact group with no small subgroups is a Lie group, and any locally compact group is an inverse limit of Lie groups. Consequently, the only obstruction to a locally compact transformation group being a Lie group is the presence of arbitrarily small compact subgroups; a structure-theoretic argument (due in this context to Newman, Smith, Bochner–Montgomery and others) shows that one may reduce the entire conjecture to a single test case: the group $\mathbb{Z}_p$ of **$p$-adic integers**. The conjecture therefore acquires its sharp modern form:

> **No $p$-adic integer group $\mathbb{Z}_p$ can act effectively (by homeomorphisms) on a connected topological manifold.**

**Paul Smith** isolated exactly this formulation in the 1930s while developing the theory of transformation groups and the cohomological invariants of periodic maps. His 1941 paper made the $p$-adic reduction explicit and framed the problem as the central open question of the field.

A key tool is **Smith theory** and the work of **M. H. A. Newman** (1931), whose theorem bounds how a finite cyclic group of homeomorphisms can act, forbidding actions with arbitrarily small orbits on a manifold — precisely the behavior a $\mathbb{Z}_p$-action would force.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1900** — Hilbert poses his fifth problem on continuous transformation groups and the role of differentiability.
- **1931** — M. H. A. Newman proves his theorem on periodic transformations of manifolds, bounding small group actions.
- **1933** — The conjecture in its transformation-group form crystallizes in the work surrounding Smith; commonly cited as the year posed.
- **1941** — Paul A. Smith makes the reduction to $p$-adic groups explicit, giving the conjecture its standard sharp statement.
- **1943** — Bochner and Montgomery study differentiable and analytic transformation groups, sharpening structural results.
- **1952–1953** — Gleason, Montgomery, Zippin, and Yamabe solve Hilbert's fifth problem; the no-small-subgroups criterion reduces the conjecture to $\mathbb{Z}_p$.
- **1955** — Montgomery and Zippin's monograph _Topological Transformation Groups_ codifies the field and the conjecture.
- **1957** — Yang proves cohomological constraints on hypothetical $\mathbb{Z}_p$-actions (dimension-raising of orbit spaces).
- **1963** — The conjecture is confirmed for actions on $2$-manifolds (Montgomery–Zippin methods; surfaces).
- **1969** — Confirmed for $3$-manifolds via Smith-theoretic and geometric arguments.
- **1997** — Repovš and Ščepin prove the conjecture for actions by **Lipschitz** (and quasi-conformal) homeomorphisms on Riemannian manifolds.
- **2013** — John Pardon proves the **three-dimensional** Hilbert–Smith conjecture in full (arbitrary topological actions), using $L^2$-cohomology / dimension-theoretic methods.
- **2010s–present** — Effort concentrates on dimension $\geq 4$ and on weakening regularity hypotheses (Hölder, quasi-conformal). The general case in dimension $\geq 4$ remains open.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
text:
_Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

The conjecture carries two names because it sits at the junction of a famous problem posed by **David Hilbert** and a precise reformulation supplied by **Paul A. Smith**.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: David Hilbert (1862–1943)
text:
Hilbert, born in Königsberg and for most of his career the dominant figure at Göttingen, shaped nearly every branch of early-twentieth-century mathematics: invariant theory, algebraic number theory, the foundations of geometry, integral equations, mathematical physics, and the foundations of mathematics. His address to the 1900 International Congress of Mathematicians in Paris listed twenty-three problems that set much of the agenda for the century.

The **fifth problem** asked, in modern language, whether every locally Euclidean topological group is a Lie group — whether the differentiability assumed in Lie's theory of continuous groups could be derived rather than postulated. Hilbert's motivation came from the foundations of geometry (his _Grundlagen der Geometrie_, 1899) and from Lie's program: he wanted to know how much of the rigid analytic apparatus of transformation groups was forced by mere topology. The narrow group-theoretic question was settled affirmatively by Gleason, Montgomery–Zippin, and Yamabe around 1952. But the broader spirit of the problem — understanding which abstract groups can act on which spaces — generated the action-theoretic strengthening that is the Hilbert–Smith conjecture. In this sense Hilbert is the lineage originator: the conjecture is the still-open residue of his fifth problem once "group" is replaced by "transformation group."

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: Paul Althaus Smith (1900–1980)
text:
Smith, an American topologist, took his Ph.D. at Princeton in 1926 under Solomon Lefschetz, and spent much of his career at Barnard College and Columbia University. He was a foundational figure in the topology of transformation groups, and his name attaches to **Smith theory** — the homological study of the fixed-point sets of periodic maps, especially $\mathbb{Z}_p$-actions on spheres and other spaces. Smith theory shows, for example, that the fixed-point set of a $\mathbb{Z}_p$-action on a mod-$p$ homology sphere is again a mod-$p$ homology sphere, a result that became a cornerstone of equivariant topology.

It was precisely this mastery of $p$-primary periodic actions that let Smith see the heart of Hilbert's question. He recognized, building on M. H. A. Newman's 1931 theorem and the structure theory of compact groups, that the entire Hilbert-fifth-problem-for-actions could be reduced to ruling out a single hypothetical phenomenon: a faithful action of the compact group of **$p$-adic integers** $\mathbb{Z}_p = \varprojlim \mathbb{Z}/p^n$ on a manifold. A $\mathbb{Z}_p$-action would be an inverse limit of cyclic actions of order $p^n$ with arbitrarily small orbits — exactly what Newman's theorem makes implausible on a manifold but does not, by itself, forbid. Smith's 1941 formulation turned a vague structural worry into a crisp, testable conjecture, and that is the form mathematicians work on today.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: Legacy and the two formulations
text: The **historical root** is Hilbert's fifth problem: is a locally Euclidean topological group a Lie group? — now a theorem. The **modern formulation**, due to Smith, is the action-theoretic conjecture: a locally compact group acting effectively on a connected manifold is a Lie group, equivalently no $\mathbb{Z}_p$ acts effectively on a manifold. Smith's reduction is what makes the problem tractable enough to attack; Hilbert's name records its intellectual descent. Both men left vast legacies — Hilbert across all of mathematics, Smith across equivariant topology — and their joint conjecture remains one of the last open fragments of the 1900 list still resisting full resolution in dimensions four and above.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: Approaches — The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
text:
_Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

Every modern attack proceeds from the **standard reduction**: by the Gleason–Montgomery–Zippin–Yamabe solution of Hilbert's fifth problem, a locally compact group with no small subgroups is a Lie group, and any locally compact group is an inverse limit of Lie groups. The only way to fail the conjecture is to have arbitrarily small compact subgroups, and a further reduction (Newman, Smith, Bochner–Montgomery) shows it suffices to forbid an effective action of the $p$-adic integers $\mathbb{Z}_p$ on a connected manifold $M^n$. So all approaches below aim to derive a contradiction from a hypothetical effective $\mathbb{Z}_p$-action.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: Cohomological dimension theory (Smith–Yang)
text:
**Core idea.** If $\mathbb{Z}_p$ acted effectively on an $n$-manifold $M$, the orbit space $M/\mathbb{Z}_p$ would have cohomological dimension strictly larger than $n$ — a "dimension-raising" pathology. C. T. Yang (1957–1960) quantified this: for a $\mathbb{Z}_p$-action the orbit space has $\dim_{\mathbb{Z}_p} (M/\mathbb{Z}_p) = n + 2$. Combined with the fact that the action is free outside a small set, this yields strong constraints and resolves several special cases.

**Best result.** Definitive in low dimensions and the engine behind the surface ($n=2$) and $3$-manifold cases; it also underlies later $L^2$ arguments.

**Barrier.** Classical cohomological dimension theory does not, on its own, contradict the existence of an $(n+2)$-dimensional orbit space when $n \geq 4$; one needs additional manifold-specific input (Poincaré duality, $L^2$-estimates) to close the gap.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: Smith theory and Newman's theorem
text:
**Core idea.** Newman's theorem (1931) forbids a nontrivial finite cyclic group from acting on a manifold with all orbits arbitrarily small (there is a positive lower bound on the diameter of a largest orbit). A $\mathbb{Z}_p$-action is an inverse limit of $\mathbb{Z}/p^n$-actions whose orbits shrink to points, which sits right at the boundary of what Newman forbids. Smith theory controls the fixed-point and singular sets of the finite quotient actions.

**Best result.** Provides the conceptual reason the conjecture "should" be true and supplies the local constraints used in every dimension.

**Barrier.** Newman's bound is not uniform across the inverse system in a way that immediately rules out the limit action in high dimensions; the finite-stage estimates can degrade as $n \to \infty$.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: The geometric / metric regularity approach (Repovš–Ščepin)
text:
**Core idea.** Restrict the regularity of the homeomorphisms. If $\mathbb{Z}_p$ acts by **Lipschitz** maps on a Riemannian manifold, one can use the metric to control orbit geometry and Hausdorff dimension, and derive a contradiction. Repovš and Ščepin (1997) proved the Hilbert–Smith conjecture for Lipschitz (and, with related arguments, quasi-conformal) actions in all dimensions.

**Best result.** Full conjecture under Lipschitz/quasiconformal regularity, any dimension — a major unconditional theorem.

**Barrier.** Topological homeomorphisms can be wildly non-Lipschitz; the metric estimates collapse without a uniform modulus of continuity. Bridging from Lipschitz to merely continuous actions is the central obstruction.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: $L^2$-cohomology and analytic methods (Pardon)
text:
**Core idea.** John Pardon (2013) settled the **three-dimensional** case by showing that a free $\mathbb{Z}_p$-action on a $3$-manifold would force the orbit space to be a manifold of the wrong cohomological dimension, using $L^2$-cohomology / a "torsion in cohomology" argument that controls the inverse limit analytically. The method exploits the rigidity of $3$-manifold cohomology (essentially a refined dimension count made airtight in dimension three).

**Best result.** The full topological Hilbert–Smith conjecture in dimension $3$ — unconditional.

**Barrier.** The argument is dimension-specific: the cohomological coincidence Pardon exploits is special to $n=3$ and does not generalize to $n \geq 4$, where the relevant cohomology groups do not constrain the action tightly enough.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: Reduction to free actions and the structure of $M/\mathbb{Z}_p$
text:
**Core idea.** One shows any effective $\mathbb{Z}_p$-action can be taken essentially free, then studies whether $M/\mathbb{Z}_p$ can be a finite-dimensional space at all — a question in **infinite-dimensional / dimension theory** (Kolmogorov-type examples of dimension-raising maps, Dranishnikov's work).

**Best result.** Sharp statements about when quotient maps raise cohomological dimension; clarifies exactly which pathology must be excluded.

**Barrier.** Dranishnikov-style examples show dimension-raising cell-like maps _do_ exist in general topology, so the contradiction cannot come from dimension theory alone — it must use that $M$ is a genuine manifold (local Euclidean structure, duality), which is hard to feed into the limit.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: Summary of the live obstruction
text: In every framework the gap is the same: low-dimensional and regularity-restricted arguments close because of duality coincidences (dimensions $2,3$) or metric control (Lipschitz). For **topological** actions in **dimension $\geq 4$**, no method yet converts the finite-stage Newman/Smith/Yang constraints into a contradiction for the $p$-adic inverse limit.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: Attempts — The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
text: _Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: Confirmed special cases (genuine partial results)
text:
- **Dimension 2 (surfaces).** The conjecture holds for actions on $2$-manifolds, established through Montgomery–Zippin-style structure theory and Smith theory; a $\mathbb{Z}_p$-action on a surface is impossible. This was understood relatively early and is uncontroversial.

- **Dimension 3.** Settled affirmatively by **John Pardon** in 2013 (_"The Hilbert–Smith conjecture for three-manifolds,"_ Journal of the AMS / arXiv:1112.2324). Pardon shows a free $\mathbb{Z}_p$-action on a $3$-manifold would produce a quotient of impossible cohomological dimension, ruling out all topological actions in dimension three. This is the headline modern result and is accepted; it is dimension-specific and does not extend to $n\geq 4$.

- **Lipschitz and quasi-conformal actions, all dimensions.** **Repovš and Ščepin** (1997) proved that no $\mathbb{Z}_p$ can act effectively by Lipschitz homeomorphisms on a Riemannian manifold, and related work extends this to quasi-conformal actions (Martin and collaborators). These are full-dimension theorems but only under regularity hypotheses far stronger than mere continuity.

- **Smooth and real-analytic actions.** The conjecture is classically true for actions by diffeomorphisms (a compact group acting smoothly and effectively on a manifold is a Lie group, via Bochner–Montgomery and Montgomery–Zippin). The genuine difficulty is entirely in the purely **topological** category.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: Confirmed special cases (genuine partial results)
text: - **Hölder-regularity results.** Subsequent work (e.g. by Maleshich and others) pushed the Repovš–Ščepin technique to certain Hölder exponents, narrowing — but not closing — the gap between Lipschitz and continuous.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: Near-misses and partial frameworks
text:
- **Yang's dimension estimates (1957–60).** C. T. Yang's computation that a hypothetical $\mathbb{Z}_p$-action raises cohomological dimension by exactly $2$ is the closest the cohomological program comes to a contradiction; in dimensions $2$ and $3$ it suffices, but in higher dimensions the resulting $(n+2)$-dimensional orbit space is not by itself contradictory.

- **Newman's theorem (1931)** is repeatedly the "almost there" ingredient: it forbids the small-orbit behavior at every finite stage $\mathbb{Z}/p^n$, but the bounds need not survive the inverse limit in high dimension.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: On claimed full proofs
text:
The general Hilbert–Smith conjecture (all dimensions, topological actions) has attracted **announced or preprinted claims of a complete proof** from time to time, as is common for long-standing problems. As of the current frontier none has been accepted by the community: the consensus, reflected in the literature and surveys, is that the conjecture remains **open for $n \geq 4$**. Where specific preprints have circulated claiming the full result, the standard objection has been a gap in passing from finite-stage estimates to the $p$-adic inverse limit, or an implicit regularity/manifold-structure assumption smuggled into a step that is only valid in the Lipschitz or low-dimensional setting. This dossier does not endorse any such claim; the verifiable record is the set of partial results above (surfaces, $n=3$, Lipschitz/quasiconformal, smooth), all of which are accepted, and the open status of the topological case in dimension four and higher.

The honest summary: there are no famous _retracted_ proofs that entered the canon, but there is a recurring pattern of optimistic announcements that do not withstand scrutiny at exactly the inverse-limit step. Readers should treat any "Hilbert–Smith solved" report with caution until it appears in a refereed venue with community vetting.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: Top Mathematicians — The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
text: (Row 9 names a working pair; Gaven Martin's extension to quasiconformal actions belongs to the same frontier.)

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: Notes
text: The active community is small but vigorous, sitting at the interface of geometric topology, transformation groups, and geometric dimension theory. The structural backbone was laid by the Princeton–Institute for Advanced Study circle (Smith, Montgomery, Zippin, Gleason, Yamabe) in the 1940s–50s. The modern frontier is carried by **Dušan Repovš** and **Evgenij Ščepin** (metric/regularity methods and dimension theory, Ljubljana and Moscow schools), **Gaven Martin** (quasiconformal analysis, New Zealand), and **John Pardon** (Princeton/Stanford), whose 2013 three-manifold theorem reignited interest. Adjacent expertise comes from **Alexander Dranishnikov** and the cohomological-dimension school, whose dimension-raising-map examples sharpen exactly what pathology the conjecture must exclude, and from expositors such as **Terence Tao**, whose graduate text on Hilbert's fifth problem made the structure theory broadly accessible. Current effort concentrates on dimension $\geq 4$ and on lowering the regularity hypotheses in the Repovš–Ščepin program toward genuine continuity.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: Status & Frontier — The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
text:
_Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

**Status: active progress, open in general.** The conjecture is established in several important regimes but remains open for arbitrary topological actions in dimension four and above. The sharp form under attack is:

> No $p$-adic integer group $\mathbb{Z}_p = \varprojlim \mathbb{Z}/p^n$ acts effectively by homeomorphisms on a connected topological manifold $M^n$.

By the Gleason–Montgomery–Zippin–Yamabe solution of Hilbert's fifth problem (1952–53), this $p$-adic statement is equivalent to the full conjecture that a locally compact group acting effectively on a connected manifold is a Lie group.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: What is known (unconditional)
text:
- **Dimension 2:** True. No $\mathbb{Z}_p$-action on a surface.
- **Dimension 3:** True — **John Pardon**, _"The Hilbert–Smith conjecture for three-manifolds,"_ J. Amer. Math. Soc. (2013), arXiv:1112.2324. This is the strongest recent unconditional advance and the current high-water mark.
- **Smooth / real-analytic actions, all dimensions:** True classically (Bochner–Montgomery, Montgomery–Zippin): a compact group acting smoothly and effectively on a manifold is a Lie group.
- **Lipschitz and quasiconformal actions, all dimensions:** True — **Repovš–Ščepin** (1997) for Lipschitz actions on Riemannian manifolds; **Martin** for quasiconformal actions. These are full-dimension theorems but require regularity strictly stronger than continuity.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: What is known (conditional / partial)
text:
- **Yang's dimension theory** shows any hypothetical effective $\mathbb{Z}_p$-action on $M^n$ would make the orbit space $M/\mathbb{Z}_p$ have cohomological dimension $n+2$ — a constraint sufficient to derive contradictions in low dimensions but not (yet) for $n\geq 4$.
- Various Hölder-regularity refinements of the Repovš–Ščepin method narrow the gap between Lipschitz and continuous actions without closing it.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: What a full resolution would require
text: A proof of the general case must convert the finite-stage obstructions (Newman's theorem, Smith theory, Yang's dimension estimates), each valid for the cyclic quotients $\mathbb{Z}/p^n$, into a contradiction that survives the **inverse limit** defining $\mathbb{Z}_p$ — and it must do so using genuine manifold structure (local Euclidean geometry, Poincaré duality) rather than any imposed metric regularity, since Dranishnikov-type dimension-raising maps show that pure dimension theory permits the pathology in general. Concretely, one needs either (i) a high-dimensional analogue of Pardon's $L^2$/cohomological-rigidity argument that no longer relies on a dimension coincidence special to $n=3$, or (ii) a way to manufacture, from a topological action, enough metric control to invoke the Repovš–Ščepin estimates.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: Plausible routes
text:
1. **Extend Pardon's analytic method beyond $n=3$**, replacing the three-manifold cohomological coincidence with a duality-based estimate valid in all dimensions — currently the most-watched direction.
2. **Lower the regularity in the Repovš–Ščepin program** from Lipschitz/Hölder toward continuous, perhaps by averaging or smoothing a topological action into a controlled one.
3. **Sharpen cohomological dimension theory of $p$-adic quotients** to show $\dim(M/\mathbb{Z}_p)=n+2$ is incompatible with $M$ being a manifold for all $n$.

title: The Hilbert–Smith Conjecture
heading: Related problems
text:
- [Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem](../hilbert-sixteenth-problem/README.md) — another still-open fragment of Hilbert's 1900 list.
- [Novikov Conjecture](../novikov-conjecture/README.md) — large-scale topology of manifolds and group actions.
- [Baum–Connes Conjecture](../baum-connes-conjecture/README.md) — actions of groups and the $K$-theory of their operator algebras.
- [Whitehead Asphericity Conjecture](../whitehead-asphericity-conjecture/README.md) — a deep open problem in geometric/algebraic topology.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Plain statement
text: On a non-singular complex projective variety, every Hodge class is a rational linear combination of cohomology classes of algebraic cycles.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: History — The Hodge Conjecture
text: _Origin, formulation, and timeline._

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: How the problem arose
text:
The Hodge conjecture sits at the junction of three nineteenth- and twentieth-century developments: the theory of algebraic cycles (Severi, Lefschetz), the topology of complex manifolds, and the harmonic theory of differential forms. On a compact Kähler manifold $X$, W. V. D. Hodge's theory of harmonic integrals (developed in the 1930s and codified in his 1941 book *The Theory and Applications of Harmonic Integrals*) produces the **Hodge decomposition** of complex cohomology,
$$H^{n}(X,\mathbb{C}) \;=\; \bigoplus_{p+q=n} H^{p,q}(X), \qquad \overline{H^{p,q}} = H^{q,p},$$
where $H^{p,q}$ consists of classes representable by harmonic forms of type $(p,q)$. An irreducible algebraic subvariety $Z \subset X$ of complex codimension $p$ defines, via its fundamental class, a cohomology class in $H^{2p}(X,\mathbb{Q})$ whose image in $H^{2p}(X,\mathbb{C})$ lies in the middle piece $H^{p,p}(X)$. The question Hodge raised is the **converse**: which rational $(p,p)$-classes arise this way?

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: How the problem arose
text:
A **Hodge class** of codimension $p$ is an element of
$$\mathrm{Hdg}^{p}(X) := H^{2p}(X,\mathbb{Q}) \cap H^{p,p}(X).$$
The conjecture asserts that on a *non-singular complex projective* variety $X$, every Hodge class is a $\mathbb{Q}$-linear combination of fundamental classes of algebraic subvarieties (algebraic cycles). For $p=1$ this is a theorem — the **Lefschetz $(1,1)$-theorem** (Lefschetz 1924) — which identifies $\mathrm{Hdg}^{1}(X)$ with the image of the Picard group (divisors / line bundles). The Hodge conjecture is the program to extend that theorem to all codimensions.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Formulation and reformulations
text:
Hodge's original 1950 statement was broader and partly *false* as stated: it included an integral version and a statement about the Hodge filtration's lower pieces. Two corrections shaped the modern form:

- **Integral Hodge conjecture is false.** Atiyah and Hirzebruch (1962) used topological $K$-theory and Steenrod operations to produce torsion cohomology classes that are Hodge but not algebraic, forcing the rational coefficients $\mathbb{Q}$.
- **The "general Hodge conjecture" (lower filtration levels) needed amendment.** Grothendieck (1969), in a sharply worded note, showed Hodge's general statement was false and proposed the corrected version now called the **Grothendieck amended general Hodge conjecture**, phrased via coniveau / the Hodge filtration.

The standard reference statement today is the one in the official **Clay Mathematics Institute Millennium Problem description** (Deligne, 2000), which restricts to the codimension-$p$ rational case on smooth projective varieties.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1924** — Lefschetz proves the $(1,1)$-theorem, the codimension-1 case, via normal functions and Poincaré normal functions.
- **1941** — Hodge publishes *The Theory and Applications of Harmonic Integrals*, establishing the Hodge decomposition.
- **1950** — Hodge poses the conjecture in his ICM (Cambridge, MA) address, "The topological invariants of algebraic varieties."
- **1957** — Kodaira's embedding theorem and Hodge theory mature; Weil and others reformulate cycles cohomologically.
- **1961–62** — Atiyah–Hirzebruch disprove the **integral** Hodge conjecture.
- **1969** — Grothendieck publishes "Hodge's general conjecture is false for trivial reasons," giving the amended general conjecture.
- **1969** — Mumford shows $0$-cycles on surfaces with $p_g>0$ are "infinite-dimensional," signalling deep obstructions for related cycle questions.
- **1977** — Deligne, Milne, Ogus, Shih develop **absolute Hodge cycles**; Deligne proves Hodge classes on abelian varieties are absolutely Hodge.
- **1995** — Lewis publishes *A Survey of the Hodge Conjecture*, the standard modern reference.
- **2000** — Clay Mathematics Institute names it one of seven **Millennium Prize Problems** (\$1,000,000), with Deligne's official problem description.
- **2013** — Voisin's monograph and survey articles map the obstruction landscape (Kähler counterexamples, defect of the integral conjecture).
- **Present** — The conjecture remains **open** in every codimension $p\ge 2$; verified only in special families (abelian varieties of low dimension, hypersurfaces, certain $K3$ and Calabi–Yau cases) and via the unconditional Lefschetz $(1,1)$ result.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Hodge Conjecture
text: _Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: William Vallance Douglas Hodge (1903–1975)
text: W. V. D. Hodge was a Scottish mathematician, born 17 June 1903 in Edinburgh. He studied at the University of Edinburgh and then at St John's College, Cambridge, where the influence of the Cambridge geometric tradition — and contact with the Italian school of algebraic geometry — shaped his early interests. After a period at Bristol, he returned to Cambridge, becoming the **Lowndean Professor of Astronomy and Geometry** in 1936 and, later, Master of Pembroke College. He was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society in 1938 and knighted in 1959; he served as a leading figure in British mathematics until his death on 7 July 1975.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Mathematical background and the road to harmonic integrals
text:
Hodge's defining achievement was the **theory of harmonic integrals**, which he developed through the 1930s and presented systematically in his 1941 monograph *The Theory and Applications of Harmonic Integrals*. The core idea fused three strands:

- **Riemann's and Lefschetz's topology of algebraic varieties** — period integrals, the homology of complex manifolds, and Lefschetz's hard and weak theorems on hyperplane sections.
- **De Rham's theorem**, identifying cohomology with closed differential forms modulo exact ones.
- **Elliptic PDE / potential theory** — the recognition that within each de Rham class there is a *unique harmonic representative*, a form annihilated by the Laplace–Beltrami operator of a chosen (Kähler) metric.

On a compact Kähler manifold the Laplacian respects the bidegree $(p,q)$ of forms, and this is precisely what yields the **Hodge decomposition**. The original proofs in the 1941 book contained a gap in the existence theory for harmonic forms; this was repaired rigorously by **Hermann Weyl** (the "method of orthogonal projection") and, in greater generality, by the elliptic theory of **Kodaira** and others. The fully rigorous Hodge theory that resulted became one of the foundational pillars of modern complex and algebraic geometry.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Why he posed the conjecture
text: Hodge understood that his decomposition gave a purely *analytic/topological* invariant — the spaces $H^{p,q}$ — sitting on top of a variety whose origin was *algebraic*. Algebraic subvarieties (cycles) contribute classes living in the diagonal pieces $H^{p,p}$. The natural and ambitious question was whether these geometric cycles **account for all** of the rational $(p,p)$ cohomology. The $p=1$ case was already a theorem (Lefschetz's $(1,1)$-theorem), tying $H^{1,1}\cap H^2(\mathbb{Z})$ to divisors. Hodge's conjecture is the bold extrapolation of that single known case to all codimensions, articulated in his **1950 International Congress of Mathematicians address** in Cambridge, Massachusetts, "The topological invariants of algebraic varieties."

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Historical root vs. modern formulation
text: Hodge's 1950 statement was, as posed, partly incorrect. He included an **integral** version (later disproved by Atiyah–Hirzebruch, 1962) and a "general" form about deeper levels of the Hodge filtration that **Grothendieck (1969)** showed to be false "for trivial reasons," replacing it with the amended general Hodge conjecture. The surviving, sharpened statement — rational coefficients, smooth projective varieties, codimension-$p$ Hodge classes — is the one enshrined as a Clay Millennium Problem.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Legacy
text: Beyond the conjecture, Hodge co-authored with **Daniel Pedoe** the influential three-volume *Methods of Algebraic Geometry*, a bridge between the classical Italian school and modern foundations. **Hodge theory** — Hodge structures, variations of Hodge structure, mixed Hodge structures (Deligne), and the entire industry of period maps — descends directly from his work and underlies much of twentieth- and twenty-first-century algebraic geometry, transcendental number theory, and mathematical physics. The London Mathematical Society's Hodge Prize and the Hodge Institute in Edinburgh both honour his name.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Approaches — The Hodge Conjecture
text: _Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: The Lefschetz $(1,1)$-theorem and induction by codimension
text: The only fully proved instance is codimension $p=1$: the **Lefschetz $(1,1)$-theorem** identifies $H^{1,1}(X)\cap H^2(X,\mathbb{Z})$ with the classes of divisors, via the exponential sequence and the Picard group. The natural hope is to climb in codimension. **Hard Lefschetz** and the **Lefschetz hyperplane theorem** let one transfer cohomology between $X$ and its hyperplane sections, and the **Lefschetz standard conjecture** (that the inverse of hard Lefschetz is induced by an algebraic correspondence) would yield large parts of the Hodge picture. *Best result:* hard Lefschetz is a theorem (Hodge theory; Deligne in char $p$), but the standard conjecture itself is open, so this route stalls. *Barrier:* no general mechanism produces cycles in codimension $\ge 2$; products and intersections of divisors generate only a (generally small) subring of $H^{2p,p}$.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Absolute Hodge cycles and the abelian-variety case
text: Deligne's theory of **absolute Hodge cycles** (Deligne–Milne–Ogus–Shih, 1982) provides Hodge classes that are simultaneously Hodge in every $\ell$-adic and de Rham realization. Deligne **proved every Hodge class on an abelian variety is absolutely Hodge**, a major structural result and the strongest unconditional evidence for the conjecture on abelian varieties. *Best result:* the Hodge conjecture is known for abelian varieties whose Hodge classes are generated by divisors (e.g. simple abelian varieties of prime dimension, products of elliptic curves; many cases via the **Hodge group / Mumford–Tate group** being large). *Barrier:* the **Weil and Mumford–Tate abelian varieties** carry "exotic" Hodge classes not built from divisors; for these the conjecture remains open, and absolute-Hodge-ness does not by itself produce a cycle.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Mumford–Tate groups and the André–Deligne machinery
text: One reframes Hodge classes as the invariants of the **Mumford–Tate group** acting on cohomology; algebraicity questions become questions about which invariants are "motivated." André's theory of **motivated cycles** unconditionally realizes many Hodge classes (those obtained from hard Lefschetz and Künneth) as motivated, capturing properties expected of algebraic cycles. *Best result:* the standard conjectures hold for abelian varieties' motivated motives; Künneth and Lefschetz components are motivated. *Barrier:* "motivated" is weaker than "algebraic"; bridging that final gap is exactly the open problem.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Normal functions and Griffiths' infinitesimal method
text: Griffiths revived **Lefschetz's normal functions** as a tool: a Hodge class spreads out over a family and defines a normal function (a section of a family of intermediate Jacobians); the class is algebraic iff a certain singularity / zero-locus condition holds. Green–Griffiths and Brosnan–Fang–Nie–Pearlstein turned this into a program: the Hodge conjecture would follow from the existence of **singularities of normal functions** along the right loci. *Best result:* the **Hodge conjecture is equivalent** to such a singularity statement after spreading out (Green–Griffiths, de Cataldo–Migliorini variants); the algebraicity of the zero locus of admissible normal functions (Brosnan–Pearlstein, building on Cattani–Deligne–Kaplan's algebraicity of Hodge loci) is established. *Barrier:* producing the singularities themselves — i.e. actually constructing the cycle — has not been achieved in general.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Cattani–Deligne–Kaplan: algebraicity of Hodge loci
text: A landmark *unconditional* result: **Cattani, Deligne, and Kaplan (1995)** proved that the locus where a given Hodge class remains Hodge (the **Hodge locus**) is a countable union of algebraic subvarieties of the base. This is exactly what the Hodge conjecture predicts (Hodge loci should be cut out by the algebraicity of cycles) and is strong evidence, recently reproved and extended by o-minimality / **definability** methods (Bakker–Klingler–Tsimerman, 2020). *Barrier:* algebraicity of the *locus* does not produce an algebraic *cycle* realizing the class.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Hodge theory in characteristic $p$ and the Tate conjecture
text: The **Tate conjecture** is the arithmetic analogue (Galois-invariant $\ell$-adic classes are algebraic). Progress on Tate (e.g. for $K3$ surfaces, abelian varieties by Faltings, Tate, Zarhin) informs Hodge via comparison theorems. *Barrier:* the two conjectures are independent in general; neither is known to imply the other unconditionally.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Negative and limiting results (obstructions)
text:
- **Integral Hodge conjecture is false** (Atiyah–Hirzebruch, 1962; refined by Kollár's degree examples and Totaro): torsion and non-torsion integral classes can be non-algebraic. This forces rational coefficients and is a hard ceiling on naive integral strengthenings.
- **Kähler (non-projective) version is false** (Voisin, 2002): there exist compact Kähler manifolds with Hodge classes not generated by classes of *analytic* cycles, so the conjecture is genuinely tied to **projectivity**.
- **Griffiths' non-finitely-generated / non-algebraic intermediate Jacobians** and **Mumford's infinite-dimensionality** of $0$-cycles show the Chow-group / cycle-class map is far from an isomorphism, dimming hopes for a soft surjectivity argument.
- There is no known **diagonal cycle / motivic** construction outputting codimension-$\ge 2$ cycles from cohomological input — the structural shortage of cycles, not a logical obstruction, is the central difficulty.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Attempts — The Hodge Conjecture
text: _Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Established partial results (genuine progress, not full proofs)
text:
- **Lefschetz $(1,1)$-theorem (Lefschetz, 1924).** The codimension-1 case is a full theorem; it is the historical anchor and the reason the conjecture seemed plausible.
- **Abelian varieties (Deligne, 1982).** Deligne proved every Hodge class on an abelian variety is **absolutely Hodge**. The Hodge conjecture itself is known for many abelian varieties — e.g. when the Hodge ring is generated by divisors, and for various Mumford–Tate configurations — but **not all**; **Weil-type** abelian varieties with exotic Hodge classes remain open.
- **Cattani–Deligne–Kaplan (1995).** Hodge loci are algebraic. Widely viewed as the strongest unconditional structural evidence; it confirms a prediction of the conjecture without producing cycles.
- **$K3$ and abelian surfaces.** The conjecture holds for $K3$ surfaces and abelian surfaces (Hodge classes in $H^2$ are divisor classes by Lefschetz $(1,1)$, and there are no higher non-trivial codimensions to test).
- **Hypersurfaces and complete intersections.** For low degrees/dimensions the Hodge classes are spanned by the hyperplane class and its powers; the conjecture holds trivially or via explicit cycles in many such families.
- **Shioda, Schoen, and others** verified the conjecture for specific families (Fermat hypersurfaces of certain degrees, products of curves, some Calabi–Yau threefolds) by explicitly exhibiting the cycles.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Conditional reductions and equivalences
text:
- **Standard conjectures (Grothendieck).** The Lefschetz standard conjecture would settle major structural pieces; it remains open but is a recognized, respectable line.
- **Normal-function singularity program (Green–Griffiths; Brosnan–Fang–Nie–Pearlstein).** Reduces the Hodge conjecture to the existence of singularities of admissible normal functions. The supporting algebraicity inputs (zero loci of normal functions) were proved, but the reduction does not by itself yield a proof — the predicted singularities have not been produced in general.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Disputed / withdrawn / unverified claimed proofs
text:
The Hodge conjecture, being a Millennium Prize Problem, attracts purported proofs; **none has been accepted by the mathematical community**, and several have been formally withdrawn or refuted. Stated neutrally:

- Numerous **arXiv preprints** claiming a general proof have appeared over the years (e.g. periodically in the math.AG listings). In every case to date, experts have identified gaps — most commonly an unjustified leap from a Hodge class being *cohomologically* or *motivically* distinguished to it being represented by an *actual algebraic cycle* — and the claims have not survived refereeing. Because these are not published, peer-reviewed results and the specific authors' claims have not been validated, the Atlas does **not** endorse any of them.
- The Clay Mathematics Institute requires publication in a refereed venue and a two-year waiting period before a prize claim is considered; **no claim has reached that bar.**

The honest summary: there are deep, genuine partial results and several precise equivalent reformulations, but **no correct proof, and no widely credited near-miss that the community treats as "almost there."** The principal obstruction is not a known logical barrier (unlike, say, the parity barrier in sieve theory or relativization in complexity) but the structural **scarcity of constructions producing high-codimension algebraic cycles** from cohomological data.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Notes
text: The active community sits at the intersection of **complex algebraic geometry, Hodge theory, and arithmetic geometry**. Core contemporary figures include Claire Voisin (Collège de France), Pierre Deligne (IAS, emeritus), and the **Hodge-loci / o-minimality** circle — Bruno Klingler, Benjamin Bakker, Jacob Tsimerman, and collaborators — who have reproved and extended Cattani–Deligne–Kaplan using definability (tame geometry). A second cluster works on **normal functions and singularities** (Gregory Pearlstein, Patrick Brosnan, Matt Kerr, Mark Green, and the late–career program of Griffiths). Arithmetic analogues (the **Tate conjecture**, motivated cycles) are pushed by Yves André, and by researchers on $K3$ surfaces and Shimura varieties. Much current activity is *indirect*: rather than attacking the full statement, the field accumulates structural results (algebraicity of Hodge loci, motivic realizations, special families) that the conjecture predicts. The problem is widely regarded as one of the hardest of the Millennium Problems, with no consensus route to resolution.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Status & Frontier — The Hodge Conjecture
text:
_Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

**Status: OPEN.** A Clay Mathematics Institute Millennium Prize Problem (\$1,000,000), unresolved in every codimension $p \ge 2$. No accepted proof or refutation of the rational, smooth-projective statement exists.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: What is known (unconditional)
text:
- **Codimension 1 is settled:** the Lefschetz $(1,1)$-theorem realizes every integral $(1,1)$-class as a divisor class. This is the only complete case.
- **Algebraicity of Hodge loci** (Cattani–Deligne–Kaplan, 1995; reproved via tame geometry by Bakker–Klingler–Tsimerman, 2020): the locus where a Hodge class stays Hodge is algebraic — a prediction of the conjecture, confirmed without producing cycles.
- **Abelian varieties:** Deligne proved all Hodge classes there are **absolutely Hodge** (1982); the Hodge conjecture itself holds for many — but not all — abelian varieties (those with Hodge ring generated by divisors; numerous Mumford–Tate cases). Weil-type classes remain open.
- **Special families:** $K3$ and abelian surfaces, low-degree hypersurfaces and complete intersections, Fermat varieties of certain degrees, and various explicit Calabi–Yau threefolds are verified by exhibiting cycles directly.
- **Hard ceilings:** the **integral** Hodge conjecture is false (Atiyah–Hirzebruch, 1962; Kollár; Totaro), and the **Kähler** extension is false (Voisin, 2002) — pinning the live statement to *rational* coefficients on *projective* varieties.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Strongest conditional results
text:
- The **Lefschetz standard conjecture** (Grothendieck) would deliver major structural pieces (algebraicity of Künneth and Lefschetz operators).
- The **normal-function singularity program** (Green–Griffiths; Brosnan–Fang–Nie–Pearlstein) reduces the conjecture to producing singularities of admissible normal functions; the supporting algebraicity (zero loci) is proved, but the construction of cycles is not.
- **Motivated cycles** (André) realize many Hodge classes with cycle-like properties, conditionally bridging part of the gap.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: What a full resolution would require
text: A proof must supply, for an *arbitrary* Hodge class on an arbitrary smooth projective $X$, an **actual algebraic cycle** (a $\mathbb{Q}$-combination of subvarieties) with that cohomology class. The central difficulty is the **structural scarcity of cycle constructions** in codimension $\ge 2$: cohomology offers no general recipe for producing subvarieties. Unlike sieve theory's parity barrier or complexity's relativization, there is no identified *logical* obstruction — but also no known engine of cycles. A disproof, conversely, would need a *projective* variety with a *rational* Hodge class provably not algebraic; all current counterexamples evade projectivity or rationality.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Plausible routes
text:
1. **Tame geometry / o-minimality** pushing beyond algebraicity of Hodge loci toward effective cycle construction.
2. Settling the **standard conjectures** or the Tate conjecture in enough generality to transfer.
3. **Normal functions:** proving the predicted singularities exist.
4. New **motivic or derived-category** constructions producing high-codimension cycles.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Related problems
text:
- [Birch–Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture](../birch-swinnerton-dyer/README.md) — sibling Millennium Problem; algebraic cycles / Chow groups and arithmetic of varieties.
- [Riemann Hypothesis](../riemann-hypothesis/README.md) — Millennium Problem; the function-field analogue (Weil conjectures) links to the standard conjectures.
- [P versus NP](../p-versus-np/README.md) — companion Millennium Prize Problem.
- [Yang–Mills Mass Gap](../yang-mills-mass-gap/README.md) — Millennium Problem at the geometry–physics interface.
- [Navier–Stokes Smoothness](../navier-stokes-smoothness/README.md) — Millennium Problem; PDE/analytic methods adjacent to Hodge-theoretic harmonic analysis.
