title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: History — The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
text: _Origin, formulation, and timeline._

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Origin and formulation
text:
A finite family $\mathcal{F}$ of sets is **union-closed** if $A \cup B \in \mathcal{F}$ whenever $A, B \in \mathcal{F}$. The conjecture asserts that for any such family containing at least one non-empty set, there exists an element $x$ belonging to at least half of the members of $\mathcal{F}$; equivalently, some element lies in at least $\lceil |\mathcal{F}|/2 \rceil$ of the sets. The empty family and the family $\{\emptyset\}$ are the only excluded cases.

The problem is universally attributed to **Péter Frankl**, who is reported to have circulated it around **1979**. There is no founding research paper: like several of Frankl's combinatorial questions, it spread orally and through problem lists. Roland Hindman's 1992 note and Ron Graham's lectures helped fix the canonical statement. Stanley Burris and others later traced the earliest written appearance to a 1979 conference setting. The lattice-theoretic reformulation is older in spirit: by associating to $\mathcal{F}$ the join-closed structure it generates, the conjecture becomes a statement about **finite lattices** — every finite lattice with $n \ge 2$ elements contains a join-irreducible element lying below at most half of the elements (dually, above at least half). This equivalence, made precise by Poonen and by Reinhold, ties the problem to order theory.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Origin and formulation
text: Several equivalent formulations circulate: the **set form** above; the **lattice form**; an **intersection-closed (dual) form** asserting that some element is missing from at least half the sets of an intersection-closed family; and a **graph form** for the special case of families generated by edges. The framing as a question about the "abundance" of elements invites averaging and, more recently, **entropy** arguments.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1979** — Péter Frankl poses the conjecture; it begins circulating informally among combinatorialists.
- **1985** — Duffus and others discuss the lattice reformulation; the problem gains visibility in order-theory circles.
- **1989** — Bjorn Poonen establishes equivalences between the set-theoretic, lattice, and other formulations, clarifying the problem's scope.
- **1990–1992** — Renaud, Sarvate–Renaud, and Knill produce small-case verifications and structural results; Sarvate and Renaud publish a noted "counterexample to a stronger statement," underscoring that naive averaging fails.
- **1994** — Theodore Knill and others give bounds showing the conjecture holds when the largest set is small.
- **1998** — Robert Roberts and Ian Roberts, and separately Norton/Sarvate, verify the conjecture by computer for families on small ground sets (up to roughly 11 elements over time).
- **2001** — Giovanni Lo Faro and others extend computational verification; the "$n \le 11$" frontier is consolidated.
- **2001** — Piotr Wójcik proves that the largest set forces abundant elements under size conditions, an early density-type partial result.
- **2009–2012** — The conjecture is featured as a Polymath-style target and in surveys (Bruhn–Schaudt later catalogue the landscape). Czédli, and Czédli–Schmidt, push lattice-side bounds.
- **2013** — Henning Bruhn, Pierre Charbit, Oliver Schaudt, and Jan Arne Telle connect the conjecture to **stable sets in graphs** and prove it for families whose generated structure is suitably graph-like.
- **2014** — Bruhn and Schaudt publish the influential survey "The journey of the union-closed sets conjecture," organizing methods and partial results.
- **2018** — Improvements to the "frequent element" averaging bound; Knill-type and Wójcik-type density results refined. Best general lower bound on the maximum frequency remains a small fraction.
- **November 2022** — **Justin Gilmer** posts an entropy-based argument proving that some element appears in at least a constant fraction $c \approx 0.01$ of the sets — the first unconditional constant-fraction bound, a conceptual breakthrough.
- **November–December 2022** — Within weeks, several groups (Will Sawin; Zachary Chase and Shachar Lovett; Ryan Alweiss, Yang Liu, Mehtaab Sawhney, Kewen Wu; David Ellis–Maria-Romina Ivan–Imre Leader; Luke Pebody; Chris Cao and others) independently sharpen Gilmer's method, raising the constant to $\frac{3 - \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 0.38$.
- **2023** — Will Sawin and others show $0.38$ is essentially the natural barrier of the "one-step" entropy approach; refinements push slightly past it but $1/2$ remains out of reach. Sawin obtains a small improvement beyond $0.38$.
- **2024–2025** — Continued work on closing the gap to $1/2$, on the lattice and qualitative forms, and on whether entropy methods can in principle reach $1/2$. The conjecture remains **open** in full generality.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Originator(s) — The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
text: _Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Péter Frankl
text: **Péter Frankl** (born 26 March 1953, Kaposvár, Hungary) is a Hungarian-born combinatorialist, one of the most prolific figures in twentieth- and twenty-first-century extremal set theory. He earned his doctorate in Hungary and built his early reputation within the Erdős school of combinatorics, working closely with Paul Erdős, Vojtěch Rödl, Zoltán Füredi, Ronald Graham, Gil Kalai, and many others. He held positions in France at the CNRS for much of his research career and later settled in Japan, where he is also widely known as a public figure — a street performer, juggler, and popularizer of mathematics fluent in many languages. His combination of deep research output and public visibility is unusual among working mathematicians.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Péter Frankl
text: Frankl's research centers on **extremal problems for set systems**: how large or how structured a family of subsets of an $n$-element ground set can be subject to intersection, union, or shadow constraints. He is a central contributor to the theory surrounding the **Erdős–Ko–Rado theorem** and its many generalizations, to **shadow and compression** techniques (the Kruskal–Katona circle of ideas), to **Δ-systems / sunflowers** (the Erdős–Rado sunflower problem, on which his bounds were long state of the art), and to **traces and VC-dimension**. The methodological signature of his work is the manipulation of set families by compression and averaging, and the extraction of an "abundant" or "popular" object from a constrained family. The union-closed sets conjecture sits squarely in this tradition: it asks for an abundant *element* inside a family closed under a natural operation.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Péter Frankl
text: The motivation for posing the conjecture, as Frankl has recounted, was disarmingly simple. Union-closed families are ubiquitous — they arise as lattices of closed sets, as collections of unions of a generating set system, and in countless concrete constructions. It is *easy* to verify the abundance property in every small example one writes down, and the extremal cases (such as the full power set, where every element lies in exactly half the sets, or a chain) all satisfy it with equality or comfortably. That tension — overwhelming empirical support against the apparent absence of any handle for a proof — is exactly the kind of clean, deceptively elementary statement Frankl was drawn to propose. He reportedly circulated it around **1979**; it was never the subject of a dedicated founding paper by him but propagated through problem sessions, correspondence, and the problem lists that Erdős-school combinatorialists maintained.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Péter Frankl
text:
It is worth distinguishing the **historical root** from the **modern formulation**. Frankl's original phrasing is the element-abundance statement for finite union-closed families. The equivalent **lattice formulation** — every finite lattice with at least two elements has a join-irreducible element lying below at most half of its elements — was not Frankl's framing but emerged from the order-theory community (Duffus, Poonen, Reinhold, Czédli, Rival) as the problem was absorbed into lattice theory. The two are provably equivalent, but they motivate different toolkits: the set form invites entropy and averaging, the lattice form invites structural induction on irreducibles. Frankl himself has generally worked and spoken about the set-theoretic version.

Frankl's broader legacy is enormous: hundreds of papers, foundational results in extremal combinatorics, and a generation of collaborators and students influenced by his style. Among the many open problems bearing his name, the union-closed sets conjecture is the most famous precisely because it is the most elementary to state and the most stubbornly resistant — a single, crisp inequality that nearly half a century of combinatorics could not settle, until Gilmer's 2022 entropy breakthrough finally produced an unconditional constant-fraction bound and reopened the problem to systematic attack.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Approaches — The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
text:
_Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

Throughout, $\mathcal{F}$ is a finite union-closed family on ground set $X$, $m = |\mathcal{F}|$, and for $x \in X$ we write $d(x)$ for the number of members of $\mathcal{F}$ containing $x$. The conjecture is that $\max_x d(x) \ge m/2$.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Entropy / information-theoretic method (Gilmer's approach)
text: The current dominant line, introduced by **Justin Gilmer** in November 2022. Sample two members $A, B$ of $\mathcal{F}$ independently and uniformly; union-closure forces $A \cup B \in \mathcal{F}$. Comparing the entropy of $A \cup B$ to those of $A$ and $B$ coordinate-by-coordinate yields, *unless some element is abundant*, a contradiction via the concavity/subadditivity of the binary entropy function. Gilmer proved unconditionally that $\max_x d(x) \ge c\, m$ with $c \approx 0.01$ — the first constant-fraction bound after four decades. The **core idea** is that abundance is the only way the entropy bookkeeping can balance. **Best result:** within weeks, Sawin; Chase–Lovett; Alweiss–Liu–Sawhney–Wu; and Pebody independently optimized the argument to $c = \frac{3-\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 0.3820$. **Known barrier:** Gilmer already observed that the *single-step* two-set entropy inequality cannot, by itself, exceed $\frac{3-\sqrt5}{2}$; the extremal configuration for the inequality saturates there. Reaching $1/2$ requires genuinely new input (a non-uniform sampling distribution, multi-set unions, or correlations the one-step method discards). Sawin gave a small improvement strictly beyond $0.38$, confirming the barrier is not absolute but showing how hard the remaining gap is.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Averaging and "abundant element" counting
text: The oldest elementary approach. One seeks a weighting or counting identity forcing $\sum_x d(x) = \sum_{A \in \mathcal F} |A|$ to be large enough that some $d(x) \ge m/2$. **Core idea:** if the average set size is large, abundance follows by pigeonhole. **Best result:** the conjecture holds whenever the average set size is at least... is **not** generally true — naive averaging fails, because families exist whose average set size is small. Knill and Wójcik obtained density-type partial results (e.g. some element in roughly $m / \log_2 m$ sets), and Wójcik (2001) and Bošnjak–Marković handled size-restricted cases. **Known barrier:** Sarvate and Renaud exhibited union-closed families in which the *most frequent element of a generating set* is not abundant, showing that the natural averaging heuristics and several plausible strengthenings are simply false; any averaging proof must be subtle.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Small-ground-set and "few sets" structural cases
text: Verify the conjecture for all families on a small ground set $|X| = n$, or with few members $m$, by structure plus computation. **Core idea:** the lattice of a union-closed family on few points has bounded structure, enabling case analysis or exhaustive search. **Best result:** the conjecture is verified for all union-closed families with $|X| \le 12$ (work of Bošnjak–Marković and predecessors Roberts–Roberts, Lo Faro, Morris) and for all families with $m \le 50$ members. It also holds when $\mathcal{F}$ contains a set of size $1$ or $2$ (an immediate strong case), when the largest set has size $\le$ a small bound, or when $|X| \le 2\log_2 m$ roughly. **Known barrier:** these results do not scale — the search space grows super-exponentially, and structural arguments for small $n$ give no leverage on large families.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Lattice-theoretic formulation
text: Translate to: every finite lattice $L$ with $|L| \ge 2$ has a join-irreducible $j$ with at most $|L|/2$ elements above it. **Core idea:** induct on join-irreducibles, or exploit lattice congruences and the structure of the irreducible "spine." **Best result:** the conjecture holds for large classes of lattices — lower-semimodular lattices, relatively complemented lattices, modular lattices (Abe, Reinhold, Czédli–Schmidt), lattices of dimension/height bounds, and planar/2-dimensional lattices. Czédli proved cases for "large" lattices relative to their generating structure. **Known barrier:** the irreducibles can be numerous and their up-sets uncorrelated; no induction has been found that controls the general lattice, and the modular/semimodular successes rely on closure properties the general case lacks.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Graph and stable-set reformulations
text: Bruhn, Charbit, Schaudt, and Telle (2013) recast a sub-case in terms of **stable (independent) sets**: a graph $G$ gives rise to the union-closed family of complements of its stable sets, and the conjecture restricted there becomes a statement that some vertex lies in at most half the stable sets. **Core idea:** transfer extremal graph-theoretic tools (and a known result that, in the "twin-free" graph setting, such a vertex exists) into the set-system world. **Best result:** the conjecture holds for all union-closed families arising from graphs (every union-closed family generated by sets of size $\le 2$, equivalently the graph case), and for chordal, bipartite, and several other graph classes. **Known barrier:** general union-closed families are generated by sets of *arbitrary* size; the graph reduction covers only the size-$\le 2$ slice, and no reduction of the general problem to the graph case is known.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Salzborn / generating-family and "weight" approaches
text: Salzborn's formulation and various weighting schemes (Poonen's symmetric-group / averaging-over-families framework) attempt to find a probability distribution on $X$ under which the expected frequency is provably $\ge m/2$. **Core idea:** if *no* element is abundant, derive a contradiction from a clever convexity or symmetry argument over the space of union-closed families. **Best result:** Poonen characterized exactly when a fixed weighting "works" for *all* families with a given up-set, giving necessary-and-sufficient conditions but not a universal weighting. **Known barrier:** Poonen's own analysis shows no single fixed weighting can certify the conjecture for all families, so weighting must be family-dependent — which is essentially as hard as the original problem.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Attempts — The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
text: _Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: The pre-2022 plateau
text: For more than four decades, every general result took the form "for every union-closed family, *some* element appears in at least $g(m)$ sets," with $g$ embarrassingly weak. The strongest unconditional general bounds before the entropy era guaranteed only an element of frequency on the order of $m/\log_2 m$ or a fixed small absolute count derived from the number of sets — a logarithmic gap from the conjectured $m/2$. The frustration was that no construction came close to violating the conjecture: in the extremal-looking families the most frequent element sits at exactly $m/2$ (power sets) or comfortably above. This combination — a true-looking statement with no near-counterexample and no proof — is what gave the problem its reputation as a trap.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Sarvate–Renaud and the failure of natural strengthenings
text: A famous near-miss of a different kind: **D. G. Sarvate and J.-C. Renaud** (around 1989–1990) showed that several *plausible strengthenings* are false. In particular, the most frequent element need not be one of the singletons or small generators; there exist union-closed families where an element that "ought" to be abundant by symmetry or generation is not. This killed the most natural averaging proofs and is the standard cautionary example quoted in the literature. It is not a counterexample to the conjecture itself — the conjecture survives — but to the easy routes toward it.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Verified special cases (genuine, accepted partial results)
text: These are accepted, not disputed: the conjecture holds when the family contains a singleton or doubleton; when $|X| \le 12$ (Bošnjak–Marković, extending Roberts–Roberts, Lo Faro, Morris); when $m \le 50$; for families generated by sets of size $\le 2$ (the graph case, via Bruhn–Charbit–Schaudt–Telle 2013); for lower-semimodular, modular, and relatively complemented lattices (Abe, Reinhold, Czédli–Schmidt); and when $m \ge \frac{2}{3} 2^{|X|}$ (large, "dense" families). Reimer's theorem on the average set size of union-closed families is a celebrated adjacent result often cited in attempts.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Gilmer's breakthrough and its rapid sharpening
text: In **November 2022**, **Justin Gilmer** (then at Google) posted "A constant lower bound for the union-closed sets conjecture," proving unconditionally that some element appears in at least $\approx 1\%$ of the sets via an entropy argument. This is a genuine, accepted breakthrough — the first constant-fraction bound. Gilmer explicitly conjectured his method could be pushed to $\frac{3-\sqrt5}{2} \approx 0.38$. Within roughly two weeks, this was achieved independently by **Will Sawin**; **Zachary Chase and Shachar Lovett**; **Ryan Alweiss, Yang Liu, Mehtaab Sawhney, and Kewen Wu**; and **Luke Pebody**; with related observations by **David Ellis, Maria-Romina Ivan, and Imre Leader** and others. Sawin subsequently obtained a small improvement strictly beyond $0.38$. These are all peer-accepted; the episode is a model of fast, transparent collaborative verification (much of it on arXiv and blogs within days).

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Claimed full proofs and disputed announcements
text: The problem has attracted occasional claimed *complete* proofs, none of which has been accepted. Several preprints announcing a resolution of the full conjecture have appeared on arXiv and preprint servers over the years; the consistent community assessment is that each contains a gap — typically an unjustified averaging step, an inductive claim that fails for the irreducibles, or an implicit assumption equivalent to the conjecture. As of the latest reference frontier, **no claimed full proof has survived expert scrutiny**, and the conjecture is regarded as open. We state this neutrally: the standard objection to such announcements is that they reduce, at a crucial step, to exactly the abundance inequality being proved, or invoke a strengthening already refuted by Sarvate–Renaud-type examples. Readers should treat any claim of a complete proof as unverified until it appears in a refereed venue with independent confirmation. The accepted state of the art remains the entropy bound of roughly $0.38$, conditional improvements aside, with $1/2$ unproven.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Key Papers — The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)

The arXiv numbers attached to rows 22–25 are the plausible identifiers from that burst but are flagged for verification because several near-simultaneous notes shared similar titles and the exact id/author pairing should be confirmed. Older rows (1–20) are real works in the documented literature, but precise titles, years, and venues for several should be checked against a bibliographic source before citing — hence "—" identifiers and conservative flags rather than invented DOIs.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Top Mathematicians — The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
text: _Ten figures who shaped the problem, past and present._

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Top Mathematicians — The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
text:
_Ten figures who shaped the problem, past and present._

| # | Name | Era | Role | Key contribution |
|---|------|-----|------|------------------|
| 1 | Péter Frankl | 1979– | originator | Posed the conjecture (~1979); central figure of the extremal set theory tradition in which it lives. |
| 2 | Bjorn Poonen | 1980s–90s | expositor | Clarified equivalent formulations (set / lattice) and characterized when fixed weightings certify the conjecture; foundational structural analysis. |
| 3 | J.-C. Renaud & D. G. Sarvate | 1980s–90s | frontier | Early bounds and the influential demonstration that natural strengthenings fail, ruling out the easy averaging routes. |
| 4 | David Reimer | 1990s | breakthrough | Theorem on the average set size of union-closed families, a celebrated adjacent result repeatedly used in attempts. |
| 5 | Theodore P. Knill | 1990s | frontier | Density-type lower bounds (an element in roughly $m/\log_2 m$ sets) for general families. |
| 6 | Jürgen Reinhold | 1990s–2000s | frontier | Proved the lattice form for important classes (e.g. lower-semimodular / relatively complemented lattices). |
| 7 | Gábor Czédli | 2000s | frontier | Lattice-theoretic results, including cases for large and semimodular lattices (with E. T. Schmidt). |
| 8 | Henning Bruhn & Oliver Schaudt | 2010s | expositor | Authored the standard survey "The journey of the union-closed sets conjecture"; with Charbit and Telle, the graph/stable-set reformulation. |
| 9 | Justin Gilmer | 2022 | breakthrough | Entropy method giving the first unconditional constant-fraction bound ($\approx 1\%$), reopening the problem. |
| 10 | Will Sawin | 2022– | frontier | Sharpened Gilmer's bound to (and slightly beyond) $\frac{3-\sqrt5}{2}\approx0.38$; clarified the one-step entropy barrier. |

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Notes
text: The active community splits into three overlapping camps. The **lattice / order-theory** group (descendants of Poonen, Reinhold, Czédli, Abe) continues to chip at structured classes and the irreducible-element formulation. The **graph-theoretic** group (Bruhn, Schaudt, Charbit, Telle and successors) works the stable-set reduction and tries to widen the size-$\le 2$ slice. Since November 2022 the **information-theoretic** group has been the most active and fast-moving: Gilmer's preprint triggered near-simultaneous optimizations by Sawin, Chase and Lovett, Alweiss–Liu–Sawhney–Wu, Pebody, and Ellis–Ivan–Leader, much of it conducted openly on arXiv and research blogs within days. The central open question now belongs to this group — whether entropy methods can in principle be pushed from $0.38$ to the full $1/2$, or whether a genuinely different idea is needed to close the gap. The problem also draws periodic Polymath-style and competition-mathematics interest because of its elementary statement.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Status & Frontier — The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
text:
_Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

**Status: open, with active progress.** The conjecture — that every finite union-closed family $\mathcal{F}$ (with a non-empty member) has an element lying in at least half of its $m$ sets — is *not* proved in general. What changed dramatically in late 2022 is that the problem moved from "no constant lower bound at all" to "a concrete constant fraction, provably."

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Strongest current results (unconditional)
text:
The state of the art is the **entropy bound**. Justin Gilmer's November 2022 preprint *A constant lower bound for the union-closed sets conjecture* (arXiv:2211.09055) proved that some element appears in at least $c\,m$ sets with $c \approx 0.01$ — the first unconditional constant. Within weeks this was sharpened independently — by Will Sawin (arXiv:2211.11504), Zachary Chase and Shachar Lovett (arXiv:2211.11689), Ryan Alweiss–Yang Liu–Mehtaab Sawhney–Kewen Wu (arXiv:2211.11731), and Luke Pebody — to

$$\max_x d(x) \ \ge\ \frac{3-\sqrt5}{2}\,m \ \approx\ 0.3820\,m,$$

the natural optimum of Gilmer's single-step two-set entropy inequality. Will Sawin then obtained a small improvement strictly beyond $0.38$, showing the $0.38$ barrier is not absolute. (These arXiv identifiers are from the late-2022 burst and are flagged for verification in `papers.md`; the Gilmer paper itself is certain.)

Alongside the entropy bound stand robust **special-case** results, all accepted: the conjecture holds when $\mathcal{F}$ contains a set of size $1$ or $2$; for all ground sets with $|X| \le 12$ and all families with $m \le 50$ (computational, Bošnjak–Marković and predecessors); for "dense" families with $m \ge \tfrac{2}{3}2^{|X|}$ (Balla–Bollobás–Eccles); for the graph / size-$\le 2$ case (Bruhn–Charbit–Schaudt–Telle); and for modular, lower-semimodular, and relatively complemented lattices (Abe, Reinhold, Czédli–Schmidt).

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: Conditional results
text: There is no widely-used hard conditional ("assuming X, the conjecture holds") that drives the field, unlike Riemann-hypothesis-style conditionals elsewhere. The "conditional" texture here is rather that the entropy bound improves under stronger forms of the two-set inequality or under non-uniform sampling assumptions, and that lattice cases follow from closure properties (modularity, semimodularity) the general family need not have.

title: The Union-Closed Sets Conjecture (Frankl)
heading: What a full resolution requires
text: A complete proof must produce, for *every* finite union-closed family, an element of frequency $\ge m/2$ — with no restriction on ground-set size, number of sets, lattice structure, or generator size. The known barriers are sharp: any **averaging** proof must evade the Sarvate–Renaud examples that refute natural strengthenings; any **single-step entropy** proof cannot exceed $\frac{3-\sqrt5}{2}$; any **graph reduction** must somehow extend past generators of size $2$; and any **lattice induction** must control numerous, uncorrelated join-irreducibles. The plausible routes are (i) a *multi-step* or *non-uniform* entropy argument that breaks the $0.38$ ceiling and reaches $1/2$, (ii) a structural reduction of the general case to a settled class, or (iii) a wholly new invariant. Most current optimism sits with the entropy programme, tempered by the recognition that the gap from $0.38$ to $0.5$ has resisted the very people who found the first $0.38$.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: Plain statement
text: For every ε, distinguishing unique-label-cover instances that are (1−ε)-satisfiable from ε-satisfiable is NP-hard; if true, it pins down the exact approximability threshold of many optimization problems.

*Originators: Subhash Khot.*

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: History — The Unique Games Conjecture
text: The Unique Games Conjecture (UGC) was born from a precise gap in the theory of *hardness of approximation*. By the late 1990s, the PCP theorem (1992) and the parallel-repetition machinery of Raz had turned the study of NP-hardness into a study of *probabilistically checkable proofs*, and Håstad's 1997 optimal inapproximability results had shown that for problems such as MAX-3LIN and MAX-3SAT one could pin down the exact approximation threshold. Yet for a cluster of fundamental problems — Vertex Cover, Max-Cut, and the broad family of constraint satisfaction problems (CSPs) — the best NP-hardness factors lagged far behind the best known algorithms. The obstruction was structural: the standard PCP constructions produced *label-cover* instances whose constraints were complicated projections, and the analysis bottlenecked on the lack of a clean "unique" constraint structure.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: History — The Unique Games Conjecture
text: In his 2002 STOC paper *On the Power of Unique 2-Prover 1-Round Games*, Subhash Khot isolated exactly the missing hypothesis. A **unique game** is a 2-prover-1-round game (equivalently, a label-cover instance) in which every constraint on an edge $(u,v)$ is a *bijection* $\pi_{uv}$ between the label sets: a label for $u$ forces a unique label for $v$ and vice versa. The conjecture asserts that for every $\varepsilon>0$ there is a label-set size $k$ such that it is NP-hard to distinguish unique-games instances that are $(1-\varepsilon)$-satisfiable from those that are at most $\varepsilon$-satisfiable. The decisive feature is the *gap location near 1*: nearly-satisfiable versus nearly-unsatisfiable, which is precisely what feeds Fourier-analytic "long-code" tests.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: History — The Unique Games Conjecture
text: Khot's motivation was pragmatic and architectural. Several inapproximability proofs would go through *if only* one had a unique-games starting point; UGC packaged that hypothesis once and for all. The payoff arrived quickly and decisively. In 2004–2005, Khot, Kindler, Mossel, and O'Donnell showed UGC implies Max-Cut is NP-hard to approximate beyond the Goemans–Williamson constant $\alpha_{GW}\approx 0.878$, with the missing analytic ingredient — the **Majority Is Stablest** theorem — supplied by Mossel, O'Donnell, and Oleszkiewicz via the *invariance principle*. Khot and Regev proved UGC implies Vertex Cover is hard to approximate within $2-\varepsilon$. The capstone was Raghavendra's 2008 theorem: UGC implies that for *every* CSP a single semidefinite programming relaxation achieves the optimal approximation ratio, making UGC equivalent to a universal "SDP is optimal" principle.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1992** — Arora–Lund–Motwani–Sudan–Szegedy and Arora–Safra establish the PCP theorem, the foundation of modern hardness of approximation.
- **1997** — Håstad proves optimal inapproximability for MAX-3LIN, MAX-3SAT via the long code and Fourier analysis.
- **2002** — Subhash Khot poses the Unique Games Conjecture (STOC 2002).
- **2004** — Khot–Kindler–Mossel–O'Donnell connect UGC to Max-Cut; Mossel–O'Donnell–Oleszkiewicz prove Majority Is Stablest via the invariance principle.
- **2005** — Khot–Regev: UGC implies Vertex Cover is $(2-\varepsilon)$-hard.
- **2008** — Raghavendra: UGC implies the basic SDP gives optimal approximation for every CSP.
- **2010** — Arora–Barak–Steurer give a subexponential-time ($2^{n^{\varepsilon}}$) algorithm for Unique Games, showing it is not NP-hard in the strongest sense unless NP has subexponential algorithms — a soft barrier.
- **2010–2012** — Sum-of-squares / Lasserre hierarchy studied as a candidate refutation; Barak–Brandão–Harrow–Kelner–Steurer–Zhou analyze SoS rounding.
- **2017** — Khot–Minzer–Safra prove a key combinatorial conjecture on the Grassmann graph (expansion of certain subsets), a central lemma.
- **2018** — Khot–Minzer–Safra complete the proof of the **2-to-2 Games Theorem** (with imperfect completeness), establishing NP-hardness of 2-to-2 games and, as a corollary, that Vertex Cover is hard to approximate within $\sqrt{2}-\varepsilon$. This is the strongest unconditional progress toward UGC.
- **2018–present** — The 2-to-2 result yields that unique games are hard to distinguish $(1/2-\varepsilon)$ from $\varepsilon$-satisfiable; closing the remaining gap to perfect (near-1) completeness — full UGC — remains open and is the active frontier.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: Subhash Khot
text: Subhash Khot (born 1978 in Ichalkaranji, Maharashtra, India) is the originator of the Unique Games Conjecture and the central figure in its development. He won a silver medal at the International Mathematical Olympiad in 1994 and ranked first in the Indian Institute of Technology Joint Entrance Examination, earning his B.Tech. in Computer Science from IIT Bombay in 1999. He moved to Princeton University for graduate study, completing his Ph.D. in 2003 under the supervision of Sanjeev Arora — one of the architects of the PCP theorem. After positions at the Institute for Advanced Study, Princeton, and Georgia Tech, Khot joined New York University's Courant Institute, where he is a professor of computer science.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: Subhash Khot
text: Khot's intellectual milieu was the theory of *probabilistically checkable proofs* and *hardness of approximation*, then in a period of rapid maturation. Arora's circle had shown that approximation thresholds were not heuristic curiosities but exact, provable constants governed by the algebra of proof verification. Håstad's optimal inapproximability results (1997) had demonstrated the power of the *long code* and discrete Fourier analysis, but had also revealed a wall: the constraint structures emerging from parallel repetition were "projection" constraints, not bijections, and the cleanest analytic tests demanded bijective ("unique") constraints. Khot's insight, formulated during his doctoral work and published in 2002, was to *name the missing hypothesis* rather than re-derive each consequence from scratch. He recognized that a single clean assumption — the NP-hardness of distinguishing nearly-satisfiable from nearly-unsatisfiable unique games — would unlock a whole class of optimal hardness results at once.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: Subhash Khot
text: The conceptual move was characteristic of Khot's style: identify the precise combinatorial object whose hardness is both *plausible* and *maximally leveraged*. Unique games sit at a sweet spot. They are simple enough to plug into long-code dictatorship tests — the bijective constraints let label assignments compose cleanly under the Fourier transform — yet not so simple as to be obviously easy. The subexponential algorithm of Arora–Barak–Steurer (2010) later confirmed that they straddle a genuine complexity boundary. Khot did not merely pose the conjecture; he drove much of the subsequent program, co-authoring the Max-Cut connection (with Kindler, Mossel, and O'Donnell), the Vertex Cover hardness (with Regev), and ultimately the 2-to-2 Games Theorem (with Dor Minzer and Muli Safra, 2016–2018), the deepest unconditional progress toward the conjecture to date.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: Subhash Khot
text: Khot's broader legacy is the elevation of UGC into the organizing principle of modern approximation theory. Raghavendra's 2008 theorem made the conjecture *equivalent* to the statement that a single semidefinite program optimally approximates every constraint satisfaction problem — a unification that turned a technical hypothesis into a structural law. For this body of work Khot received the National Science Foundation's Alan T. Waterman Award (2010), the Rolf Nevanlinna Prize at the International Congress of Mathematicians (2014), and a MacArthur Fellowship (2016); he was elected to the U.S. National Academy of Sciences. His career illustrates how the act of *posing the right conjecture* — sharply, at the precise leverage point of a theory — can be as consequential as proving a theorem, reshaping a field's research agenda for two decades and counting.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: Approaches — The Unique Games Conjecture
text: The UGC is studied from two opposing directions: *proving* it (constructing a PCP reduction that produces hard unique-games instances) and *refuting* it (designing algorithms that solve unique games, which would make UGC false unless P = NP fails in unexpected ways). The frontier sits between these, and the most important results are barriers that constrain both.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: PCP / 2-to-2 reduction approach (toward a proof)
text: The principal route to *proving* UGC mimics Håstad's program: build a probabilistically checkable proof whose verifier reads a constant number of symbols and whose acceptance test corresponds to a unique constraint. The core difficulty is *completeness*: long-code based reductions naturally produce constraints that are "$d$-to-$1$" (each label of one side is consistent with $d$ labels of the other) rather than perfectly bijective, and they achieve completeness bounded away from $1$. The landmark achievement of this line is the **2-to-2 Games Theorem** of Khot, Minzer, and Safra (2016–2018), which proves NP-hardness of 2-to-2 games with imperfect completeness, and as a corollary that unique games are NP-hard to distinguish between $(1/2-\varepsilon)$-satisfiable and $\varepsilon$-satisfiable. The technical heart is the **Grassmann graph** analysis: showing that small non-expanding sets in the Grassmann graph must be "structured" (correlate with a low-dimensional subspace), which controls the soundness of the test. **Best result:** hardness with completeness $\approx 1/2$. **Barrier:** the remaining gap to completeness $\to 1$ (the full UGC) appears to require fundamentally new gadgets; the 2-to-2 framework's imperfect completeness is intrinsic to its current form.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: Subexponential algorithms (soft refutation / barrier)
text: Arora, Barak, and Steurer (2010) gave an algorithm solving Unique Games in time $2^{n^{\varepsilon}}$ for any constant $\varepsilon>0$, via graph decomposition into pieces of low threshold-rank on which the problem becomes tractable. **Significance:** this is a *barrier to strong forms* of UGC — it shows unique games cannot be NP-hard in the way 3SAT presumably is (no $2^{\Omega(n)}$ lower bound under the Exponential Time Hypothesis), so any proof of UGC must produce instances solvable in subexponential time. **Best result:** $\exp(n^{\varepsilon})$ runtime. **Barrier it imposes:** the conjecture's truth, if it holds, is "fragile" — sitting just above polynomial time — which is why both believers and skeptics find it genuinely hard to settle.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: Semidefinite programming and Sum-of-Squares hierarchies (refutation attempts)
text: If the constant-round Sum-of-Squares (SoS / Lasserre) SDP hierarchy solved unique games in polynomial time, UGC would be false. Much effort (Barak–Brandão–Harrow–Kelner–Steurer–Zhou 2012; Barak–Raghavendra–Steurer; Guruswami–Sinop) sought either an SoS algorithm or integrality-gap instances defeating it. **Best result on the algorithmic side:** SoS solves several previously-hard instance families and underlies the ABS-style decompositions. **Best result on the lower-bound side:** known SDP integrality gaps (e.g., from Khot–Vishnoi instances) survive a constant number of SoS rounds but are *not* known to survive $n^{\Omega(1)}$ rounds. **Barrier:** the question of whether $\text{poly}(n)$-round SoS refutes unique games remains open and is itself a central, unresolved sub-problem; this ambiguity is precisely why SoS is the leading candidate to refute UGC.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: Small-set expansion and the SSE hypothesis (equivalence / reformulation)
text: Raghavendra and Steurer (2010) introduced the **Small-Set Expansion (SSE) Hypothesis** — that it is NP-hard to certify whether a graph has a small non-expanding set — and showed SSE implies UGC, with the two being closely intertwined (UGC reduces to a "covering" variant of SSE). **Value:** SSE recasts UGC as a purely graph-theoretic expansion question, decoupling it from the gadget-engineering of PCP reductions and exposing it to spectral and geometric tools. **Best result:** the reduction SSE ⇒ UGC, plus subexponential algorithms for SSE paralleling ABS. **Barrier:** SSE inherits the same subexponential-algorithm soft barrier, and no unconditional proof of SSE is known.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: Invariance principle / analytic core (enabling machinery)
text: Underlying the *consequences* of UGC is the Gaussian-noise analytic toolkit: the **Majority Is Stablest** theorem (Mossel–O'Donnell–Oleszkiewicz, 2005) and the **invariance principle**, which show that among low-influence functions the majority/dictator functions are extremal for noise stability. **Best result:** these make UGC tight — Raghavendra's 2008 theorem proves UGC implies the basic SDP achieves the *exact* optimal approximation ratio for every CSP, so under UGC the analytic constants are not merely lower bounds but the truth. **Barrier:** this machinery presupposes UGC; it explains *why* UGC is so consequential but offers no independent route to proving the hardness itself.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: Attempts — The Unique Games Conjecture
text: The UGC has not been the subject of widely-publicized *retracted* proofs in the manner of, say, the $P\neq NP$ literature — its difficulty is well-respected and the community is small and expert. The notable events are instead a sequence of deep partial results, surprising algorithmic discoveries that reshaped expectations, and a still-unsettled debate over whether the conjecture is even true.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: The 2-to-2 Games Theorem (the great near-miss)
text: The most important partial result is the proof of the **2-to-2 Games Theorem** by Subhash Khot, Dor Minzer, and Muli Safra, completed in 2018 after a multi-paper effort begun in 2016. It establishes the NP-hardness of unique games *with imperfect completeness*: distinguishing instances that are $(1/2-\varepsilon)$-satisfiable from those at most $\varepsilon$-satisfiable. This is genuinely "halfway" to UGC, which demands completeness $(1-\varepsilon)$. The proof's engine — the resolution of a conjecture about expansion of non-expanding sets in the **Grassmann graph** — was the missing combinatorial lemma; its proof (Khot–Minzer–Safra, with contributions building on Dinur–Khot–Kindler–Minzer–Safra) was hailed as a major breakthrough. The result immediately improved unconditional Vertex Cover hardness from $1.36$ (Dinur–Safra) toward $\sqrt 2 \approx 1.41$. **Open gap:** lifting completeness from $1/2$ to $1-\varepsilon$ remains unsolved and is not a routine extension.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: The subexponential algorithm (the expectation-shifter)
text: Arora, Barak, and Steurer's 2010 subexponential ($2^{n^{\varepsilon}}$) algorithm for Unique Games was a near-miss in the *opposite* direction: it was widely read as evidence that the problem might be substantially easier than NP-hard, and for a period it cooled confidence that UGC is true. It did not refute the conjecture — UGC concerns NP-hardness of a gap problem, which subexponential algorithms do not contradict — but it demonstrated that no proof of UGC can produce instances requiring exponential time, sharply constraining the proof space.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: Sum-of-Squares and integrality-gap instances
text: A recurring research thread has been attempts to *refute* UGC using the Sum-of-Squares (Lasserre) SDP hierarchy. Khot and Vishnoi (2005) constructed integrality-gap instances for Unique Games (and used them to derive an unconditional MAX-CUT integrality gap), instances later studied as candidate hard cases for SoS. Work by Barak, Brandão, Harrow, Kelner, Steurer, and Zhou (2012) showed that the basic SoS algorithm solves several instance families previously believed hard, including subexponential-time guarantees, again nudging expert opinion toward skepticism. No polynomial-round SoS refutation has been found, and no SoS lower bound ruling one out has been proven — the matter is genuinely open.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: The standing of the conjecture
text: Unusually for a famous conjecture, the community is *divided* on whether UGC is true. Some researchers regard the 2-to-2 theorem as strong evidence for eventual proof; others, citing the subexponential algorithm and SoS progress, suspect it may be false. There is no disputed *claimed proof* to adjudicate; the honest status is an open problem with substantial, carefully-vetted partial progress in both directions and no consensus on the answer.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: Notes
text: The active community around the UGC is small, deeply interconnected, and centered in theoretical computer science rather than classical mathematics, though it draws heavily on analysis, probability, and geometry. The current frontier is driven by the group around the **2-to-2 / Grassmann** breakthrough — Khot, Minzer, Safra, and collaborators such as Guy Kindler and Irit Dinur — who are pushing to lift completeness toward $1$. In parallel, the **Sum-of-Squares** school (Barak, Steurer, Raghavendra, and younger researchers including Pravesh Kothari and Tselil Schramm) probes whether constant- or polynomial-round SDP hierarchies might refute the conjecture instead. Bridging figures such as Oded Regev, Guy Kindler, and Madhur Tulsiani connect the hardness and algorithmic camps. The field's small size means progress tends to arrive in concentrated bursts (2004–2005, 2008, 2010, 2016–2018), each reorganizing expert opinion about whether UGC is ultimately true or false.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: Status & Frontier — The Unique Games Conjecture
text: **Status: active-progress; open.** The Unique Games Conjecture remains unproven and unrefuted. The community does not have consensus on whether it is true. What has changed dramatically since 2002 is the depth of partial results bracketing it from both sides.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: What is known (unconditional)
text:
The strongest unconditional result is the **2-to-2 Games Theorem** of Khot, Minzer, and Safra (completed 2018, building on Dinur–Khot–Kindler–Minzer–Safra 2018). It proves NP-hardness of 2-to-2 games with imperfect completeness, which implies that unique games are NP-hard to distinguish between $(1/2-\varepsilon)$-satisfiable and $\varepsilon$-satisfiable. Concretely, this yields that **Vertex Cover is NP-hard to approximate within $\sqrt{2}-\varepsilon$**, improving on the prior $1.36$ bound of Dinur–Safra (2005). The technical core was the resolution of the **Grassmann-graph expansion** conjecture: non-expanding sets in the Grassmann graph are structured. Key arXiv references for the 2-to-2 cluster are *On Independent Sets, 2-to-2 Games, and Grassmann Graphs* and *Pseudorandom Sets in Grassmann Graph Have Near-Perfect Expansion* (Khot–Minzer–Safra, 2017–2018); the existence of these papers is high-confidence, though specific identifiers should be verified before citation.

The opposing unconditional fact is the **subexponential algorithm** of Arora–Barak–Steurer (2010), solving Unique Games in time $2^{n^{\varepsilon}}$. This is a *barrier*: any proof of UGC must yield instances solvable in subexponential time, so UGC cannot hold in the strongest exponential-hardness sense.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: What is known (conditional)
text: Assuming UGC, the picture is extraordinarily complete. **Raghavendra (2008)** proved that UGC implies a single semidefinite-programming relaxation achieves the *optimal* approximation ratio for **every** constraint satisfaction problem — so under UGC, approximation thresholds are exactly the SDP integrality gaps. UGC implies Max-Cut is hard beyond $\alpha_{GW}\approx 0.878$ (Khot–Kindler–Mossel–O'Donnell, with Majority Is Stablest), Vertex Cover beyond $2-\varepsilon$ (Khot–Regev), and tight bounds for sparsest-cut, multicut, and many ordering/grouping problems. UGC is also tightly linked to the **Small-Set Expansion Hypothesis** (Raghavendra–Steurer 2010), giving a purely graph-theoretic reformulation.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: What a full resolution requires
text: To **prove** UGC: a PCP/long-code reduction producing unique-games instances with completeness $1-\varepsilon$. The 2-to-2 framework reaches completeness $\approx 1/2$; bridging the gap to near-$1$ completeness is the central open obstacle and is not believed to be a routine extension. To **refute** UGC: a polynomial-time (or polynomial-round Sum-of-Squares) algorithm distinguishing the gap, which would make the conjecture false. Whether $\text{poly}(n)$-round SoS solves unique games is itself open and is the leading candidate refutation route.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: Plausible routes
text:
1. **Lift completeness in the Grassmann/2-to-2 program** — extend the structured-expansion machinery to $d$-to-$1$ games with completeness $\to 1$.
2. **Settle Sum-of-Squares** — prove either an SoS algorithm (refuting UGC) or an $n^{\Omega(1)}$-round SoS integrality-gap lower bound (strong evidence for UGC).
3. **Resolve Small-Set Expansion** — an unconditional proof or refutation of SSE would likely carry UGC with it.

The honest assessment in 2026: substantial, carefully-vetted progress on both sides, a halfway hardness result in hand, and no settled answer.

title: The Unique Games Conjecture
heading: Related problems
text:
- [P versus NP](../p-versus-np/README.md) — UGC is a refined hardness hypothesis living inside the P-vs-NP landscape of optimal inapproximability.
- [Hadwiger Conjecture](../hadwiger-conjecture/README.md) — a hard combinatorial-coloring problem of the kind whose approximability UGC would help pin down.
- [Sunflower Conjecture](../sunflower-conjecture/README.md) — a neighboring extremal/combinatorial question central to theoretical computer science.

text: What is the maximum number of unit distances among n points in the plane? Conjectured n^(1+o(1)); the best bounds remain far apart.

*Originators: Paul Erdős.*

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: History — The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
text: _Origin, formulation, and timeline._

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: How the problem arose
text: In 1946 Paul Erdős published a short, now-canonical paper, *On sets of distances of $n$ points* (American Mathematical Monthly, 53, 248–250), in which he posed two intertwined extremal questions about a planar point set of size $n$. The first — the **distinct distances problem** — asks for the minimum number of *distinct* distances the set must determine. The second — the **unit distance problem** — asks for the **maximum** number of pairs at one fixed distance (say distance $1$). Write $u(n)$ for that maximum. Erdős's geometric instinct was that the densest possible configuration is essentially a piece of the integer lattice, where number-theoretic counting of representations as sums of two squares governs the answer.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Precise formulation
text:
Let $P$ be a set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ and let
$$u(n) = \max_{|P|=n}\#\{(p,q)\in P\times P : p<q,\ \|p-q\|=1\}.$$
Erdős asked for the growth rate of $u(n)$. From the $\sqrt{n}\times\sqrt{n}$ integer grid he derived the lower bound $u(n) \ge n^{1+c/\log\log n}$, using Landau's theorem on the number of representations of an integer by sums of two squares. He conjectured this lower bound is essentially the truth: $u(n) = n^{1+o(1)}$, in particular $u(n) \le n^{1+\varepsilon}$ for every $\varepsilon>0$. The lower bound was never matched; every known upper bound has the form $u(n) = O(n^{4/3})$ or close variants, and the factor-of-$n^{1/3}$ gap remains the central open problem.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Reformulations
text: The problem is naturally cast in **incidence geometry**: unit distances among $P$ are incidences between the points $P$ and the family of $n$ unit circles centered at those points. Bounding point–circle incidences (Szemerédi–Trotter-type) yields the $O(n^{4/3})$ bound. It also connects to the **Hadwiger–Nelson problem** (chromatic number of the plane), the **distinct distances problem** (resolved up to logarithms by Guth–Katz in 2010/2015), and to higher-dimensional analogues where the truth is fully known in $\mathbb{R}^d$ for $d\ge 4$.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1946** — Erdős poses both the distinct-distances and unit-distance problems; obtains the grid lower bound $n^{1+c/\log\log n}$ and the easy upper bound $O(n^{3/2})$.
- **1972** — Józsa and Szemerédi improve the upper bound to $o(n^{3/2})$, the first sub-$3/2$ result.
- **1973** — Beck and others refine; the problem becomes a flagship of the emerging field of combinatorial geometry.
- **1984** — Spencer, Szemerédi, and Trotter prove $u(n) = O(n^{4/3})$, the bound that still stands today, via a crossing-number / incidence argument.
- **1983–1984** — Szemerédi and Trotter establish the general incidence theorem; Clarkson, Edelsbrunner, Guibas, Sharir, and Welzl (1990) give a simpler crossing-number proof (after Székely).
- **1997** — Székely's crossing-number method gives a transparent reproof of $O(n^{4/3})$, decoupling it from heavy machinery.
- **2008** — Constraints on near-extremal configurations studied; the conjecture's number-theoretic flavor sharpened.
- **2010/2015** — Guth and Katz resolve the *distinct* distances problem ($\Omega(n/\log n)$) using polynomial partitioning and algebraic geometry — but their method does **not** transfer to the unit-distance count.
- **2015–2020** — Polynomial-method and algebraic approaches probed for the unit-distance problem; partial results for structured point sets (e.g., points on few lines/circles), and improved bounds for unit distances in special norms.
- **2020s** — The $O(n^{4/3})$ upper bound remains unbeaten in general; activity focuses on algebraic structure of extremal sets and on the analogous problem in other metrics and over finite fields.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Timeline
text: The defining tension persists: the lower bound $n^{1+o(1)}$ and the upper bound $n^{4/3}$ have not moved toward each other in the general planar case since 1984.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Originator(s) — The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
text: _Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Paul Erdős (1913–1996)
text: Paul Erdős was born in Budapest on 26 March 1913 to two Jewish high-school mathematics teachers, Lajos and Anna Erdős. A child prodigy, he could multiply three-digit numbers in his head at age three and rediscovered negative numbers on his own. He entered the Pázmány Péter University in Budapest and earned his doctorate in 1934 at age 21, supervised in spirit by Lipót Fejér, with a thesis that included an elementary proof of Chebyshev's theorem (Bertrand's postulate). The rise of antisemitism and fascism in Hungary made an academic career there impossible; Erdős left in 1934 for a fellowship in Manchester and, for the rest of his life, became the archetypal stateless, itinerant mathematician — travelling from one colleague's home to the next with a half-empty suitcase, famously declaring "another roof, another proof."

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Mathematical background and style
text: Erdős worked in number theory, combinatorics, probability, set theory, and the geometry of point sets. His signature method was **elementary but extremal**: he asked, for a finite configuration, how large or small some combinatorial quantity could be, and sought the answer through clever counting, the probabilistic method (which he pioneered with the 1947 lower bound for Ramsey numbers), and a deep feel for when number theory secretly controls a geometric count. He published roughly 1,500 papers with more than 500 collaborators, giving rise to the "Erdős number." His monetary prizes for unsolved problems — small cheques he genuinely paid out — seeded entire research programs.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: The ideas that led to the problem
text: The unit distance problem grew directly out of Erdős's 1946 study of the *distance distribution* of a finite planar set. He asked the dual extremal questions: what is the **fewest** number of distinct distances $n$ points must span, and what is the **most** times a single distance can recur? For the second, his intuition pointed to the integer lattice. Counting how many lattice points lie at distance exactly $\sqrt{k}$ from the origin is precisely the problem of representing $k$ as a sum of two squares, governed by **Landau's theorem** and the multiplicative structure of Gaussian integers. From the $\sqrt n \times \sqrt n$ grid this yields the lower bound $n^{1+c/\log\log n}$ — superlinear, but only barely, with the $\log\log n$ in the exponent reflecting the largest number of sum-of-two-squares representations below a bound. Erdős conjectured this near-linear rate is the truth, i.e. $u(n)=n^{1+o(1)}$. The conjecture thus encodes his lifelong thesis that the densest geometric configurations are arithmetic ones.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Historical root vs. modern formulation
text: The **historical root** is the single 1946 Monthly paper, where the unit-distance and distinct-distance questions appear together as two faces of one inquiry. The **modern formulation** isolates the unit-distance count $u(n)$ and recasts it in the language of **incidence geometry**: unit distances are point–unit-circle incidences, and the central tools are the Szemerédi–Trotter theorem and the crossing-number / polynomial methods that postdate Erdős. The conjectured answer is unchanged, but the surrounding apparatus — and the recognition that the distinct-distances sibling fell to algebraic methods while this one did not — is entirely a development of the decades after Erdős posed it.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Legacy
text: Erdős's problems defined combinatorial and discrete geometry as fields. The distinct-distances conjecture (his companion question) was essentially settled by Guth and Katz in 2010, an event that ranks among the great triumphs of the polynomial method; the unit-distance problem remains his most famous unsolved geometric question. He died on 20 September 1996 in Warsaw, at a conference — working to the end. His habit of treating mathematics as a communal, problem-driven enterprise is the direct ancestor of the active community still attacking $u(n)$ today.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Approaches — The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
text: _Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Incidence geometry (Szemerédi–Trotter / point–circle incidences)
text: The dominant strategy reformulates unit distances as incidences. Place a unit circle around each of the $n$ points; a unit distance between $p$ and $q$ is exactly the incidence "$p$ lies on the unit circle centered at $q$." The number of unit distances is therefore half the number of incidences between $n$ points and $n$ congruent (unit-radius) circles. Bounding point–circle incidences gives the count. Because two distinct unit circles meet in at most two points, the incidence graph contains no $K_{2,3}$, and a Kővári–Sós–Turán argument already yields $O(n^{3/2})$. Sharpening to congruent circles and applying the Szemerédi–Trotter machinery yields the **best known upper bound**, $u(n) = O(n^{4/3})$ (Spencer–Szemerédi–Trotter, 1984). **Barrier:** the $4/3$ exponent is the *natural* limit of incidence/crossing-number arguments — it is tight for point–line and point–unit-circle *incidence* problems in general, even though unit distances are believed to be far sparser. To go below $n^{4/3}$ one must exploit that the circles are not merely pseudolines but *congruent* circles whose centers are the points themselves, a rigidity that incidence bounds alone do not see.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Crossing-number method (Székely)
text: Székely (1997) gave a strikingly short proof of $O(n^{4/3})$ via the crossing-number inequality: draw the unit circles, treat the arcs between consecutive incidences as edges of a multigraph drawn in the plane, and apply $\mathrm{cr}(G) \ge c\,e^3/v^2$ for $e \ge 4v$. **Best result:** reproves $u(n)=O(n^{4/3})$ cleanly and extends to many variants (unit distances among points on a sphere, in convex position, etc.). **Barrier:** identical $4/3$ ceiling — the method is a repackaging of the same incidence content and shares its obstruction.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Polynomial method / polynomial partitioning
text: After Guth and Katz used polynomial partitioning and the algebraic geometry of ruled surfaces to settle the *distinct distances* problem in 2010, hope ran high that the same tools would crack unit distances. They have not, in the general case. **Best result:** the polynomial method gives sharper bounds for *structured* inputs (points on a small number of lines or circles; configurations with bounded algebraic degree) and underlies progress in finite-field and higher-dimensional analogues. **Barrier:** the distinct-distances argument crucially uses that one is counting *distinct* values and can pass to a related quantity (the Elekes–Sharir framework counting rigid motions) where a polynomial partition controls the geometry. For the unit-distance *count*, the extremal example (the lattice) has genuine arithmetic structure with no low-degree algebraic surface to exploit; the partitioning loses precisely the $n^{1/3}$ factor in question. This is widely regarded as the deepest known obstruction.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Arithmetic / lattice and additive-combinatorics view
text: Since the conjectured extremizer is the integer grid, one line of attack studies the arithmetic of near-extremal sets: a set with many unit distances should look grid-like (have small additive doubling, lie largely on a sublattice). Tools from additive combinatorics (Freiman-type structure, sum-product estimates) are brought to bear. **Best result:** strong bounds for points constrained to be on a few lines, on a grid, or with prescribed additive structure, and conditional statements ("if the extremal set has property X, then $u(n)=n^{1+o(1)}$"). **Barrier:** there is no proof that an extremal set *must* be structured; a near-extremal configuration could in principle be pseudo-random in a way that defeats additive tools, and the $\log\log n$ subtlety of the lower bound is hard to capture.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Special norms and the role of the Euclidean circle
text: A clarifying negative-style insight: for many norms the unit-distance answer is *different*. With a strictly convex but smooth-enough unit ball one still gets near-$n^{4/3}$ behavior, but for polygonal norms the unit "circle" is a polygon and one can force $\Theta(n^{4/3})$ or even more unit distances easily — showing the hard Euclidean case relies on the *roundness* (curvature) of the circle. **Best result (clarifying):** Valtr and others constructed norms for which the maximum number of unit distances is $\Theta(n^{4/3})$, proving that any proof of the Erdős conjecture must use a special analytic property of the Euclidean circle (its strict, uniform convexity and the two-point intersection rigidity together). **Barrier interpretation:** this is a *barrier result* — it certifies that purely combinatorial/topological arguments, which apply to all "nice" convex distances uniformly, can never beat $n^{4/3}$; the Euclidean structure must enter.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Higher dimensions and finite fields (transfer and contrast)
text: In $\mathbb{R}^d$ for $d\ge 4$ the answer is *known*: $\Theta(n^2)$ for $d\ge 4$ (using a Lenz construction of two orthogonal circles), and $d=3$ has bounds around $n^{3/2}$ (improved to roughly $n^{295/197}$ by Zahl and others). Finite-field analogues (Iosevich–Rudnev and successors) give sharp results via Fourier/character-sum methods. **Use:** these settings test which features are essential. **Barrier:** the planar Euclidean problem is *harder* than its high-dimensional cousins precisely because the plane is too "tight" for Lenz-type constructions yet too "round" for incidence bounds to be tight — it sits in the worst spot for every available method.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Attempts — The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
text: _Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: The successive upper bounds (the real progress)
text:
The honest narrative of the problem is a short chain of *upper-bound* improvements that then stalled:

- **Erdős (1946):** $u(n) = O(n^{3/2})$ via the $K_{2,3}$-free incidence graph (Kővári–Sós–Turán). This is the trivial ceiling.
- **Józsa–Szemerédi (1972):** the first improvement, $u(n) = o(n^{3/2})$ — superficially small, but it broke the $3/2$ barrier and signaled that the truth is much smaller.
- **Beck; Spencer–Szemerédi–Trotter (1984):** $u(n) = O(n^{4/3})$. This remains the record more than four decades later. Every subsequent "improvement" has been a constant-factor sharpening or a reproof (notably Székely's 1997 crossing-number argument and Pach–Tardos refinements), **not** a new exponent.

The lower bound side has been even more static: Erdős's grid construction $n^{1+c/\log\log n}$ (1946) has never been improved. So the famous gap — between $n^{1+o(1)}$ and $n^{4/3}$ — is essentially the gap as it stood in 1984.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Near-misses via the polynomial method
text: After Guth and Katz's 2010 resolution of the distinct-distances problem, several groups attempted to port polynomial partitioning to the unit-distance count. These produced genuine partial results — sharper bounds for points on a bounded number of lines or circles, for sets of bounded algebraic complexity, and in three dimensions — but the general planar exponent did not move. The consensus, articulated repeatedly by Sheffer, Solymosi, Zahl and others, is that the distinct-distances method counts distinct *values* (and reduces to a rigid-motion incidence problem with a low-degree structure), whereas the unit-distance lattice extremizer has no comparable algebraic backbone to partition. This is a structural near-miss, not a flawed proof.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Structured-set results (partial, correct)
text: A productive body of work proves the conjecture *conditionally* or for restricted inputs: points in convex position admit only $O(n\log n)$ unit distances (a clean, tight-up-to-constants result), and points on the integer grid, on few lines, or with small additive doubling satisfy near-linear or otherwise improved bounds. These are correct and important, but they sidestep the worst case rather than resolve it.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Disputed and erroneous claims
text: The unit distance problem has, fortunately, not been plagued by high-profile false "solutions" of the kind that surround, say, $P$ vs $NP$. There is **no widely circulated, seriously entertained claimed proof** of either the $n^{1+o(1)}$ upper bound or a matching lower bound that the community treats as a live dispute. Periodic preprints claiming to settle the problem appear on arXiv and elsewhere; none has survived expert scrutiny, and they are generally found either to (i) re-derive the known $O(n^{4/3})$ bound while *claiming* to beat it, (ii) silently assume the extremal set is grid-like, or (iii) misapply incidence bounds past their tightness threshold (which Valtr-type norm constructions show cannot be beaten by general convex-distance arguments). In keeping with house policy, no specific claimed proof is endorsed here; readers should treat any announcement of a resolution as unverified until it passes refereeing by the discrete-geometry community.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: The barrier that frames all attempts
text: The single most cited reason the problem resists is the **norm barrier**: there exist (non-Euclidean) norms for which the maximum number of unit distances genuinely is $\Theta(n^{4/3})$. Any correct proof of Erdős's conjecture must therefore invoke a property special to the round Euclidean circle — its strict, uniform convexity together with the two-intersection rigidity of congruent circles. Every attempt that uses only combinatorial or topological features common to all "nice" convex bodies is, by this barrier, doomed to stop at $n^{4/3}$.

Rows 13–24 are plausible representatives of the modern incidence/polynomial-method literature; the exact titles and author lists for some should be checked against the published record before citation.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Notes
text: The active community sits at the interface of **discrete and combinatorial geometry**, **incidence theory**, and the **polynomial method**. Beyond those tabled above, frequent contributors include Joshua Zahl (sharp incidence and $\mathbb{R}^3$ unit-distance bounds), Frank de Zeeuw, József Solymosi, Orit Raz, and Gábor Tardos, with the broader Pach–Sharir school supplying the algorithmic and arrangement-theoretic backbone. The Guth–Katz breakthrough of 2010 redrew the field's expectations: it proved the polynomial method can crush one Erdős distance problem, which both energized attempts on the unit-distance count and clarified — through their repeated non-transfer — exactly why this problem is harder. Today the frontier is pursued in two registers: structural/arithmetic results on what an extremal set must look like, and barrier results (notably non-Euclidean norms achieving $\Theta(n^{4/3})$) that constrain which methods could ever succeed.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Status & Frontier — The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
text:
_Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

**Status: open.** The problem has stood unresolved since 1946, and the decisive gap has barely narrowed since 1984. Let $u(n)$ denote the maximum number of unit distances among $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: What is known (unconditional)
text:
- **Lower bound (1946, Erdős).** The $\sqrt n \times \sqrt n$ integer grid realizes
$$u(n) \ge n^{1 + c/\log\log n}$$
for some constant $c>0$, via Landau's count of sum-of-two-squares representations. This is the best lower bound known; it has *never* been improved.
- **Upper bound (1984, Spencer–Szemerédi–Trotter).**
$$u(n) = O(n^{4/3}).$$
This remains the record. Székely's 1997 crossing-number proof reestablished it transparently, and subsequent work (Pach–Tardos and others) has improved only the constant, not the exponent. The current best constant places the bound near $u(n) \lesssim c\, n^{4/3}$ with a small explicit $c$.
- **Conjecture (Erdős).** $u(n) = n^{1+o(1)}$; equivalently $u(n) = O(n^{1+\varepsilon})$ for every $\varepsilon>0$. The believed truth is essentially the lattice rate.

So the live gap is the factor $n^{1/3}$ between $n^{1+o(1)}$ (conjectured/lower) and $n^{4/3}$ (proved upper).

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Conditional and special-case results
text:
- **Convex position:** points in convex position determine $O(n\log n)$ unit distances (essentially tight), far below $n^{4/3}$.
- **Structured inputs:** points on few lines or circles, or sets with small additive doubling, satisfy near-conjectural bounds via polynomial partitioning and additive combinatorics.
- **Other dimensions:** in $\mathbb{R}^d$, $d\ge 4$, the answer is $\Theta(n^2)$ (Lenz construction); in $\mathbb{R}^3$ the best bounds are around $n^{3/2}$, improved by Zahl and others. These are *not* conditional in the plane but illuminate which features make $d=2$ hard.
- **Sibling resolved:** the *distinct* distances problem — Erdős's companion 1946 question — was settled up to a logarithm by Guth and Katz (2010 preprint; Annals of Mathematics, 2015; DOI 10.4007/annals.2015.181.1.2), giving $\Omega(n/\log n)$ distinct distances. Their polynomial method has **not** transferred to the unit-distance count.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: What a full resolution would require
text: A proof of $u(n) = n^{1+o(1)}$ must do something no current method can: exploit a property **special to the round Euclidean circle**. This is forced by a barrier — there exist norms (with suitable unit balls) for which the maximum number of unit distances is genuinely $\Theta(n^{4/3})$. Hence any argument relying only on combinatorial, topological, or generic-convexity features (which apply uniformly to all "nice" norms) cannot beat $n^{4/3}$. A resolution must invoke the strict, uniform convexity of the circle together with the rigidity that two congruent circles meet in at most two points, and likely must prove a structural theorem forcing near-extremal sets to be lattice-like.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Plausible routes
text:
1. **A genuinely algebraic upper bound** that, unlike Guth–Katz, controls a *count* rather than a set of distinct values — e.g., a polynomial partition adapted to congruent-circle arrangements that uses curvature.
2. **A structure theorem** for near-extremal sets (additive-combinatorics / Freiman-type), proving they must concentrate on a sublattice, then bootstrapping the grid lower bound to an upper bound.
3. **A new incidence inequality for congruent circles** that strictly improves on Szemerédi–Trotter by using metric (not just topological) information — the route most directly blocked by the norm barrier, hence requiring an essentially Euclidean input.

Expert consensus rates the problem genuinely hard (the metadata difficulty is 84, tractability 28): the conjecture is almost universally believed, but no approach is currently near closing the $n^{1/3}$ gap.

title: The Erdős Unit Distance Problem
heading: Related problems
text:
- [Erdős Distinct Distances / Falconer Distance Conjecture](../falconer-distance-conjecture/README.md) — the continuous and distinct-value cousins of the same distance philosophy.
- [Hadwiger–Nelson Problem](../hadwiger-nelson-problem/README.md) — chromatic number of the unit-distance graph of the plane; same graph, different invariant.
- [Kakeya Conjecture](../kakeya-conjecture/README.md) — another geometry problem where the polynomial method reshaped the landscape.

title: Vaught's Conjecture
heading: Plain statement
text: A countable complete first-order theory has either at most countably many or exactly continuum-many countable models — never strictly in between.
